> - **Topic:** Type Nomenclature in Sample Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 2 — Type Nomenclature in Sample Languages

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type Nomenclature** refers to the **systematic set of rules, names, and syntactic conventions** that a programming language uses to **declare, name, and classify data types**. It encompasses primitive type names, user-defined type names, type constructors, and the way types are referenced in expressions, declarations, and signatures.

In formal programming-language theory (Sebesta, Scott, Pierce), a **type** is a precise characterization of a set of values and a set of operations that may be applied to those values. The **nomenclature** is the *lexical layer* of the type system — i.e., the actual identifiers, keywords, and syntactic forms used to express types in source code.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECST758):** Under Module 2, students are required to compare type naming across sample languages such as **C, Java, Python, ML/Haskell, and Ada**, and to understand how each language's nomenclature reflects its underlying typing discipline (static vs. dynamic, strong vs. weak, nominal vs. structural).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toolbox Label" Metaphor

Imagine every programming language as a **hardware workshop** with a wall of labeled drawers:

- In **C**, the drawers have very short, machine-style labels: `int`, `char`, `float`, `double`. Nothing fancy — the workshop is designed for engineers who already know the tools.
- In **Java**, the drawers have medium-length, object-flavored labels: `int`, `Integer`, `String`, `double`. There is a primitive drawer *and* an object-wrapper drawer.
- In **Python**, the drawers are dynamically relabeled at runtime: `int`, `float`, `str`, `list`, `dict`. The labels are uniform, but their meaning depends on what you put inside.
- In **Haskell / ML**, the drawer labels are *typesafe blue prints* with arrows and stars: `Maybe Int`, `[Char]`, `Int -> Bool`. The labels *describe how the tool will be used*.
- In **Ada**, the drawer labels are explicit and verbose: `Integer`, `Positive`, `My_Custom_Color_Type`. Even subranges get their own labeled compartment.

**Type Nomenclature = the labeling system on the workshop drawers.** It tells the programmer (and the compiler/interpreter) what kind of value lives where.

### 1.3 Why Type Nomenclature Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners for PECST758 frequently test three skills:
> 1. **Identification** — recognising how a specific language names its basic types.
> 2. **Translation** — converting a type expression from one language to another.
> 3. **Justification** — explaining *why* a language's design choice (e.g., `Integer` vs. `int`) reflects its typing philosophy.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Type Nomenclature

Types in modern languages fall into two broad nomenclature groups:

| Nomenclature Class | Description | Example Languages |
|---|---|---|
| **Primitive / Scalar Type Names** | Built-in, atomic types named by reserved keywords or standard identifiers | C, Java, C++ |
| **Composite / Constructed Type Names** | Types built by applying type constructors (`array`, `pointer`, `record`, `set`, `function`) to existing types | C, Ada, ML |
| **Abstract / Object Type Names** | Class or interface names used as type names | Java, C#, Python |
| **Algebraic Data Type (ADT) Names** | Sum and product types named by constructors | Haskell, ML, Rust, Scala |
| **Type Variable / Parametric Names** | Placeholder type identifiers used in generic / parametric polymorphism | Java `<T>`, Haskell `a`, ML `'a` |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Layers of Type Nomenclature

A complete type-nomenclature system has **five functional layers**. Understanding each is essential for KTU part-(b) answers.

### Layer 1 — Primitive (Atomic) Type Names

These are the **non-decomposable** scalar types provided by the language's standard library or built into the compiler.

> [!NOTE]
> The naming convention for primitives often reveals the language's **era** and **design philosophy**:
> - **C / C++** — short, lowercase keywords (`int`, `char`, `float`, `double`, `void`, `_Bool`).
> - **Java** — mixed: lowercase primitives (`int`, `boolean`) **and** uppercase object types (`Integer`, `Boolean`, `String`). The duality is deliberate to support the **two-type-system** model (stack vs. heap).
> - **Python** — lowercase, descriptive class names (`int`, `float`, `str`, `bool`, `bytes`, `complex`).
> - **Ada** — verbose, PascalCase standard names (`Integer`, `Float`, `Boolean`, `Character`, `Natural`, `Positive`).
> - **Haskell / ML** — capitalised constructor names for types (`Int`, `Bool`, `Char`, `String`).

### Layer 2 — Aggregate / Constructed Type Names

Constructed types are built by **applying type constructors** to one or more existing types. The nomenclature here is the *syntax* used for the construction.

| Constructor | C | Java | Python | Ada | Haskell |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array | `int a[10]` | `int[] a` | `list` (dynamic) | `array (1..10) of Integer` | `[Int]` (list) |
| Record / Struct | `struct Point {int x;}` | `class Point {int x;}` | `class Point:` | `record P: ... end record` | `data Point = Pt Int Int` |
| Pointer | `int* p` | (no exposed pointer) | (no pointer) | `access Integer` | (no direct pointer) |
| Function | `int f(int, int)` | `Function<Integer,Integer>` | `def` (no type name) | `access function` | `Int -> Int` |
| Set | (no native) | `Set<Integer>` | `set` | `set of Integer` | `Data.Set.Set Int` |

### Layer 3 — User-Defined Type (UDT) Names

UDTs are **identifiers introduced by the programmer** to name a new type. The keyword used to introduce them varies by language:

- **C / C++** → `typedef`, `struct`, `union`, `enum`
- **Java** → `class`, `interface`, `enum`
- **Python** → `class` (dynamic, duck-typed)
- **Ada** → `type ... is` declaration
- **Haskell** → `data`, `type`, `newtype`

### Layer 4 — Type Parameter / Variable Names

Generic / parametric types use a **type variable** as a placeholder. Each language has its own syntactic convention:

| Language | Type Variable Syntax | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Java | Single uppercase letter (convention) | `class Box<T> { T item; }` |
| C++ | `typename T` or any class name | `template<typename T> ...` |
| Haskell | Lowercase single letter by convention | `id :: a -> a` |
| ML / OCaml | Single quote prefix | `let id x = x`  (inferred as `'a -> 'a`) |
| Ada | Any identifier, declared in `generic` | `generic type T is private;` |
| C# | Any name, often `T`, `TKey`, `TValue` | `List<T>` |

### Layer 5 — Type Equivalence & Compatibility Names

Two languages may give the **same name** to two semantically **different type categories** (e.g., `int` in C vs. Java). This is the most subtle layer of nomenclature.

- **Name Equivalence** (Ada, C): two types are the same *only* if they have the same declared name.
- **Structural Equivalence** (ML, some Haskell): two types are the same if they have the *same structure*, regardless of name.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Type Nomenclature Across Sample Languages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this comparison table for both Part A and Part B questions.** It is the single most tested artifact in Module 2 of PECST758.

### 2.2.1 Primitive Type Nomenclature

| Concept | C | Java | Python | Ada | Haskell |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Integer | `int`, `short`, `long` | `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` | `int` | `Integer` | `Int` |
| Floating | `float`, `double` | `float`, `double` | `float` | `Float` | `Double`, `Float` |
| Character | `char` | `char` | `str` (string) | `Character` | `Char` |
| Boolean | `_Bool`, `bool` (via stdbool) | `boolean` | `bool` | `Boolean` | `Bool` |
| Void / Unit | `void` | `void` | `None` (singleton) | n/a | `()` (unit) |
| String | `char[]`, `char*` | `String` (immutable class) | `str` | `String` (fixed) | `String = [Char]` |
| Arbitrary precision | (via libraries) | `BigInteger`, `BigDecimal` | `int` (auto) | (libraries) | `Integer` (auto) |

### 2.2.2 Type Constructor Nomenclature

| Constructor | C Syntax | Java Syntax | Python Syntax | Ada Syntax | Haskell Syntax |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array | `T name[N]` | `T[]` | `list[T]` (PEP 585) | `array (range) of T` | `[T]` |
| Pointer | `T*` | n/a | n/a | `access T` | (via `IORef`) |
| Record/Product | `struct {...}` | `class {...}` | `class ...` | `record ... end record` | `(T1, T2, T3)` tuple or `data` |
| Sum / Variant | `union` | `sealed interface` | n/a (uses `Union` from typing) | `case ... is ...` | `data Either a b = Left a \vert Right b` |
| Function | `T1 (*)(T2)` | `Function<T1, R>` | n/a (first-class but untyped) | `access function` | `T1 -> T2` |
| Set | n/a | `Set<T>` | `set[T]` | `set of T` | `Data.Set.Set T` |
| Map | n/a | `Map<K,V>` | `dict[K,V]` | `map of K to V` | `Data.Map.Map K V` |
| Tuple | n/a | `record`-like | `(a, b, c)` | `record ... end record` | `(T1, T2)` |

### 2.2.3 Type Variable Nomenclature

| Language | Convention | Example Declaration |
|---|---|---|
| Java | Single uppercase letter, often `T`, `E`, `K`, `V` | `class List<E> { ... }` |
| C++ | `typename` or `class` prefix | `template<typename T> class List { ... };` |
| Haskell | Lowercase, often `a`, `b`, `m` | `length :: [a] -> Int` |
| ML / OCaml | Single-quote prefix | `val length : 'a list -> int` |
| C# | Any name in angle brackets | `List<T>` |
| Ada | Any identifier declared after `type` in `generic` | `generic type Element is private;` |
| Python | Any uppercase name (PEP 484) | `List[int]`, `def func(x: T) -> T` |

### 2.2.4 Type-Declaration Keyword Nomenclature

| Language | Keyword for Naming a UDT | Example |
|---|---|---|
| C | `typedef`, `struct`, `union`, `enum` | `typedef int Age;` |
| Java | `class`, `interface`, `enum` | `class Point { int x, y; }` |
| Python | `class` | `class Point: ...` |
| Ada | `type ... is` | `type Age is new Integer;` |
| Haskell | `data`, `type`, `newtype` | `data Color = Red \vert Green \vert Blue` |
| ML | `type`, `datatype` | `type age = int;` |

---

## 2.3 Why Type Nomenclature Differs — Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility for KTU Answers (high-scoring "Justify" point):**
> The choice of type naming in a language is **not arbitrary** — it is engineered to express the language's typing philosophy. Use the points below as a 3–4 line justification whenever an examiner asks "Why does Java use `Integer` and `int` while C uses only `int`?"

1. **C** — Uses short, machine-mapped primitive names because C was designed for **system programming**, where the programmer must be conscious of memory layout, word size, and direct hardware correspondence. A single `int` keyword is enough because the language is **weakly typed** at the structural level — implicit conversions are allowed.
2. **Java** — Retains C-style primitives for performance (stack allocation) **and** introduces object types (`Integer`, `Boolean`) so that primitives can participate in the **object-oriented type system** (used in generics, collections, reflection). This dual nomenclature is the cost of unifying the primitive and object worlds.
3. **Python** — Uses a single, uniform class-based nomenclature because Python is **dynamically and strongly typed**. The runtime `int` class is the *only* integer; the compiler does not need a separate keyword.
4. **Ada** — Uses verbose, PascalCase names with explicit subranges (`Natural`, `Positive`) to enforce **strong, name-based typing** in safety-critical domains (avionics, railways, defence). Verbosity is traded for unambiguity and certification-readability.
5. **Haskell / ML** — Capitalised type names and lowercase type variables follow the Hindley-Milner tradition, where the **type system is a primary design tool**. The `Maybe Int` syntax expresses a **sum type** in a way that is mathematically precise.
6. **C++** — Keeps C's nomenclature and adds `template<typename T>` so that **parametric polymorphism** is grafted onto a system-programming base.

---

## 2.4 Type Expressions — Formal Grammar

A **type expression** is a textual representation of a type using the language's nomenclature rules. Formally, for a typical imperative language, type expressions can be defined by the following BNF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type\_expr} &::= \text{type\_name} \\
&\vert \text{array\_of} \\
&\vert \text{struct\_of} \\
&\vert \text{pointer\_to} \\
&\vert \text{function\_of} \\
\text{array\_of} &::= \texttt{array}(\text{num}, \text{type\_expr}) \\
\text{struct\_of} &::= \texttt{struct}((\text{name}_1, \text{type\_expr}_1), \ldots, (\text{name}_n, \text{type\_expr}_n)) \\
\text{pointer\_to} &::= \texttt{pointer}(\text{type\_expr}) \\
\text{function\_of} &::= \text{type\_expr} \to \text{type\_expr}
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar is the **theoretical backbone** of how compilers parse type names regardless of the surface syntax used in any particular language.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section is exhaustive by KTU requirement.** Every derivation, every translation, and every comparison step is written out in full — no skipped steps, no "it is clear that…" shortcuts.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translating a Type Expression Across Languages

**Problem:** Translate the following C declaration into equivalent type expressions in **Java, Python, Ada, and Haskell**.

```c
typedef struct {
    int id;
    char name[50];
    float* scores;
    int (*operation)(int, int);
} Student;
```

### Step 1 — Decompose the C type

The C type has four components, each a distinct **type constructor**:

1. `int id` — primitive type `int`.
2. `char name[50]` — array of 50 elements of type `char`.
3. `float* scores` — pointer to `float`.
4. `int (*operation)(int, int)` — pointer to a function taking two `int` arguments and returning `int`.

We will translate each component separately.

### Step 2 — Translate `int id`

The primitive integer type is named differently across our four target languages.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}: & \quad \texttt{int} \\
\text{Java}: & \quad \texttt{int} \\
\text{Python}: & \quad \texttt{int} \\
\text{Ada}: & \quad \texttt{Integer} \\
\text{Haskell}: & \quad \texttt{Int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The keyword/identifier for the primitive integer reflects each language's *naming tradition*. C and Java share the lowercase keyword; Python elevates the runtime class to a type name; Ada uses a verbose PascalCase identifier; Haskell uses a capitalised algebraic-type constructor.

### Step 3 — Translate `char name[50]`

This is an **array constructor** applied to the character type.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}: & \quad \texttt{char[50]} \\
\text{Java}: & \quad \texttt{char[ ]} \;\; \text{(size implicit)} \\
\text{Python}: & \quad \texttt{list[str]} \;\; \text{(dynamic, no fixed size)} \\
\text{Ada}: & \quad \texttt{array (1 .. 50) of Character} \\
\text{Haskell}: & \quad \texttt{[Char]} \;\; \text{(i.e., a list of Char, which is the same as String)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The size `50` is *part of the type* in C and Ada (statically fixed) but is *erased* in Java, Python, and Haskell — the array type itself carries no length information in those languages. This is a major **type-nomenclature consequence** of static vs. dynamic sizing.

### Step 4 — Translate `float* scores`

This is a **pointer constructor** applied to `float`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}: & \quad \texttt{float*} \\
\text{Java}: & \quad \text{No exposed pointer; use } \texttt{float[ ]} \text{ (array reference)} \\
\text{Python}: & \quad \text{No pointer type; use } \texttt{list[float]} \text{ or a NumPy } \texttt{ndarray} \\
\text{Ada}: & \quad \texttt{access Float} \\
\text{Haskell}: & \quad \text{No direct pointer; use } \texttt{IORef Float} \text{ or } \texttt{MVar Float}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The *nomenclature* for reference / pointer types reveals a **language's safety stance**. C exposes raw pointer arithmetic; Java hides pointers behind array-object references; Ada uses an explicit, GC-tracked `access` type; Haskell has no user-visible pointer, only monadic mutable references.

### Step 5 — Translate `int (*operation)(int, int)`

This is a **function-type constructor** applied to a parameter list and a return type.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}: & \quad \texttt{int (*)(int, int)} \\
\text{Java}: & \quad \texttt{java.util.function.BiFunction<Integer, Integer, Integer>} \\
\text{Python}: & \quad \texttt{Callable[[int, int], int]} \;\; \text{(from typing module)} \\
\text{Ada}: & \quad \texttt{access function (I1, I2 : Integer) return Integer} \\
\text{Haskell}: & \quad \texttt{Int -> Int -> Int}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Function-type nomenclature varies wildly because first-class functions have different statuses in each language. Haskell's `Int -> Int -> Int` is **right-associative**, meaning the function takes one `Int` and returns a function `Int -> Int` — a consequence of **currying**.

### Step 6 — Assemble the Full Type Expression

Now we wrap each translation inside the **record / class / data** constructor of the target language.

| Language | Full Declaration |
|---|---|
| **C** | (given) `typedef struct { int id; char name[50]; float* scores; int (*operation)(int, int); } Student;` |
| **Java** | `class Student { int id; char[] name; float[] scores; java.util.function.BiFunction<Integer,Integer,Integer> operation; }` |
| **Python** | `class Student: id: int; name: list[str]; scores: list[float]; operation: Callable[[int,int], int]` |
| **Ada** | `type Int_Func is access function (I1, I2 : Integer) return Integer; type Student is record Id : Integer; Name : String(1..50); Scores : access Float; Operation : Int_Func; end record;` |
| **Haskell** | `data Student = Student { id :: Int, name :: String, scores :: [Float], operation :: Int -> Int -> Int }` |

The **type nomenclature** changes, but the **type's structural intent** (a record with four fields of fixed categories) is preserved.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving Type Equivalence

**Problem:** Given the following Ada declarations, determine which types are **equivalent** and which are **distinct** under Ada's name-equivalence rule.

```ada
type Celsius    is new Integer;
type Fahrenheit is new Integer;
type Color      is (Red, Green, Blue);
type Light      is (Red, Green, Blue);
A : Celsius;
B : Fahrenheit;
C : Color;
D : Light;
```

### Step 1 — Recall the Equivalence Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ada uses NAME EQUIVALENCE for derived types.**
> Two type names are equivalent if and only if they are *literally the same* declared name. **Structure does not matter.** Even if `Celsius` and `Fahrenheit` are both *internally* `Integer`, the Ada compiler treats them as **distinct types**.

### Step 2 — Apply the Rule Pairwise

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A : Celsius} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \text{B : Fahrenheit} & \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{NOT equivalent (different names)} \\
\text{C : Color} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \text{D : Light} & \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{NOT equivalent (different names, even though structure is identical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Test the Compiler's Behaviour

Assigning `A := B;` is a **compile-time type error** in Ada, even though both are internally `Integer`. This protects against the **Mars Climate Orbiter**-class bug, where a US/imperial value was fed into a SI/metric slot because they were both "just integers" in C.

### Step 4 — Contrast with a Structurally-Typed Language

In ML or Haskell, the equivalence rule is **structural**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{data Color}  & = \text{Red} \mid \text{Green} \mid \text{Blue} \\
\text{data Light}  & = \text{Red} \mid \text{Green} \mid \text{Blue}
\end{aligned}
$$

If these two declarations are made in **separate modules**, a structural type system may treat them as equivalent (or not, depending on whether they are made `data` or `type` in ML — `type` is name-equivalent, `datatype` is structurally equivalent).

This example shows **why type nomenclature is more than labels** — it encodes the language's *typing philosophy*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Type Inference from a Haskell Signature

**Problem:** Given the Haskell type signature, identify the type-nomenclature elements present.

```haskell
mapMaybe :: (a -> Maybe b) -> [a] -> [Maybe b]
```

### Step 1 — Tokenise the Type

The type expression has the following tokens:

- `mapMaybe` — function name.
- `(a -> Maybe b)` — function from `a` to `Maybe b`.
- `[a]` — list of `a`.
- `[Maybe b]` — list of `Maybe b`.

### Step 2 — Identify Each Type Nomenclature Element

| Token | Element Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `a` | **Type variable** (Hindley-Milner) | Polymorphic placeholder |
| `b` | **Type variable** | Polymorphic placeholder |
| `Maybe` | **Type constructor** (sum type) | Wrapper for optional values |
| `b` (inside Maybe) | **Type argument** | Applied to the `Maybe` constructor |
| `[a]` | **Syntactic sugar** for list type | Equivalent to `List a` in Haskell's `data List a = Cons a (List a) \vert Nil` |
| `->` | **Function type constructor** | Right-associative |

### Step 3 — Verify the Inference Direction

Reading right-to-left (because `->` is right-associative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mapMaybe} &:: (a \to \text{Maybe } b) \to [a] \to [\text{Maybe } b] \\
&\equiv (a \to \text{Maybe } b) \to \text{List } a \to \text{List } (\text{Maybe } b)
\end{aligned}
$$

This means `mapMaybe` takes a function that converts `a` to an optional `b`, and a list of `a`, and returns a list of optional `b`. The **nomenclature** of `Maybe b` is Haskell's standard way of expressing *partial information*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Type Nomenclature Philosophies

| Dimension | C | Java | Python | Ada | Haskell |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typing Discipline | Static, weak | Static, strong | Dynamic, strong | Static, very strong | Static, strong + inferred |
| Primitive Naming Style | Lowercase keywords | Lowercase keywords | Class names | PascalCase keywords | Capitalised constructors |
| Type Variables | n/a | `<T>` angle-bracket | `[T]` (PEP 484) | `generic type T` | Lowercase `a`, `b` |
| Naming Equivalence | Name (for `struct`) | Name | n/a (dynamic) | **Name** | **Structural** |
| Type Constructors Exposed | Many (`*`, `[]`, `()`) | Few (`[]`) | Implicit | Many (`access`, `array`) | All (ADT first-class) |
| User-Defined Type Keyword | `typedef` | `class` | `class` | `type ... is` | `data` / `type` / `newtype` |
| Verbosity | Low | Medium | Low | High | Medium |
| Primary Engineering Domain | Systems, OS | Enterprise, mobile | Scripting, ML | Aerospace, defence | Compilers, formal methods |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Long-Answer Skeleton — "Discuss Type Nomenclature in C, Java, and Haskell"

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **recommended structure** for a 14-mark answer. A well-organised answer in this skeleton typically scores 12–14 marks.

**Part (a) — Primitive Type Nomenclature (7 marks)**
1. Define type nomenclature (1 mark).
2. Discuss C's primitive naming: `int`, `char`, `float`, `double`, `void`. Emphasise short keywords and machine-mapped design. (2 marks)
3. Discuss Java's dual naming: `int`/`Integer`, `boolean`/`Boolean`. Explain the rationale (stack primitives + heap objects for generics). (2 marks)
4. Discuss Haskell's capitalised constructors: `Int`, `Char`, `Bool`, `()` (unit). Connect to Hindley-Milner type system. (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Constructed & Polymorphic Type Nomenclature (7 marks)**
1. Define type constructors and give the BNF of a type expression. (2 marks)
2. Show translations of an array, pointer, and record type across the three languages. (3 marks)
3. Explain type-variable naming: `T` (Java), `a` (Haskell), and how it expresses parametric polymorphism. (2 marks)

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — The Five-Layer Type Nomenclature Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Type Nomenclature System"] --> B["Layer 1: Primitive Scalar Names"]
    A --> C["Layer 2: Aggregate Type Constructors"]

    B --> B1["C: int char float double void"]

    C --> C1["Array Constructor"]
    C --> C2["Pointer or Reference Constructor"]

    C --> C5["Sum or Variant Constructor"]

    C1 --> C1a["C: T name N"]
    C1 --> C1b["Java: T bracket name"]

    E --> E2["Haskell: lowercase a"]
    E --> E3["ML: quote a"]
    E --> E4["Ada: generic type T"]

    F --> F1["Name Equivalence: C Ada Java"]
    F --> F2["Structural Equivalence: ML Haskell"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a Compiler Uses Type Nomenclature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Source Code Text"] --> L["Lexer / Scanner"]
    L --> TOK["Token Stream with Type Keywords"]
    TOK --> P["Parser"]
    P --> AST["Abstract Syntax Tree with Type Nodes"]
    AST --> TC["Type Checker"]
    TC --> TS["Symbol Table with Type Entries"]
    TS --> GEN["Code Generator or Interpreter"]
    TS --> ERR["Type Error Reporter if Mismatch"]

    TC --> T1["Primitive Type Table"]
    TC --> T2["Constructor Application Rules"]
    TC --> T3["Equivalence Rule Engine Name vs Structural"]
    TC --> T4["Polymorphic Inference Engine Hindley Milner"]

    T1 --> OUT["Typed IR or Runtime Tagged Value"]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, PECST758, Module 2]**

**Q1.** Define **type nomenclature** in a programming language. Give two examples from different languages to illustrate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Type nomenclature is the **systematic collection of names, keywords, and syntactic rules** that a programming language uses to declare, identify, and compose data types. It includes (i) primitive type names, (ii) type constructors, (iii) user-defined type identifiers, and (iv) type variables used in generics.
>
> **Example 1:** In **C**, the integer primitive is named with the lowercase keyword `int` (e.g., `int x;`), and an array of ten integers is named `int arr[10];`.
>
> **Example 2:** In **Haskell**, the integer primitive is named with the capitalised constructor `Int` (e.g., `x :: Int`), and a list of integers is named `[Int]`, which is syntactic sugar for the constructed type `List Int`.
>
> These two examples show that type nomenclature is not just a label — it encodes a language's typing philosophy: C's short keyword reflects system-level design, while Haskell's constructor-based naming reflects its algebraic type theory foundation.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, PECST758, Module 2]**

**Q2.** Compare the **primitive type naming conventions** in **C** and **Ada**. State two differences and justify each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Difference 1 (Casing and Verbosity):** C uses short, lowercase, often single-word keywords for primitive types — `int`, `char`, `float`, `double`, `void`. Ada uses verbose, **PascalCase** identifiers — `Integer`, `Float`, `Boolean`, `Character`. The justification is that Ada is designed for **safety-critical engineering domains** (avionics, railways, medical), where verbose and unambiguous type names reduce misreading in code reviews and certification audits, while C targets **low-level system programming** where brevity and proximity to machine code are valued.
>
> **Difference 2 (Subrange Type Names):** Ada defines a **hierarchy of named integer subtypes** — `Positive` (1 .. Integer'Last), `Natural` (0 .. Integer'Last), `Non_Negative`, and user-defined subranges such as `type Digit is range 0 .. 9;`. C has **no equivalent** — it would simply use `int` for all of these, relying on programmer discipline or runtime checks. This is justified by Ada's **name-equivalence typing**, which prevents accidental mix-up of semantically different numeric ranges.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model, PECST758, Module 2]**

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Study of Type Nomenclature

**(a)** With suitable examples, discuss the **primitive type nomenclature** in **C, Java, and Haskell**. Explain how each language's naming reflects its typing philosophy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a **type expression**. Using a BNF grammar, describe the common type constructors (array, pointer, record, function). For each constructor, give the C, Java, and Haskell syntax. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Type Variables, Polymorphism, and Equivalence

**(a)** Explain **type variables and parametric polymorphism** in Java, C++, and Haskell. Compare the syntactic conventions used to name type parameters in each language. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **name equivalence** and **structural equivalence** of types. Using Ada and ML, show with an example how the two rules lead to different conclusions about whether two types are the same. Explain why the choice matters in safety-critical software. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 Model Solutions

#### 🅰️ Question A Solutions

**(a) Primitive Type Nomenclature in C, Java, and Haskell (7 marks):**

| Language | Naming Style | Example Primitive Types | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C** | Lowercase, short keywords | `int`, `char`, `float`, `double`, `void`, `_Bool` | Designed for **system programming**. Names are short because they map directly to CPU word sizes and the language is weakly typed; the programmer is expected to know the underlying representation. |
| **Java** | Lowercase keywords **and** CapitalCase object classes | `int` (primitive) / `Integer` (wrapper), `boolean` / `Boolean` | Designed for **object-oriented portability**. The dual naming allows primitives to live on the stack (fast) while still being usable as `Object` types in generics, collections, and reflection. |
| **Haskell** | Capitalised algebraic constructors | `Int`, `Char`, `Bool`, `Double`, `()` (unit) | Designed for **pure functional programming**. The capitalisation matches the **Hindley-Milner type system**'s convention: types are values of the type level, and lowercase identifiers are reserved for type *variables*. |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The three naming styles — C's keywords, Java's dual names, and Haskell's constructors — are not arbitrary: each is engineered to make the most common programmer error in that language *visually obvious*. In C, the brevity keeps system code readable; in Java, the duality prevents `int` and `Integer` confusion in generic code; in Haskell, capitalisation forces the programmer to distinguish concrete types from polymorphic variables.

---

**(b) Type Expressions and Type Constructors (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A *type expression* is the textual/lexical representation of a type in a programming language's source code. The vocabulary and grammar of type expressions is what we call the *type nomenclature*.

**BNF Grammar (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{type} \rangle &::= \langle \text{primitive\_name} \rangle \\
&\vert \langle \text{type} \rangle \,\langle \text{array\_ctor} \rangle \\
&\vert \langle \text{pointer\_ctor} \rangle \,\langle \text{type} \rangle \\
&\vert \langle \text{record\_ctor} \rangle \,(\,\overline{\langle \text{field\_decl} \rangle}\,) \\
&\vert \langle \text{type} \rangle \,\rightarrow\, \langle \text{type} \rangle \\
\langle \text{array\_ctor} \rangle &::= \texttt{[} \langle \text{size} \rangle \texttt{]} \\
\langle \text{pointer\_ctor} \rangle &::= \texttt{*} \\
\langle \text{record\_ctor} \rangle &::= \texttt{struct} \mid \texttt{class} \mid \texttt{data}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-Language Syntax Table (2 marks):**

| Constructor | C | Java | Haskell |
|---|---|---|---|
| Array | `int a[10]` | `int[] a = new int[10]` | `[Int]` (a list) |
| Pointer | `int* p` | (no exposed pointer) | (use `IORef Int`) |
| Record | `struct P {int x;}` | `class P {int x;}` | `data P = P Int` |
| Function | `int (*f)(int)` | `Function<Integer,Integer> f` | `Int -> Int` |

---

#### 🅱️ Question B Solutions

**(a) Type Variables and Parametric Polymorphism (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** *Parametric polymorphism* allows a single piece of code (function or class) to operate uniformly on values of *any* type. A *type variable* is the placeholder identifier used in the code's type signature to stand in for the actual type that will be supplied at instantiation/use.

**Per-language conventions (4 marks):**

| Language | Syntax for Type Variable | Example Declaration |
|---|---|---|
| **Java** | Single uppercase letter, conventionally `T`, `E`, `K`, `V`, `R` | `class Box<T> { private T item; }` |
| **C++** | `typename T` or `class T` prefix, with `T` by convention | `template<typename T> class Box { T item; };` |
| **Haskell** | Lowercase single letter, conventionally `a`, `b`, `c` | `id :: a -> a; id x = x` |

**Comparison (1 mark):** Java and C++ use **uppercase letters** to visually distinguish type parameters from values; Haskell uses **lowercase** because in the Hindley-Milner tradition types and values are both first-class, and a notational convention (capitalised constructors, lowercase variables) is the only disambiguator.

---

**(b) Name Equivalence vs. Structural Equivalence (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Name Equivalence:** Two types are the *same* if and only if they were declared with the same type name. Internal structure is irrelevant.
- **Structural Equivalence:** Two types are the *same* if and only if they have the same internal structure, regardless of the names used in their declarations.

**Ada example — Name Equivalence (2.5 marks):**

```ada
type Celsius    is new Integer;
type Fahrenheit is new Integer;
A : Celsius;
B : Fahrenheit;
A := B;   -- COMPILE-TIME ERROR: name mismatch
```

Although both `Celsius` and `Fahrenheit` are internally `Integer`, Ada treats them as **distinct types** because they were *named* differently. This prevents accidentally mixing SI and imperial measurements.

**ML example — Structural Equivalence (2.5 marks):**

```sml
type celsius    = int;
type fahrenheit = int;
val a : celsius    = 5;
val b : fahrenheit = a;   -- LEGAL: structurally equivalent
```

In ML, `celsius` and `fahrenheit` are *synonyms* for `int` (declared with `type`, which is name-equivalent for distinct declarations in ML, but `int` is the *same* built-in name on both sides). If they were declared with `datatype` and had identical constructors, the structural rule would treat them as the same.

**Why it matters (1 mark — the safety-critical justification):** In **safety-critical software** (aerospace, medical, railway signalling), name equivalence is preferred because it forces the programmer to *explicitly* convert between semantically distinct quantities, preventing the **Mars Climate Orbiter**-class bug. Structural equivalence is more convenient in scripting and functional programming, where type names are usually abbreviations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Module 2 of PECST758:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "type" with "data type."** The word *type* in nomenclature refers to the *category*, not a specific value. Writing "an `int` is 4 bytes" loses a mark; writing "`int` is the *name* of the integer type in C, typically mapped to a 4-byte hardware word" scores the full mark.
>
> 2. **Listing primitive types without explaining nomenclature.** Examiners want *why* the language named it that way, not just a list. Always pair each example with a one-line **justification**.
>
> 3. **Treating Java's `int` and `Integer` as the same.** They are deliberately different: `int` is a primitive (stack, no methods), `Integer` is an object class (heap, has methods like `parseInt`). Confusing them in a 14-mark answer is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Skipping the BNF grammar.** When asked to "define a type expression," always include a small BNF or formal grammar block. Examiners explicitly look for this.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the equivalence rule.** When two type declarations are presented, the question is implicitly testing name vs. structural equivalence. Always state *which rule applies in the chosen language* and *what the consequence is*.
>
> 6. **Writing `String` for C or `string` for Java.** `String` is a Java class — in C, the equivalent is `char[]` or `char*`, NOT `String`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master these bullets before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- **Type Nomenclature** = the *lexical layer* of a type system: the keywords, identifiers, and syntactic rules used to *name* types.
- **Five Layers:** (1) Primitive / scalar names, (2) Aggregate / constructed type names, (3) User-defined type names, (4) Type variables / parameters, (5) Type equivalence and compatibility rules.
- **C** uses **short, lowercase keywords** (`int`, `char`, `float`, `void`) reflecting its system-programming, machine-mapped design.
- **Java** uses a **dual naming system** — lowercase primitives (`int`, `boolean`) and capitalCase object wrappers (`Integer`, `Boolean`) — to bridge stack-allocated primitives and heap-allocated objects, especially for generics.
- **Python** uses a **single uniform class-based nomenclature** (`int`, `float`, `str`, `list`, `dict`) because it is dynamically and strongly typed.
- **Ada** uses **verbose PascalCase** (`Integer`, `Positive`, `Natural`, `Boolean`) reflecting its safety-critical, name-equivalent design.
- **Haskell / ML** uses **capitalised constructor names** for concrete types (`Int`, `Bool`, `Maybe`, `Either`) and **lowercase identifiers** for type variables (`a`, `b`), following the Hindley-Milner tradition.
- **Type Constructors** are operators that build new types from existing ones: `array`, `pointer` (or `access`), `record` (or `struct`/`class`/`data`), `function` (or `->`), and (in functional languages) `sum` (or `data ... | ...`).
- **Type Expressions** can be formally defined by a BNF grammar that lists the constructors; this is the *theoretical backbone* of every compiler's type checker.
- **Type Variables** are placeholders for parametric polymorphism. Java uses `<T>`, C++ uses `template<typename T>`, Haskell uses lowercase `a`, ML uses `'a`, Ada uses any identifier inside `generic`.
- **Name Equivalence** (Ada, C, Java) — types are equivalent only if their **declared names** match. Prevents mix-ups like SI vs. imperial.
- **Structural Equivalence** (ML, some Haskell) — types are equivalent if their **internal structure** matches, regardless of name. More flexible, but riskier in safety-critical domains.
- The **Mars Climate Orbiter** (1999) is the canonical example of why *strict, named type nomenclature* matters: a `double` in one system fed into a `float`-expecting system caused a $327 million loss.
- For Part A 3-mark answers: always pair each example with a one-line **engineering justification**.
- For Part B 14-mark answers: structure as **(a) Definition + 2–3 examples** and **(b) Comparative table + BNF grammar + safety argument**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Equivalence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Type Equivalence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal theory of programming languages, **Type Equivalence** is the formal mechanism used by a language's **type system** and **type checker** to determine whether two distinct type expressions denote the *same* underlying type. Equivalence is the strictest form of type compatibility — if two types are *equivalent*, a value of one can be substituted for a value of the other *without any runtime conversion, coercion, or representation change*.

The two principal equivalence relations employed by statically typed languages are:

1. **Name Equivalence** — Two types are equivalent *iff* (if and only if) they originate from the same type **declaration** (i.e., they share the same type name/identifier).
2. **Structural Equivalence** — Two types are equivalent *iff* they have the **same underlying structure** (the same constructor applied to equivalent component types), regardless of where the types were declared or what they are named.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Board examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **Type Equivalence**, **Type Compatibility**, and **Type Coercion**. Many students lose marks by using these terms interchangeably. **Equivalence is a binary relation (`≡`) that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.**

## 1.2 The Intuition — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine two passports issued by the **Government of India**:
- **Name Equivalence** analogy: The passport office checks *who issued* the passport. If the issuing authority is the same, the documents are considered "equivalent" for verification — even if the holder's photo has aged. Two Indian passports = equivalent, regardless of internal content.
- **Structural Equivalence** analogy: A customs officer doesn't care *which country* issued the passport; they only check that the document *looks structurally identical* — same number of pages, same biometric chip, same machine-readable zone, same hologram. If it structurally matches a known template, it is accepted as equivalent.

In the programming world, the **compiler's type checker** plays the role of the verifying authority, and the question "Are these two types the same?" is resolved using one (or a hybrid) of these two strategies.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Type Checking

| Concept | Formal Symbol | Meaning | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type Equivalence** | `T1 ≡ T2` | Types are *identical* — no conversion needed. | Strictest, most common. |
| **Type Compatibility** | `T1 ~ T2` | A value of `T1` *may be legally assigned* to `T2` (e.g., subtype). | Covers subtyping. |
| **Type Coercion (Conversion)** | `T1 → T2` | Compiler/runtime *automatically converts* `T1` to `T2`. | May incur runtime cost. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 outcome for this module maps to **CO2**: *"Illustrate the design choices available in the type systems of various programming languages."* Type Equivalence is a direct design choice.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters

Type equivalence decisions ripple through nearly every phase of compilation:
- **Symbol Table construction** — must track type identities.
- **Expression type checking** — `a + b` is legal only if the types of `a` and `b` are *compatible* (a weaker relation than equivalence).
- **Function call validation** — argument and parameter types must satisfy the language's equivalence/compatibility rules.
- **Module linkage** — across translation units, two separately compiled `struct` definitions may or may not be considered the same type.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Type Equivalence — The Definitional Rules

Two types are **structurally equivalent** if and only if they are built using the same type constructors applied to structurally equivalent component types. Formally, this is defined recursively over the **abstract syntax of type expressions**.

### 2.1.1 Recursive Definition (Type Expression Algebra)

Let `T` denote the set of all type expressions. A structural-equivalence relation `≡_S` is the smallest relation satisfying the following inference rules:

1. **Base Rule:** Every predefined (primitive) type is equivalent only to itself.
   $$ \text{int} \equiv_S \text{int}, \quad \text{float} \equiv_S \text{float}, \quad \text{char} \equiv_S \text{char} $$
2. **Reflexivity:** Every type is structurally equivalent to itself.
   $$ T \equiv_S T $$
3. **Array Rule:** Two array types are equivalent if their element types and dimensions match.
   $$ \frac{T_1 \equiv_S T_2 \quad \text{and} \quad n_1 = n_2}{\text{array}(n_1, T_1) \equiv_S \text{array}(n_2, T_2)} $$
4. **Product (Record) Rule:** Two product types are equivalent if their component types are pairwise equivalent in the same order.
   $$ \frac{T_1 \equiv_S T_3 \quad \text{and} \quad T_2 \equiv_S T_4}{T_1 \times T_2 \equiv_S T_3 \times T_4} $$
5. **Pointer Rule:** Two pointer types are equivalent if their target types are equivalent.
   $$ \frac{T_1 \equiv_S T_2}{\text{pointer}(T_1) \equiv_S \text{pointer}(T_2)} $$
6. **Function (Arrow) Rule:** Two function types are equivalent if domains and codomains match in order, including *contravariance* of arguments.
   $$ \frac{T_2 \equiv_S T_3 \quad \text{and} \quad T_1 \equiv_S T_4}{T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \equiv_S T_3 \rightarrow T_4} $$
7. **Transitivity (Closure):** If $A \equiv_S B$ and $B \equiv_S C$, then $A \equiv_S C$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Function Rule** is subtle. The *domain* (parameter) comparison is **contravariant** under strict equivalence — the parameter types must still match exactly. The covariance/contravariance distinction is more relevant to *subtyping* than to *equivalence* per se.

## 2.2 Name Equivalence — The Definitional Rules

Under **name equivalence**, every named type declaration introduces a *new, distinct* type identity. Two types are equivalent only if they refer to the **same declaration**. Formally:

$$ \frac{\text{decl}(T_1) = \text{decl}(T_2)}{T_1 \equiv_N T_2} $$

where `decl(T)` is the *canonical declaration identifier* of type `T`.

### 2.2.1 Sub-Variants of Name Equivalence

| Variant | Rule | Language Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Strict Name Equivalence** | Even an exact textual copy of a `struct` in two files is treated as a *different* type. | C (separate compilation), Module systems. |
| **Declaration Equivalence** | Two types are the same if defined in the *same declaration* — repeated `struct` declarations may be folded. | Early Pascal, some Algol dialects. |
| **Transitive/Translative Name Equivalence** | Two types are equivalent if their name chains can be *traced back* to the same root type. | Ada (limited form), Modula-2. |

## 2.3 Structural vs. Name — A Side-by-Side Comparison

| Design Dimension | Structural Equivalence | Name Equivalence |
|---|---|---|
| **Comparison Basis** | Internal *structure* of the type. | *Identity* of the declaration. |
| **Aliasing** | Type aliases are *transparent* (a `typedef int myInt` is treated as `int`). | Type aliases are *opaque* (a `typedef int myInt` is a new type). |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Harder — recursive traversal of type AST. | Easier — pointer comparison in symbol table. |
| **Surprise Factor** | High — accidental equivalence possible. | Low — types are distinct by design. |
| **Software Engineering** | Poor — encourages fragile coupling. | Excellent — strong abstraction barriers. |
| **Languages Using It** | Early Pascal, C (loose), ML, Haskell, Go (interface satisfaction). | Ada, Modula-2, Java, C++ (with `class`), C# (`struct` = nominal). |
| **Cross-Module Behavior** | Usually unsafe. | Safe — each module exports named types. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick-Reference Table

| # | Rule / Concept | Formal Statement | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base Equivalence | $\text{prim} \equiv \text{prim}$ iff same primitive. | `int` ≡ `int`. |
| 2 | Array Equivalence | $\text{array}(n_1,T_1) \equiv \text{array}(n_2,T_2)$ iff $n_1=n_2$ and $T_1 \equiv T_2$. | `int[10]` vs `int[10]`. |
| 3 | Record (Product) Equivalence | $T_1 \times T_2 \equiv T_3 \times T_4$ iff $T_1 \equiv T_3$ and $T_2 \equiv T_4$. | Field order matters. |
| 4 | Pointer Equivalence | $\text{ptr}(T_1) \equiv \text{ptr}(T_2)$ iff $T_1 \equiv T_2$. | `int*` vs `int*`. |
| 5 | Function Equivalence | $T_a \to T_r \equiv T_b \to T_s$ iff $T_a \equiv T_b$ and $T_r \equiv T_s$. | Signatures must match. |
| 6 | Name Equivalence | $T_1 \equiv_N T_2$ iff $\text{decl}(T_1) = \text{decl}(T_2)$. | `typedef` creates a new identity. |
| 7 | Transitivity | $A \equiv B \land B \equiv C \Rightarrow A \equiv C$. | Compositional reasoning. |
| 8 | Symmetry | $A \equiv B \Rightarrow B \equiv A$. | Order-independent checks. |
| 9 | Reflexivity | $A \equiv A$ | Trivially true. |

> [!NOTE]
> **For inline LaTeX within text** (e.g., $T_1 \equiv T_2$), always use single-dollar delimiters. **For display equations**, always use double-dollar delimiters with a blank line before and after.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Type Equivalence Matters |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Drives the type-checker, one of the most complex passes in a compiler. |
| **Database Systems** | Schema matching, query optimization, and data integration rely on structural rules. |
| **API / Microservice Contracts** | Strong (nominal) typing prevents accidental cross-version breakage. |
| **Hardware Description Languages (HDL)** | Bit-width equivalence is critical for synthesis (VHDL uses nominal types). |
| **Type Theory / Proof Assistants** | Definitional equality (used in Coq, Lean) is the formal cousin of structural equivalence. |
| **Interoperability Layers (FFI)** | Bridging C and Java requires careful handling of nominal vs structural C `struct`s. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example 1 — Resolving Structural Equivalence of Record Types

> **Problem:** Given the following two type declarations in a hypothetical structurally-typed language, determine whether they are equivalent.

```
Type A:  record { int x ; float y ; }
Type B:  record { int x ; float y ; }
```

### Solution — Step-by-Step Inference

We will use the **Product (Record) Rule** from Section 2.1.1.

**Step 1:** Decompose `A` into its component types.

$$ A = \text{record}(T_{A1}, T_{A2}) \quad \text{where} \quad T_{A1} = \text{int}, \quad T_{A2} = \text{float} $$

*Textual logic:* We break the record into an ordered sequence of fields. In a structurally typed language, the order of fields matters for equivalence.

**Step 2:** Decompose `B` into its component types.

$$ B = \text{record}(T_{B1}, T_{B2}) \quad \text{where} \quad T_{B1} = \text{int}, \quad T_{B2} = \text{float} $$

*Textual logic:* Same structural decomposition for `B`.

**Step 3:** Apply the **Product Rule**.

$$ \frac{T_{A1} \equiv_S T_{B1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{A2} \equiv_S T_{B2}}{\text{record}(T_{A1}, T_{A2}) \equiv_S \text{record}(T_{B1}, T_{B2})} $$

**Step 4:** Verify each component using the **Base Rule**.

$$ \text{int} \equiv_S \text{int} \quad (\text{by Base Rule}) $$

$$ \text{float} \equiv_S \text{float} \quad (\text{by Base Rule}) $$

**Step 5:** Conclude by the **Product Rule** and **Transitivity**.

$$ A \equiv_S B $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Under **structural equivalence**, `A` and `B` are the **same type**. Under **strict name equivalence** (e.g., in Java or Ada), they are **two distinct types** even if their fields are identical.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example 2 — Demonstrating the Trap of Field-Order Sensitivity

> **Problem:** Compare the following two record types.

```
Type C:  record { int x ; float y ; }
Type D:  record { float y ; int x ; }
```

### Solution — Step-by-Step Inference

**Step 1:** Decompose both types.

$$ C = \text{record}(T_{C1}, T_{C2}) = \text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float}) $$

$$ D = \text{record}(T_{D1}, T_{D2}) = \text{record}(\text{float}, \text{int}) $$

**Step 2:** Compare the first components.

$$ T_{C1} = \text{int}, \quad T_{D1} = \text{float} $$

By the **Base Rule**, `int` and `float` are distinct primitives.

$$ \text{int} \not\equiv_S \text{float} $$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Product Rule**. The premise fails on the first component.

$$ \frac{T_{C1} \not\equiv_S T_{D1}}{\text{record}(T_{C1}, T_{C2}) \not\equiv_S \text{record}(T_{D1}, T_{D2})} $$

**Step 4:** Conclude.

$$ C \not\equiv_S D $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume that reordering fields automatically makes types equivalent. Under **strict structural equivalence**, the order of fields in a record type *is significant*. Some languages relax this (Ada's "mode" of record equivalence uses a tagged view), but the textbook rule for KTU exams is **order-sensitive structural equivalence**.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example 3 — Pointer Indirection and Infinite Recursion

> **Problem:** Consider two recursive types used for linked lists.

```
Type P:  pointer(record { int data ; P next ; })
Type Q:  pointer(record { int data ; Q next ; })
```

### Solution — Step-by-Step Inference

**Step 1:** Expand the outer pointer.

$$ P = \text{pointer}(R_P), \quad Q = \text{pointer}(R_Q) $$

where

$$ R_P = \text{record}(\text{int}, P), \quad R_Q = \text{record}(\text{int}, Q) $$

**Step 2:** Assume $P \equiv_S Q$ for the sake of contradiction. By the **Pointer Rule**, we would need:

$$ R_P \equiv_S R_Q $$

**Step 3:** By the **Product Rule**, this requires $\text{int} \equiv_S \text{int}$ (true) **and** $P \equiv_S Q$ (our assumption). The recursion is consistent.

**Step 4:** Formally, the **greatest fixed-point** of the recursive equation is the same for both types. We conclude:

$$ P \equiv_S Q $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Under **structural equivalence**, recursive types are handled via a fixed-point construction (the type is the *limit* of an infinite sequence of unfoldings). This is why languages like ML use this elegantly. Under **name equivalence**, $P$ and $Q$ would be distinct because they have distinct declarations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| # | Scenario | Structural Equivalence Verdict | Name Equivalence Verdict | KTU Examiner's Likely Question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two `int` variables. | Equivalent. | Equivalent. | Trivial warm-up. |
| 2 | Two records with identical fields, same order. | **Equivalent.** | **NOT equivalent** (different decls). | High-yield 7-marker. |
| 3 | Two records with fields in different order. | **NOT equivalent.** | **NOT equivalent.** | Tests field-order sensitivity. |
| 4 | `typedef int Integer;` then compare `int` and `Integer`. | **Equivalent** (alias is transparent). | **NOT equivalent** (new name). | Famous C `typedef` trap. |
| 5 | Two pointers to identical record types. | **Equivalent.** | **Equivalent** (same target). | Pointer indirection rule. |
| 6 | Two functions with swapped parameter order. | **NOT equivalent** (function types are order-sensitive). | **NOT equivalent.** | Tests function-type rules. |
| 7 | Two recursive pointer-to-record types. | **Equivalent** (via fixed point). | **NOT equivalent.** | Tests deep understanding. |
| 8 | `int*` and `char*` in C. | **NOT equivalent.** | **NOT equivalent.** | Tests primitive-pointer rule. |

---

## 3.5 Type Coercion and Compatibility — Extending Equivalence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While **equivalence** is strict equality, **compatibility** is broader. KTU frequently tests this distinction.

### 3.5.1 Type Compatibility ($T_1 \sim T_2$)

A value of type $T_1$ may be assigned to a variable of type $T_2$ if $T_1 \sim T_2$. Compatibility extends equivalence by allowing:

- **Identical types:** $T_1 \equiv T_2 \Rightarrow T_1 \sim T_2$.
- **Subtypes:** If $T_1$ is a subtype of $T_2$, then $T_1 \sim T_2$.
- **Numeric promotion:** $\text{short} \sim \text{int} \sim \text{long} \sim \text{float} \sim \text{double}$ in many languages.

### 3.5.2 Coercion (Implicit Conversion)

A **coercion** is an *implicit* type conversion inserted by the compiler. For an expression $e_1 \text{ op } e_2$, the compiler must check that the types are *compatible* (not necessarily *equivalent*) and may insert coercions.

**Inference rule for binary operators:**

$$ \frac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : T_1 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : T_2 \quad T_1 \sim T_2}{\Gamma \vdash e_1 \text{ op } e_2 : T_{\text{result}}} $$

**Mixed-mode arithmetic example** in C:

```c
int   i = 5;
float f = 3.14;
double d = i + f;   // i is coerced: int → float → double
```

The compiler performs **widening conversions** automatically because `int → float → double` is a *safe* promotion path.

### 3.5.3 The Cost of Coercion

| Coercion Type | Safety | Cost | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Widening (int → long) | **Safe** (no data loss). | Cheap (may be a single instruction). | `int` to `double`. |
| Narrowing (long → int) | **Unsafe** (truncation). | Cheap but error-prone. | `double` to `int`. |
| Pointer reinterpretation | **Very unsafe.** | May require alias analysis. | `int*` to `char*`. |
| User-defined coercion | **Language-defined.** | Operator overloading dispatch. | C++ conversion operators. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Examiners *love* to ask: *"Is coercion the same as type equivalence?"* The answer is **NO**. Coercion produces a *new value of a different type* at runtime; equivalence does not produce any new value — the values are already the same type.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Type Relations

The following diagram captures the hierarchical relationship between the core type-checking concepts. Type relations are arranged in a **set-inclusion hierarchy** — the innermost (most restrictive) is equivalence, and the outer rings are progressively broader.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef strict fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef medium fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef broad fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wide fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Equiv["Type Equivalence\nT1 is identical to T2\nMost Restrictive"]:::strict
    Compat["Type Compatibility\nT1 can substitute for T2\nIncludes subtypes"]:::medium
    Coerce["Type Coercion\nT1 implicitly converted to T2\nAt runtime"]:::broad
    Conv["Type Conversion / Casting\nProgrammer-requested\nExplicit operation"]:::wide

    Equiv ==> Compat
    Compat ==> Coerce
    Coerce ==> Conv

    Note1["Equivalence is a special\ncase of compatibility"] -.-> Compat
    Note2["Coercion may violate\ntype safety guarantees"] -.-> Coerce
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each ring contains the previous one. A value satisfying the *broader* relation need not satisfy the *stricter* one, but the converse always holds: equivalent ⊂ compatible ⊂ coercible ⊂ convertible.

## 4.2 The Structural Equivalence Decision Tree

The compiler follows this decision tree when checking two type expressions. Each leaf represents a verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef leafYes fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leafNo fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Start(["Type Checker Receives Two Types: T1 and T2"]):::process
    Q1{"Are T1 and T2\nnamed types?"}:::process
    Q2{"Do they share the\nsame declaration?"}:::process
    Q3{"Are T1 and T2\nprimitive types?"}:::process
    Q4{"Are they the\nsame primitive?"}:::process
    Q5{"Do T1 and T2 have\nthe same constructor?"}:::process
    Q6{"Are the\ncomponents\nstructurally\nequivalent?"}:::process

    Yes1["Yes — T1 is equivalent to T2"]:::leafYes
    No1["No — T1 is NOT equivalent to T2"]:::leafNo
    Yes2["Yes — apply Base Rule"]:::leafYes
    No2["No — types are distinct"]:::leafNo
    Yes3["Yes — recurse on components"]:::leafYes
    No3["No — different constructors"]:::leafNo

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Yes1
    Q2 -- "No" --> No1
    Q1 -- "No (both are not named types)" --> Q3
    Q3 -- "Yes (both are primitives)" --> Q4
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> Yes2
    Q4 -- "No" --> No2
    Q3 -- "No (composite type)" --> Q5
    Q5 -- "Yes" --> Q6
    Q6 -- "Yes" --> Yes3
    Q6 -- "No" --> No3
    Q5 -- "No" --> No3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Type Equivalence Engine

The following block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a typical compiler's type-equivalence module. It shows the data flow from the parsing stage to the verdict emitted by the type checker.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    SRC["Source Program\nwith Type Declarations"]:::input
    LEX["Lexical Analysis\nToken Stream"]:::process
    PARSE["Parser\nProduces Type AST"]:::process
    SYMTAB["Symbol Table\nStores Type Identities"]:::process
    EQMODULE["Type Equivalence\nModule"]:::process
    NOMECHK["Name-Equivalence\nChecker"]:::decision
    STRUCTCHK["Structural\nEquivalence Checker"]:::decision
    RESULT["Verdict:\nEquivalent or Not"]:::output
    ERR["Type Error\nReport Generator"]:::output

    SRC --> LEX
    LEX --> PARSE
    PARSE --> EQMODULE
    PARSE --> SYMTAB
    SYMTAB --> NOMECHK
    EQMODULE --> NOMECHK
    EQMODULE --> STRUCTCHK
    NOMECHK -- "Same Declaration" --> RESULT
    NOMECHK -- "Different Declarations" --> STRUCTCHK
    STRUCTCHK -- "Same Structure" --> RESULT
    STRUCTCHK -- "Different Structure" --> ERR
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Name-Equivalence Checker** is consulted first because it is the cheapest operation (typically a pointer comparison in the symbol table). Only if the names do not match does the more expensive **Structural Equivalence Checker** traverse the type AST.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **name equivalence** and **structural equivalence** of types. Give one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU 2024 valuation key):**

- **Name Equivalence (1 mark):** Two type expressions are name-equivalent *if and only if* they refer to the **same type declaration** (i.e., the same identifier in the symbol table). The internal structure is irrelevant.
- **Structural Equivalence (1 mark):** Two type expressions are structurally equivalent *if and only if* they have the **same underlying structure**, constructed by the same type constructor applied to recursively equivalent component types. Their declarations may be entirely different.
- **Examples (1 mark):**
  - *Name equivalence* example: In Java, `class A { int x; }` and `class B { int x; }` are two different types even though they have the same structure.
  - *Structural equivalence* example: In ML, `type A = int * float` and `type B = int * float` are the same type because their structures match.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]`

> **Q2.** What is **type coercion**? How is it different from **type equivalence**?

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU 2024 valuation key):**

- **Definition of Coercion (1.5 marks):** Type coercion is the *implicit* (compiler-inserted) conversion of a value from one type to another at runtime or compile time, typically to satisfy the operand-type requirements of a built-in operator or function call.
- **Distinction from Equivalence (1.5 marks):**
  - Type equivalence means two types are *already identical*; **no conversion is performed**.
  - Type coercion means the two types are *different*; **a new value of the target type is generated** by the compiler automatically.
  - Example: `int i = 5; double d = i;` — `i` is *coerced* to `double`, not equivalent to it. The original `i` is an `int`; after coercion, a `double` value `5.0` is produced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 3 — Modified]`

> **Q.A.** **(a)** Define **type equivalence** in programming languages. Explain **structural equivalence** and **name equivalence** with suitable examples. State the formal rules for determining structural equivalence. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following type declarations in a hypothetical language that uses **structural equivalence**:
>
> ```
> Type1 = record { int a ; float b ; }
> Type2 = record { int a ; float b ; }
> Type3 = record { float a ; int b ; }
> Type4 = pointer(record { int a ; float b ; })
> Type5 = pointer(Type1)
> ```
>
> Determine the equivalence relations among these types. Justify each decision using the formal rules. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
Type equivalence is the relation that determines whether two distinct type expressions denote the *same* underlying type in a programming language. A type checker uses this relation to validate assignments, function calls, and other operations. Equivalence is the strictest form of type compatibility: no coercion or conversion is needed if two types are equivalent.

**Structural Equivalence (1.5 marks):**
Two types are structurally equivalent if they have the *same internal structure*, regardless of their names or declarations. The compiler recursively compares the type constructors (records, arrays, pointers, functions) and the component types.

*Example:* In ML, `int * float` and `int * float` are structurally the same, even if declared under different names.

**Name Equivalence (1.5 marks):**
Two types are name-equivalent only if they refer to the *same type declaration*. The symbol-table identifier of the declaration must match.

*Example:* In Java, `class Point { int x; int y; }` and `class Vertex { int x; int y; }` are *not* name-equivalent, even though they have identical structures.

**Formal Rules (2 marks):**
The recursive definition from Section 2.1.1 applies:
1. Base rule on primitives.
2. Array rule: same dimension and element type.
3. Product (record) rule: pairwise equivalent components in the same order.
4. Pointer rule: equivalent target types.
5. Function rule: equivalent domain and codomain.
6. Reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Analysis:**

We check each pair using the rules. Let `≡_S` denote structural equivalence.

**Pair (Type1, Type2):**

$$ \text{Type1} = \text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float}), \quad \text{Type2} = \text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float}) $$

By the **Product Rule** and **Base Rule**, the components match pairwise. Thus:

$$ \text{Type1} \equiv_S \text{Type2} \quad (\text{1.5 marks}) $$

**Pair (Type1, Type3):**

$$ \text{Type1} = \text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float}), \quad \text{Type3} = \text{record}(\text{float}, \text{int}) $$

By the **Product Rule**, we need `int ≡_S float`, which is **false** (primitives are distinct). Thus:

$$ \text{Type1} \not\equiv_S \text{Type3} \quad (\text{1.5 marks}) $$

**Pair (Type1, Type4):**

$$ \text{Type1} = \text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float}), \quad \text{Type4} = \text{pointer}(\text{record}(\text{int}, \text{float})) $$

The outermost constructors differ (`record` vs `pointer`). The rules require matching constructors; the base case does not apply. Thus:

$$ \text{Type1} \not\equiv_S \text{Type4} \quad (\text{1.5 marks}) $$

**Pair (Type4, Type5):**

$$ \text{Type5} = \text{pointer}(\text{Type1}) $$

By the **Pointer Rule**, we compare the targets. The target of `Type4` is `record(int, float)`, and the target of `Type5` is `Type1 = record(int, float)`. As we already proved, the targets are structurally equivalent. Thus:

$$ \text{Type4} \equiv_S \text{Type5} \quad (\text{2 marks}) $$

**Summary Table (0.5 mark):**

| Pair | Verdict |
|---|---|
| (Type1, Type2) | Equivalent |
| (Type1, Type3) | NOT equivalent |
| (Type1, Type4) | NOT equivalent |
| (Type4, Type5) | Equivalent |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024 - Model Paper 4 — Modified]`

> **Q.B.** **(a)** Discuss the role of the **symbol table** in implementing type equivalence. Compare how a compiler implements name equivalence versus structural equivalence. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **type compatibility** and **type coercion** with examples. Discuss **widening** and **narrowing** conversions, and the safety implications of each. When does a language designer prefer implicit coercion over explicit casting? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Symbol Table's Role in Type Equivalence (2 marks):**
The symbol table is the central data structure that records every type's canonical declaration identifier, scope, and associated metadata. When the type checker must decide if two types are equivalent, it queries the symbol table to obtain the unique identity of each type expression.

**Implementation of Name Equivalence (2.5 marks):**
- **Strategy:** Compare the *pointer or unique ID* of the type's entry in the symbol table.
- **Algorithm:** For each named type `T`, look up `T` in the symbol table to obtain its declaration node. Two types are name-equivalent iff they point to the *same declaration node*.
- **Complexity:** `O(1)` per comparison — extremely fast.
- **Limitation:** Fails to detect accidental type duplication across modules unless the modules share a symbol table.

**Implementation of Structural Equivalence (2.5 marks):**
- **Strategy:** Recursively traverse the type's abstract syntax tree (AST) and compare node-by-node.
- **Algorithm:** A function `areStructurallyEquivalent(T1, T2)`:
  1. If both are primitives, return true iff they are the same primitive.
  2. If both are arrays, return true iff sizes match and elements are structurally equivalent.
  3. If both are records, return true iff field counts match, field names match, and corresponding field types are structurally equivalent.
  4. If both are pointers, return true iff target types are structurally equivalent.
  5. Otherwise, return false.
- **Complexity:** `O(n)` where `n` is the size of the smaller type AST. Recursive types need memoization to avoid exponential blowup.
- **Advantage:** Catches accidental equivalence of independently defined types.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Type Compatibility (1.5 marks):**
Type compatibility is a *broader* relation than type equivalence. A value of type $T_1$ is compatible with a variable of type $T_2$ if it can be *legally assigned* to it. Compatibility includes equivalence and subtyping. For example, in Java, a `Cat` object is compatible with an `Animal` reference (assuming `Cat extends Animal`).

**Type Coercion (1.5 marks):**
Coercion is the *implicit* conversion of a value from one type to another, performed automatically by the compiler. For example, in C:

```c
int  i = 10;
double d = i + 3.14;  // i is coerced from int to double
```

**Widening Conversions (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Converting a value to a type that can represent *all* values of the original type.
- **Examples:** `int → long`, `float → double`, `char → int`.
- **Safety:** **Safe** — no information is lost. The bit pattern can be losslessly extended.

**Narrowing Conversions (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Converting a value to a type that *cannot* represent all values of the original type.
- **Examples:** `double → int` (truncates fractional part), `long → short` (truncates high bits).
- **Safety:** **Unsafe** — may lose information. Some languages (e.g., Java) require explicit casts for narrowing; others (C, C++) allow implicit narrowing with a warning.

**When to Prefer Implicit Coercion (1 mark):**
- For **numeric promotions** in mixed-mode arithmetic (e.g., `int + double`), implicit coercion is convenient and matches mathematical intuition.
- For **user-defined types**, implicit coercion can introduce subtle bugs and surprising behavior, so most modern languages (Rust, Swift) prefer **explicit casting** or **type-safe** alternatives.
- The KTU 2024 design principle: **implicit coercion is justified only when the conversion is provably safe and semantically meaningful**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing equivalence with compatibility.** Equivalence is a strict relation; compatibility is broader. The KTU key *will* deduct 1–2 marks for using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the field-order rule.** Under strict structural equivalence, `record{int, float}` and `record{float, int}` are *not* equivalent. Many students incorrectly write "they are equivalent because they have the same fields."
> 3. **Treating `typedef` as a new type.** In C, `typedef int Integer;` does *not* create a new type under structural equivalence, but Java's `class Integer` is a brand-new type under name equivalence. This distinction is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 4. **Ignoring recursive types.** When asked about `pointer(record{...; T next;})`, students often attempt infinite expansion. The correct approach is the **fixed-point** argument.
> 5. **Skipping formal justification.** Even a correct verdict without the formal rule citation loses marks. Always cite the specific rule (Base, Product, Pointer, Function).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Type Equivalence** is the strictest form of type sameness: $T_1 \equiv T_2$ implies no conversion is ever needed.
- **Two main flavors:** **Name Equivalence** (declaration-identity based) and **Structural Equivalence** (recursive structure-based).
- **Five core structural rules** to memorize: Base, Array, Product (Record), Pointer, Function.
- **Equivalence is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive** — it is an equivalence relation in the mathematical sense.
- **Type Compatibility ⊇ Type Equivalence.** Compatibility adds subtyping and widening promotions.
- **Type Coercion ≠ Type Equivalence.** Coercion *creates* a new value of a different type; equivalence requires the values to already share a type.
- **Field order matters** in strict structural equivalence — reordering makes types distinct.
- **`typedef` is transparent** under structural equivalence (C) but creates a new nominal type in name-equivalence languages (Java, C#).
- **Pointer equivalence** depends only on the target type, not on whether the pointer was obtained via `&` or `malloc`.
- **Recursive types** are handled by fixed-point construction, not by infinite unfolding.
- **Symbol table** stores canonical type identities for name equivalence; AST traversal handles structural equivalence.
- **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** — design choices in type systems.
- **High-yield language examples:** Java (strict name), C (loose structural for `struct`), ML/Haskell (pure structural), Ada (strict name with derived types).
- **Safety vs convenience trade-off:** Name equivalence favors safety; structural equivalence favors flexibility.
- **Common KTU trick questions:** `int*` vs `char*`, `typedef` semantics, two identical records, reordered fields, recursive linked-list types.
- **Always cite the formal rule** in your answer — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for proper justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Type Checking** is the process of verifying and enforcing the constraints of types in a program. It is a fundamental phase in the compilation or interpretation of a programming language that ensures every expression, variable, function, and operator is used in a manner consistent with its declared or inferred type. Type checking is performed by a component of the language processor known as the **Type Checker**, which is a form of static analysis.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Programming Languages (PECST758)*, type checking forms the backbone of *type systems* — a tractable syntactic method for proving the absence of certain program behaviors by classifying phrases according to the kinds of values they compute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Type checking is central to Module 2, which focuses on **Data Types, Type Systems, Type Checking, and Scope & Extent**. The KTU examiner frequently tests the difference between **static vs dynamic**, **strong vs weak**, and **name vs structural type equivalence**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine a modern international airport:

1. **Boarding Pass (Type Declaration):** Just as every passenger must carry a boarding pass stating their flight, terminal, and seat class, every variable in a program carries a "type tag" (e.g., `int`, `float`, `char`).
2. **Security Officer (Type Checker):** Before you board, the security officer checks whether your **luggage** (the *value*) matches what your boarding pass *claims*. If your pass says "Domestic" but your bag contains international goods, you are stopped.
3. **Two Inspection Modes:**
   - **Static Checking:** Done *before takeoff* (at compile-time). The officer scans the boarding pass without opening the suitcase.
   - **Dynamic Checking:** Done *during transit* (at run-time). The officer weighs and inspects the suitcase at the gate.

A **Type Checker** is essentially the digital twin of this security officer, ensuring that operations like `5 + "hello"` never slip past without a warning or runtime error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Type checking answers the question — *"Does the way this expression is used make sense given the kinds of values being manipulated?"*

## 1.3 Why Type Checking Matters in Engineering

- **Safety:** Eliminates entire classes of bugs (e.g., buffer overflows, null dereferences, arithmetic on strings).
- **Optimization:** Compilers can generate efficient machine code once types are known (e.g., `int + int` can use a single CPU instruction, whereas generic types require indirection).
- **Documentation:** Types act as machine-checked documentation for software teams.
- **Abstraction Enforcement:** Guarantees that encapsulation and interface contracts in Object-Oriented Programming are honored.

The two critical binary classifications KTU examiners test are:
- **Static vs Dynamic Type Checking**
- **Strong vs Weak Type Systems**

These distinctions are characterized by **5 metrics**: *when types are checked, when they are bound, implicit conversions allowed, polymorphic flexibility, and error detection timing.*
