1. **LIFO Discipline:** A node remains on the tree as long as its descendants are active. A node is removed (along with its subtree) only after all its descendants have returned.
2. **Path-to-Leaf Property:** At any moment, the activations that are currently live are exactly those on the path from the root to the currently executing leaf. Their ARs are the ones on the run-time stack.

*(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for two properties.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives below are fully solved.

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components of an activation record. Illustrate with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the calling sequence of a procedure with the actions performed by the caller and the callee. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Components of an Activation Record (7 marks)

An activation record is a structured block of memory allocated on the run-time stack whenever a procedure is called. Its components, from the top of the stack downward, are:

1. **Actual Parameters** *(1 mark)* — The values (or addresses) of the arguments supplied by the caller. The callee reads them at fixed offsets from its own frame pointer.

2. **Return Value Slot** *(0.5 mark)* — A reserved location where the callee places the result of a function. Its size depends on the return type (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`, 8 for `double`).

3. **Return Address** *(1 mark)* — The address of the instruction in the caller where execution must resume after the callee returns. Pushed by the caller's `CALL` instruction.

4. **Dynamic Link / Control Link** *(1 mark)* — A pointer to the AR of the caller. Used during return to restore the caller's frame pointer.

5. **Static Link / Access Link** *(1 mark)* — A pointer to the AR of the *lexical (static) parent* of the current procedure. Required to access non-local variables in languages with nested scopes (e.g., Pascal, Ada).

6. **Saved Machine State** *(0.5 mark)* — Contents of registers the callee will modify (callee-save convention) and condition codes.

7. **Local Variables** *(1 mark)* — Storage for the procedure's locally declared variables. Allocated at compile time at fixed offsets from the frame pointer.

8. **Temporaries** *(0.5 mark)* — Compiler-generated scratch storage for intermediate sub-expressions (e.g., the result of `a*b` before adding `c`).

9. **Diagram** *(0.5 mark)* — see the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Calling Sequence (7 marks)

The calling sequence is the **coordinated set of actions** performed by the caller, the CPU, and the callee to set up, execute, and tear down a procedure invocation.

**Phase 1 — Caller's Pre-Call Actions** *(3 marks)*

1. **Argument evaluation:** The caller evaluates each actual parameter and stores it in a temp slot (often in the caller's AR).
2. **Register saving:** All caller-save registers whose values are still needed are pushed onto the stack.
3. **Push return address:** The address of the instruction after the `CALL` is pushed.
4. **Push dynamic link:** The current frame pointer (top of caller's AR) is saved as the dynamic link.
5. **Push static link** *(if statically scoped)*: Pointer to the lexical parent's AR is pushed.
6. The `CALL` machine instruction transfers control to the callee's code address.

**Phase 2 — Callee's Prologue** *(2 marks)*

1. **Save callee-save registers** that will be modified.
2. **Allocate storage** for locals and temporaries by decrementing the stack pointer.
3. **Set new frame pointer** (`FP = SP`).

**Phase 3 — Return (Epilogue)** *(2 marks)*

1. Place the return value in the agreed slot.
2. Restore callee-save registers.
3. Restore the old frame pointer from the dynamic link.
4. Execute `RETURN` — the CPU pops the return address and jumps back to the caller.
5. The caller resumes execution and may retrieve the return value.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Mention **who sets each field**. In the KTU marking scheme, the action-by-actor split (caller vs. callee) carries full marks.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** Construct the activation tree for the following program and state the order in which activations terminate. **(7 marks)**

```
main( )
   { A( ); B( ); }

A( )
   { C( ); D( ); }

B( )
   { E( ); }

C( ), D( ), E( )   { /* leaf — no calls */ }
```

**(b)** Explain parameter passing mechanisms — *call by value, call by reference, call by name* — with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Activation Tree (7 marks)

**Step-by-step construction** *(5 marks for tree + 2 marks for termination order)*:

1. Main calls A: $\text{main} \to \text{A}_1$.
2. A calls C: $\text{A}_1 \to \text{C}_1$. C finishes immediately.
3. A calls D: $\text{A}_1 \to \text{D}_1$. D finishes immediately.
4. A finishes; main now calls B: $\text{main} \to \text{B}_1$.
5. B calls E: $\text{B}_1 \to \text{E}_1$. E finishes; B finishes; main finishes.

**Activation Tree (rendered using ASCII for the answer sheet):**

```
            main
           /    \
         A1      B1
        /  \      \
      C1    D1     E1
```

**Order of termination** (LIFO, deepest-first):

$$
\text{C}_1 \;\to\; \text{D}_1 \;\to\; \text{A}_1 \;\to\; \text{E}_1 \;\to\; \text{B}_1 \;\to\; \text{main}
$$

**Justification:** The activation tree enforces strict LIFO discipline — a node finishes only after all its descendants have finished. Therefore, leaves $\text{C}_1$ and $\text{D}_1$ finish first (children of $\text{A}_1$), allowing $\text{A}_1$ to finish; similarly for the right subtree.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Parameter Passing Mechanisms (7 marks)

Parameter passing defines *how* actual arguments are bound to formal parameters.

**1. Call by Value** *(2 marks)*
- The actual parameter is *evaluated* and its *value* is copied into the formal parameter's storage in the callee's AR.
- The callee operates on a *local copy*; modifications do **not** affect the caller.
- Example (C, Pascal):

```
void swap(int a, int b) {
    int t = a; a = b; b = t;     /* does NOT swap caller's variables */
}
```

**2. Call by Reference** *(2.5 marks)*
- The *address* of the actual parameter is passed; the formal parameter becomes an *alias* of the actual.
- The callee can read and modify the caller's variable directly.
- Example (Pascal `var`, C++ reference, Fortran):

```
procedure swap(var a, b : integer);
   var t : integer;
begin
   t := a; a := b; b := t;       /* DOES swap caller's variables */
end;
```

**3. Call by Name** *(2.5 marks)*
- The actual parameter is *textually substituted* for the formal parameter in the callee's body — essentially macro-expansion.
- Re-evaluated **each time** the formal is used. Can produce surprising results (e.g., the famous *Jensen's Device*).
- Used in Algol 60; rare in modern languages but pedagogically important.
- Example (Algol 60):

```
procedure inc(i) := i := i + 1;
```
If `i` is a complex expression, it is re-evaluated at every use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table for Quick Recall:**

| Mechanism | What is passed | Caller variable modified? | Re-evaluation? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Value | Value (copy) | No | No |
| Reference | Address | Yes | No |
| Name | Re-evaluated text | Yes (if assigned) | Yes (each use) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "definition" with "activation"** — Definition is *static/compile-time*; activation is *dynamic/run-time*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix these.
> 2. **Forgetting the action-by-actor split** — When describing the calling sequence, you MUST clearly state which actions are the *caller's* and which are the *callee's*. Vague answers like "the system pushes things onto the stack" lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing static link and dynamic link** — Dynamic link points to the *caller*; static link points to the *lexical parent*. These are DIFFERENT in nested procedures. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Drawing the activation tree incorrectly** — Each node represents *one* activation, not the procedure itself. Multiple calls produce *multiple* nodes with subscripts (e.g., $\text{A}_1, \text{A}_2$).
> 5. **Forgetting the return value slot** — In a *function* (not just a procedure), the AR must reserve space for the return value; omitting this field costs a mark.
> 6. **Omitting the time-complexity justification** — When asked about stack depth, justify with the LIFO property of the activation tree.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Procedure Definition and Activation**
>
> - **Procedure Definition** = *static*; exists in source code; introduces name, parameters, locals, and body.
> - **Procedure Activation** = *dynamic*; created at run-time for *each call*; lives until return.
> - **Activation Record (AR)** is a stack frame holding: actuals, return value, return address, dynamic link, static link, saved state, locals, temporaries.
> - **Size(AR) = Σ parameter sizes + Σ local sizes + linkage overhead + temporaries.**
> - **Dynamic Link** → points to **caller's** AR (run-time parent).
> - **Static Link** → points to **lexical parent's** AR (compile-time parent).
> - **Calling Sequence** is split into *caller's pre-call actions* and *callee's prologue*.
> - **Return Sequence** = *callee's epilogue + caller's post-call resumption*.
> - **Run-time stack** is LIFO because of nested, well-bracketed call structure.
> - **Activation Tree** — root is `main`; parent-child = call relationship; LIFO removal of subtrees.
> - **Path to current leaf** = the live activations = the contents of the stack.
> - **Three parameter-passing mechanisms:** value (copy), reference (address), name (textual re-substitution).
> - **Be precise about units** — distinguish *number of frames* from *bytes on stack*.
> - **One definition → many activations** (this single sentence has earned 2 marks in past KTU papers).
> - **Activation record layout** is the most diagram-heavy sub-topic — practice drawing it from memory.
> - **Activation tree construction** requires careful attention to *temporal order* of calls, not textual order.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Procedure Semantics # 1. Procedure Semantics — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST758 – Module 3):** *Procedure Semantics* deals with the **formal meaning of procedure (subprogram) activations, parameter bindings, scope rules, and lifetime management** as described by the denotational/operational semantics of programming languages. ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Procedure Semantics** is the branch of programming language theory that rigorously defines the **meaning of procedure invocation, execution, parameter passing, and return** in terms of *environment*, *state*, and *control flow*. Formally, a procedure is modelled as a mapping: $$\rho : \text{Args} \rightarrow \text{Values} \quad \text{evaluated under a binding environment } \sigma$$ where: - **Args** = tuple of actual parameters supplied at the call site - **Values** = resulting semantic values - **σ (sigma)** = static environment (compile-time visible bindings) - **ρ (rho)** = dynamic environment (run-time activations) A procedure call is interpreted by the semantic engine using the **activation record (AR)** discipline and the **Control Stack (CS)** mechanism. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Receptionist & Office Cabin > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a busy corporate office. > - A **procedure** = a *meeting cabin* with its own rules (the formal parameter list, local variables). > - A **procedure call** = a *visitor* arrives at the cabin with documents (actual parameters). > - The **Receptionist** = the *runtime system / activation logic* that temporarily books the cabin, gives the visitor a copy of the rulebook, and ensures the original cabin state remains untouched. > - **End of meeting** = the cabin is released, its contents erased from active memory, and the visitor returns to their original desk. > - The **Cabin Register** = the *control stack*, where each active meeting is stacked on top of the previous one. > > This is exactly how **procedure semantics** work: each call pushes a fresh *activation record*, the procedure body executes within that record's bindings, and on termination the record is popped, restoring the caller's environment. ## 1.3 Three Pillars of Procedure Semantics | Pillar | Description | |--------|-------------| | **Activation** | When, where, and how a procedure body begins executing | | **Binding** | How actual parameters are linked to formal parameters | | **Lifetime** | How long activations, bindings, and locals remain valid | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Expectation:** When asked about procedure semantics, always structure the answer along these **three pillars** — *Activation*, *Binding*, *Lifetime*. Examiners allocate marks accordingly. ## 1.4 Subprogram Activation — The Heart of the Topic A subprogram execution is referred to as an **activation**. The lifecycle of an activation has **three phases**: 1. **Activation Entry** — allocation of an *activation record* on the control stack, parameter binding, and transfer of control. 2. **Execution** — execution of the body under the freshly created environment. 3. **Activation Exit / Termination** — destruction of the local environment, return of a value (if any), and transfer of control back to the caller. The set of all live activations at any moment forms the **activation tree** (or its linear representation, the **activation stack / control stack**). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Activation Record (AR) — Anatomy Every procedure invocation creates an **Activation Record** (also called a *stack frame*). The standard layout is: $$\text{AR} = \langle \text{RetAddr}, \text{StaticLink}, \text{DynamicLink}, \text{Params}, \text{Locals}, \text{Temporaries}, \text{RetValue} \rangle$$ | Field | Purpose | |-------|---------| | **Return Address** | Instruction pointer of the caller to resume after the call | | **Static Link (SL)** | Pointer to the AR of the lexically enclosing scope (for static scoping) | | **Dynamic Link (DL)** | Pointer to the AR of the caller (for dynamic scoping / chaining) | | **Parameters** | Storage for actual parameters received from caller | | **Locals** | Local variables declared inside the procedure | | **Temporaries** | Intermediate expression evaluations | | **Return Value** | Cell to hold the value being returned | > [!NOTE] > **Convention used in most compiler textbooks (Aho/Sethi/Ullman, Scott):** The Static Link is **not** needed in purely *dynamically-scoped* languages, while the Dynamic Link is **not** required for the *static chain* traversal — but most languages keep **both** for safety. ## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms — The Big Five Parameter passing is the **core of procedure semantics** for KTU board questions. Each mechanism is a different mapping function: | # | Mechanism | Formal Mapping Rule | Side Effect on Actual? | |---|-----------|---------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Call-by-Value** | $\rho(v) = v_{\text{copy}}$ | **No** (actual unchanged) | | 2 | **Call-by-Reference** | $\rho(v) = \text{addr}(v_{\text{actual}})$ | **Yes** | | 3 | **Call-by-Value-Result** | $\rho(v) = v_{\text{copy}}$ at entry **and** copy back at exit | **Yes** (at exit only) | | 4 | **Call-by-Name** | $\rho(v) = \text{re-evaluate } v_{\text{actual}}$ on every reference | **Yes** (delayed, possibly multiple) | | 5 | **Call-by-Need (Lazy)** | $\rho(v) = v_{\text{evaluated once, cached}}$ | **Yes** (once) | ### 2.2.1 Call-by-Value (CBV) The actual parameter is **evaluated once** before entry, and its *value* is bound to the formal parameter. The procedure operates on a **local copy**. Mutating the formal has no effect on the caller. $$\text{Formal} \leftarrow \text{eval}_{\text{caller-env}}(\text{Actual})$$ ### 2.2.2 Call-by-Reference (CBR) The address (l-value) of the actual parameter is passed. The formal becomes an **alias** for the actual. Mutations inside the procedure are visible to the caller. $$\text{Formal} \equiv \text{Actual} \quad \text{(same memory cell)}$$ ### 2.2.3 Call-by-Value-Result (CBVR) — *Copy-Restore* Two-way copy: at entry the actual's value is copied to the formal; at exit the formal's final value is copied back to the actual. Sometimes produces *different results* than CBR when aliases exist. ### 2.2.4 Call-by-Name (CBN) A **thunk** — a parameterless closure — is created for the actual parameter. The thunk is re-evaluated **every time** the formal is referenced. Originally used by **Algol 60**. ### 2.2.5 Call-by-Need (Lazy Evaluation) A thunk is created, but its value is **evaluated once** on first reference and **memoised** for all subsequent uses. Standard in **Haskell**, `lazy` constructs in some DSLs. ## 2.3 Scope Rules — Static vs Dynamic The **scope** of a binding is the textual/lexical region where the name resolves to that binding. | Aspect | Static Scoping (Lexical) | Dynamic Scoping | |--------|--------------------------|-----------------| | **Resolution Time** | Compile time | Run time | | **Visibility** | Textual enclosure of declaration | Call-stack chain of activations | | **Mechanism** | **Static Chain** (via Static Links) | **Dynamic Chain** (via Dynamic Links) | | **Predictability** | Highly predictable | Depends on call history | | **Used in** | C, Java, Pascal, Python, ML, Haskell | Original Lisp, APL, Snobol, early BASIC | ### 2.3.1 Static Depth & Offsets (for AR addressing) For a language with nested procedures (Algol-style), the **static depth** $d(P)$ of a procedure $P$ is the number of enclosing scopes around it. To access variable $X$ at static depth $d(X)$ from procedure $P$ at depth $d(P)$, the access path length is: $$n_{\text{links}} = d(P) - d(X)$$ The compiler precomputes this **link offset** and emits code to traverse the static chain that many times. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Procedure Semantics > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Quick Reference Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 Board Exams.** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | AR size (worst case) | $\vert\text{AR}\vert = \sum \text{Params} + \sum \text{Locals} + \text{RetAddr} + \text{SL} + \text{DL} + \text{Temp}$ | Bytes / words | | 2 | Stack height at program point $p$ | $\text{Stack}(p) = \sum_{\text{live activations}} \vert\text{AR}_i\vert$ | Maximum depth | | 3 | Static chain access offset | $\Delta d = d_{\text{caller}} - d_{\text{callee}}$ | Steps to traverse | | 4 | Parameter-passing cost (CBV) | $T_{\text{CBV}} = T_{\text{copy}} \times \text{size}(v)$ | Linear in $v$ size | | 5 | Parameter-passing cost (CBN) | $T_{\text{CBN}} = T_{\text{thunk-create}} + \sum T_{\text{eval-thunk}}$ | Variable | | 6 | Activation tree nodes | $\vert V \vert$ = number of *invocations* across the program run | Tree size | | 7 | Reference transparency (CBV) | $f(x) = f(y) \iff x = y$ | Holds only under CBV/CBN purity | | 8 | Aliasing condition (CBR) | $\text{alias}(\text{F}_i, \text{F}_j) \iff \text{addr}(\text{A}_i) = \text{addr}(\text{A}_j)$ | Caused by same actual passed twice | | 9 | Closures | $\text{Closure} = \langle \text{Code}, \text{Env} \rangle$ | First-class functions | | 10 | Tail-call optimization | Removes AR if call is in tail position | Saves stack space | > **Engineering Utility:** Procedure-semantics theory directly powers **compiler construction** (LLVM's stack-frame layout), **virtual machines** (JVM's invoke semantics), and **debugger design** (call-stack unwinding). Mastering it is foundational for compiler engineers. ## 2.5 Closures and First-Class Procedures When a procedure can be **passed as an argument, returned as a result, or stored in a variable**, it is a **first-class procedure**. To preserve its meaning after the enclosing scope has exited, the implementation must capture the lexical environment: $$\text{Closure}(P) = \langle \text{Code}(P), \text{Env}(P) \rangle$$ This is critical in **functional languages** (Haskell, ML) and modern **multi-paradigm languages** (Python, JavaScript, Kotlin, Swift, Rust). ## 2.6 Recursive Procedures A procedure is **recursive** if it invokes itself (directly or indirectly). Semantically, each invocation creates a *new* activation record, allowing independent instances of local variables. - **Direct recursion:** $P \rightarrow P$ - **Indirect recursion:** $P \rightarrow Q \rightarrow P$ - **Mutual recursion depth:** bounded by available stack space; *unbounded recursion* causes **stack overflow**. ## 2.7 Co-routines and Generators A more advanced form of procedure activation: a **co-routine** yields control rather than returning it permanently. The caller's state is preserved across yields. This is the semantic basis of **generators** in Python and `yield`-style lazy streams. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** This section walks through three rigorous worked derivations commonly asked in KTU ESE questions — (A) Static-link offset calculation, (B) Call-by-Value vs Call-by-Reference trace, and (C) Maximum stack height computation. --- ## 3.1 Derivation A — Static-Link Offset for Nested Procedures **Problem Setup.** Consider this Pascal-style nested structure: ``` program Main; var x : integer; procedure A; var y : integer; procedure B; var z : integer; begin { ... use x, y, z ... } end; begin { A } { ... } end; begin { Main } { ... } end. ``` **Goal:** From procedure `B`, determine *how many static links* must be traversed to reach each of the visible variables `x`, `y`, `z`. ### Step 1 — Assign Static Depths $$\begin{aligned} d(\text{Main}) &= 0 \\ d(A) &= d(\text{Main}) + 1 = 1 \\ d(B) &= d(A) + 1 = 2 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Assign Variable Depths (depth of declaring procedure) $$\begin{aligned} d(x) &= 0 \quad \text{(declared in Main)} \\ d(y) &= 1 \quad \text{(declared in A)} \\ d(z) &= 2 \quad \text{(declared in B)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Offset Formula For a reference from procedure at depth $d_{\text{ref}}$ to a variable declared at depth $d_{\text{var}}$: $$n_{\text{links}} = d_{\text{ref}} - d_{\text{var}}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute for Each Variable (Reference Site = B) $$\begin{aligned} n(x) &= d(B) - d(x) = 2 - 0 = 2 \\ n(y) &= d(B) - d(y) = 2 - 1 = 1 \\ n(z) &= d(B) - d(z) = 2 - 2 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation of Results - **z (n=0):** Local to `B`; access via direct offset within current AR. No static chain traversal. - **y (n=1):** Declared in `A`; traverse the static chain **once** to reach A's AR. - **x (n=2):** Declared in `Main`; traverse the static chain **twice** to reach Main's AR. > **Compiler Action:** The compiler emits a fixed load instruction `LOAD_SL n` to descend `n` static links before applying the lexical offset to the variable. --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — Call-by-Value vs Call-by-Reference Trace **Program.** ``` procedure P(a, b : integer); var t : integer; begin t := a; a := b; b := t; end; { main program } var x, y : integer; begin x := 5; y := 7; P(x, y); { Question: what are x, y here? } end. ``` **Trace under Call-by-Value (CBV):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Before call:} \quad &(x,y) = (5,7) \\ \text{At call entry:} \quad &(a,b) \leftarrow (5,7) \quad \text{(copies)} \\ \text{Swap inside P:} \quad &(a,b) = (7,5) \\ \text{After return:} \quad &(x,y) = (5,7) \quad \text{(unchanged — CBV)} \end{aligned}$$ **Trace under Call-by-Reference (CBR):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Before call:} \quad &(x,y) = (5,7) \\ \text{At call entry:} \quad &a \equiv x, \; b \equiv y \quad \text{(aliases)} \\ \text{Swap inside P:} \quad &(a,b) = (7,5) \\ \text{After return:} \quad &(x,y) = (7,5) \quad \text{(swapped! — CBR)} \end{aligned}$$ **Trace under Call-by-Value-Result (CBVR):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Entry copy:} \quad &(a,b) \leftarrow (5,7) \\ \text{Swap:} \quad &(a,b) = (7,5) \\ \text{Exit copy-back:} \quad &x \leftarrow a = 7,\; y \leftarrow b = 5 \\ \text{Final:} \quad &(x,y) = (7,5) \quad \text{(matches CBR here)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** CBVR and CBR *coincide* only when the actuals are **distinct and non-aliased**. When actuals are aliased (e.g., `P(x, x)`), they diverge — a classic KTU 2-mark trap question. --- ## 3.3 Derivation C — Maximum Control-Stack Height **Scenario:** Consider the following C-style program with three procedures: `Main` calls `A`, `A` calls `B`, and `B` calls itself recursively `k` times before unwinding. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Activation sequence:} \quad & \text{Main} \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow B \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow B \;(k \text{ times}) \\ \text{Number of live activations at peak depth:} \quad & N_{\text{peak}} = 1_{\text{Main}} + 1_{A} + k_{B} = k + 2 \end{aligned}$$ If each AR has size $S$ bytes, then: $$H_{\max} = S \times (k + 2)$$ **Special Cases:** | Pattern | $H_{\max}$ | |---------|------------| | **Linear recursion, depth k** | $S \times (k+1)$ | | **Tree recursion (e.g., Fibonacci naive)** | $S \times (2^{n} - 1)$ | | **Tail recursion (un-optimized)** | $S \times n$ | | **Tail recursion (optimized)** | $S \times 1$ | | **Mutual recursion cycle (n procedures)** | $S \times n$ | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Parameter Passing | Use Case | Recommended Mechanism | Justification | |----------|----------------------|---------------| | Large array update inside procedure | **CBR** | Avoids expensive full copy | | Pure computation, no mutation | **CBV** | Predictable, side-effect-free | | Multi-return via parameters | **CBVR** | Two-way communication | | Conditional evaluation / macros | **CBN** | Lazy re-evaluation possible | | Pure functional lazy evaluation | **CB-Need** | Memoises expensive computations | | Embedded systems with no heap | **CBV** | Predictable stack usage | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Activation Record Build-up **Code Fragment.** ``` procedure Q(x: integer); var y : integer; procedure R(z : integer); var w : integer; begin w := x + z + y; { line 1 } Q(w); { line 2 — recursive call } end; begin y := 10; R(5); end. { call Q(3) from main } ``` ### Step-by-step Activation Stack Evolution | Step | Action | Stack (top → bottom) | |------|--------|----------------------| | 1 | `main` running | `[main_AR]` | | 2 | Call `Q(3)` | `[Q_AR(x=3), main_AR]` | | 3 | Inside `Q`, `y := 10` | `[Q_AR(x=3,y=10), main_AR]` | | 4 | Call `R(5)` | `[R_AR(z=5), Q_AR, main_AR]` | | 5 | `w := 3+5+10 = 18` | `[R_AR(z=5, w=18), Q_AR, main_AR]` | | 6 | Call `Q(18)` | `[Q_AR(x=18), R_AR, Q_AR, main_AR]` | | 7 | Q finishes — pop Q_AR | `[R_AR, Q_AR, main_AR]` | | 8 | R finishes — pop R_AR | `[Q_AR, main_AR]` | | 9 | main finishes | `[]` | > [!NOTE] > At **line 1**, accessing `x` requires traversing the static chain from R's AR to Q's AR (depth difference = 1). This matches Derivation A above. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Activation Record — Memory Layout (Block Diagram via Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph AR["ACTIVATION RECORD (Stack Frame)"] RET["Return Address"] SL["Static Link (SL) — to lexically enclosing AR"] DL["Dynamic Link (DL) — to caller's AR"] PARAMS["Parameters (in / out / inout)"] LOCALS["Local Variables"] TEMP["Temporaries"] RETV["Return Value Slot"] end RET --- SL SL --- DL DL --- PARAMS PARAMS --- LOCALS LOCALS --- TEMP TEMP --- RETV RETV -. grows .-> AR ``` > **Reading Guide:** The frame grows **downwards** in most architectures (toward lower addresses). `DL` and `SL` are stored in fixed positions (often at the frame's top) so they can be located in O(1). --- ## 4.2 Control Stack — Runtime Behaviour ```mermaid graph TB TOP["Stack Top (TOS)"] AR3["AR: Inner Call 3
Static Link
Dynamic Link → AR2
Locals"] AR2["AR: Middle Call 2
Static Link
Dynamic Link → AR1
Locals"] AR1["AR: Outer Call 1
Static Link
Dynamic Link → MAIN
Locals"] MAIN["AR: main()
Static Link = NULL
Dynamic Link = NULL"] BASE["Stack Base"] TOP --> AR3 AR3 --> AR2 AR2 --> AR1 AR1 --> MAIN MAIN --> BASE PUSH["PUSH on Procedure Call"] POP["POP on Procedure Return"] PUSH --> TOP TOP --> POP ``` > **Reading Guide:** Every procedure invocation pushes a new AR onto the control stack. The dynamic links form a chain that, when followed, reproduces the **activation tree's current frontier** — the precise set of currently executing procedure calls. --- ## 4.3 Static Chain vs Dynamic Chain — Visual Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SC["STATIC CHAIN (Lexical Links)"] sB["AR: B (depth 2)"] -- SL of 1 --> sA["AR: A (depth 1)"] sA -- SL of 1 --> sM["AR: Main (depth 0)"] end subgraph DC["DYNAMIC CHAIN (Call Links)"] dB["AR: B"] -- DL --> dA["AR: A (called B)"] dA -- DL --> dM["AR: Main (called A)"] end ``` > **Why Both?** > - **Static chain (SL):** Implements *static (lexical) scoping* — bindings are resolved by textual structure. > - **Dynamic chain (DL):** Implements *dynamic scoping*, and is also essential for **stack unwinding during exceptions**. --- ## 4.4 Parameter-Passing Mechanism Flow ```mermaid graph TB CALL["Procedure Call: P(a1, a2, ..., an)"] EVAL["Evaluate Actual Parameters
(in caller environment)"] ROUTE{Route by Mechanism} CBV["CBV: Copy values
into new formal cells"] CBR["CBR: Pass l-values
(addresses) as formals"] CBVR["CBVR: Copy values in
Copy values out at exit"] CBN["CBN: Create thunks
Re-evaluate each reference"] CBNEED["CB-Need: Create thunks
Memoise first evaluation"] BODY["Execute Procedure Body"] EXIT["Exit: Copy-out / Destroy locals / Pop AR"] CALL --> EVAL --> ROUTE ROUTE --> CBV --> BODY --> EXIT ROUTE --> CBR --> BODY --> EXIT ROUTE --> CBVR --> BODY --> EXIT ROUTE --> CBN --> BODY --> EXIT ROUTE --> CBNEED --> BODY --> EXIT ``` --- ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Closure Construction ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CL["CLOSURE OBJECT"] CODE["Code Pointer
(machine instructions of P)"] ENV["Captured Environment
(free variable bindings)"] CODE --- ENV end CALLER["Caller passes closure as argument"] CALLEE["Receiving procedure invokes it"] CALLER --> CL --> CALLEE ``` > **Insight:** Closures are the mechanism that gives **first-class functions** their semantic power — the procedure code is bundled with the environment that makes its free variables resolve correctly, no matter where the closure is later invoked. --- ## 4.6 Activation Tree of `Q → R → Q → R` (Recursive Trace from §3.5) ```mermaid graph TB M["main()"] Q1["Q(3)"] R1["R(5)"] Q2["Q(18)"] R2["R(5)"] M --> Q1 --> R1 --> Q2 --> R2 ``` > **Activation Tree Properties:** > 1. **Rooted** at `main`. > 2. **Preorder traversal** of the tree equals the activation order on the control stack. > 3. **Sibling order** preserves chronological sequence of activations within a single parent. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pattern Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE):** > - **Part A:** 3-mark short questions (definition / concept / small compare). > - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer questions with **internal choice** (typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks). > - Bloom's Levels tested: **L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)**. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define an activation record. List its essential fields.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **activation record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the control stack for each invocation of a procedure. It holds all information needed for that procedure's execution and for returning control to the caller. **Essential fields (any four):** Return Address, Static Link, Dynamic Link, Parameters, Local Variables, Temporaries, Return Value. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks listing with one-line purpose of each.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Distinguish between static scoping and dynamic scoping.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static (Lexical) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping | |--------|---------------------------|-----------------| | **Binding time** | Compile time | Run time | | **Resolution basis** | Program text | Call-stack chain | | **Mechanism** | Static chain (SL) | Dynamic chain (DL) | | **Used by** | C, Java, Python, Pascal | Original Lisp, APL | *(1 mark each for the difference + 1 mark for example.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **five major parameter-passing mechanisms** used in programming languages. For each, describe how the actual parameter is bound to the formal parameter and whether changes to the formal affect the actual. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following Pascal-style program, **construct the activation tree and the control-stack evolution** at every step. Also state the values of `x` and `y` after each call when executed under (i) call-by-value, (ii) call-by-reference, and (iii) call-by-value-result. **(7 marks)** ``` program Demo; var x, y : integer; procedure Swap(a, b : integer); var t : integer; begin t := a; a := b; b := t; end; begin x := 10; y := 20; Swap(x, y); Swap(x, x); end. ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Call-by-Value (CBV):** The actual parameter is **evaluated** in the caller's environment; its resulting *value* is **copied** into a fresh cell reserved for the formal parameter. The procedure works on the copy. **Side effect on actual: None.** **2. Call-by-Reference (CBR):** The *address (l-value)* of the actual parameter is passed. The formal becomes an **alias** for the actual. The procedure directly mutates the caller's storage. **Side effect: Yes, in real time.** **3. Call-by-Value-Result (CBVR / Copy-Restore):** A *value copy* is made on entry; on exit the final value of the formal is **copied back** to the actual. **Side effect: Yes, but only on exit.** **4. Call-by-Name (CBN):** The actual parameter is **textually substituted** into the procedure body (Algol 60). A *thunk* is created and re-evaluated **every time** the formal is referenced. **Side effect: Possible multiple times.** **5. Call-by-Need (Lazy):** A thunk is created but the actual is evaluated **only once**, on first reference; subsequent references return the cached value. **Side effect: Once, if at all.** > **Mark allocation (KTU-style):** 1.5 marks per mechanism × 5 ≈ 7.5 → rounded to 7 marks via integration of comparison. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Activation Tree:** ```mermaid graph TB M["main()
x=10, y=20"] S1["Swap(x, y)"] S2["Swap(x, x)"] M --> S1 M --> S2 ``` **Step 2 — Control-Stack Evolution:** Each `Swap` call pushes a single AR; on return the AR is popped. **Step 3 — Values of x, y after each Swap:** | Pass | Mechanism | After `Swap(x, y)` | After `Swap(x, x)` | |------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | (i) | **CBV** | `x=10, y=20` (unchanged) | `x=10, y=20` (unchanged) | | (ii) | **CBR** | `x=20, y=10` (swapped) | `x=20, y=20` (a==b, so swap is no-op) | | (iii) | **CBVR** | `x=20, y=10` (swapped on copy-back) | `x=20, y=20` (aliased at exit; both copies write 20) | > **Why CBVR gives (20,20) for `Swap(x,x)`:** At entry, both `a` and `b` are aliases of `x`. After swap inside, both hold 20. On exit, the values are copied back in left-to-right order: first `a=20 → x`, then `b=20 → x`. Final `x=20`, `y=20`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Students frequently write the **same answer** for CBR and CBVR. **This loses 2 marks.** Always distinguish: CBR mutates *during* execution (visible to the callee live), whereas CBVR mutates *only on return* (and is sensitive to copy-back order when actuals are aliased). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** What is an **activation record**? With a neat diagram, explain its fields and the role of **static and dynamic links** in implementing lexical and dynamic scoping. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the nested procedure structure below. **Compute the static-link offset** required to access each variable (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`, `f`) from procedure `P`. Also, draw the **static chain** for the call sequence `Main → A → B → P`. **(7 marks)** ``` program Main; var a : integer; procedure A; var b : integer; procedure B; var c : integer; procedure P; var d : integer; begin { uses a, b, c, d, e, f } end; procedure Q; var e : integer; procedure R; var f : integer; begin { uses a, b, c, e, f } end; begin { Q } P; R; end; begin { B } Q; end; begin { A } B; end; begin { Main } A; end. ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Activation Record (1 mark)** — block of memory on the control stack storing per-call state. **Diagram (3 marks):** ``` +-----------------------+ | Return Value | +-----------------------+ | Temporaries | +-----------------------+ | Local Variables | +-----------------------+ | Parameters | +-----------------------+ | Dynamic Link (DL) | ← points to caller's AR +-----------------------+ | Static Link (SL) | ← points to lexically enclosing AR +-----------------------+ | Return Address | +-----------------------+ ``` **Static Link (1.5 marks):** Points to the AR of the **lexically enclosing** procedure. Used to traverse the static chain for resolving free variables under **static (lexical) scoping**. Length of chain traversal = difference in static depths. **Dynamic Link (1.5 marks):** Points to the AR of the **caller** (the procedure that invoked this one). Implements the activation-stack discipline and supports **dynamic scoping** as well as exception unwinding. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Static Depths of Procedures:** $$\begin{aligned} d(\text{Main}) &= 0 \\ d(A) &= 1 \\ d(B) &= 2 \\ d(P) &= 3 \\ d(Q) &= 3 \\ d(R) &= 4 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Variable Depths (declared depth):** | Variable | Declared in | Depth $d_{\text{var}}$ | |----------|-------------|------------------------| | `a` | Main | 0 | | `b` | A | 1 | | `c` | B | 2 | | `d` | P | 3 | | `e` | Q | 3 | | `f` | R | 4 | **Step 3 — Static-Link Offsets from procedure `P` (at depth 3):** $$n_{\text{links}} = d(P) - d_{\text{var}} = 3 - d_{\text{var}}$$ | Variable | $d_{\text{var}}$ | Offset $n = 3 - d_{\text{var}}$ | Traversal | |----------|------------------|----------------------------------|-----------| | `a` | 0 | 3 | Main ← A ← B ← P (3 hops) | | `b` | 1 | 2 | A ← B ← P (2 hops) | | `c` | 2 | 1 | B ← P (1 hop) | | `d` | 3 | 0 | Local to P (no traversal) | | `e` | 3 | 0 | Local to Q — **NOT accessible from P** | | `f` | 4 | — | **NOT accessible from P** (declared in nested deeper scope) | **Step 4 — Static Chain for `Main → A → B → P`:** ```mermaid graph LR P_AR["P (depth 3)"] -- SL --> B_AR["B (depth 2)"] B_AR -- SL --> A_AR["A (depth 1)"] A_AR -- SL --> MAIN_AR["Main (depth 0)"] MAIN_AR -- SL --> NULL["NULL (outermost)"] ``` > **Observation:** From `P`, we descend 3 static links to reach `Main`'s AR and find `a`. Variables `e` and `f` belong to siblings `Q`/`R` and are **not visible** to `P` — a common KTU trap for students who confuse *lexical enclosure* with *sibling scope*. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on Procedure Semantics questions:** > 1. **Confusing CBR with CBVR** — always state *when* mutation occurs (live vs on-return). > 2. **Forgetting aliasing effects** — when the *same* actual is passed twice, results can diverge from intuition. > 3. **Mixing up static and dynamic links** — SL = lexical parent, DL = runtime caller. > 4. **Wrong static-depth formula direction** — offset = `d_current − d_target`, not the other way. > 5. **Omitting the activation tree** — KTU examiners specifically look for the *tree* as part of a 14-mark answer; missing it costs 2–3 marks even if the rest is correct. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Procedure Semantics (PECST758 / Module 3)** - **Procedure Semantics** = formal study of activation, binding, and lifetime of subprograms. - **Activation Record** holds: Return Address, Static Link, Dynamic Link, Params, Locals, Temporaries, Return Value. - **Five Parameter-Passing Mechanisms** (memorize side-effect behaviour): - CBV → copy in, no copy out - CBR → alias (address pass) - CBVR → copy in, copy out (sensitive to aliasing) - CBN → thunk re-evaluated on each reference - CB-Need → thunk memoised after first evaluation - **Static Scoping** = lexical (compile-time), via **static links / chain**. - **Dynamic Scoping** = runtime, via **dynamic links / chain**. - **Static-depth offset formula:** $n = d_{\text{caller}} - d_{\text{target}}$ — traverse that many static links. - **Closure** = $\langle \text{Code}, \text{Env} \rangle$ — required for first-class procedures. - **Activation tree** root = `main`; its preorder = activation order; siblings = chronological calls. - **Tail-call optimization** removes the AR of the caller when the call is in tail position. - **Mutual recursion** depth bound = product of stack size and average AR size. - **Closures, generators, and coroutines** all extend the basic call/return discipline. - **Aliasing** in CBR/CBVR is the most-tested corner case in KTU 14-mark problems. - Always pair your answer with a **diagram** (AR layout, static chain, or activation tree) — examiners reward visual clarity. --- **End of Notes — Module 3 / Topic: Procedure Semantics** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) aligned.*

topics.title: Parameter-Passing Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parameter-Passing Mechanisms ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Parameter-Passing Mechanisms** refer to the standardized semantic protocols implemented by a programming language's run-time system (compiler/interpreter) to bind **actual parameters (arguments)** supplied at the **subprogram call site** to the **formal parameters (parameters)** declared in the **subprogram header**. In the formal framework of Programming Language Theory, parameter passing is governed by three foundational concepts that every KTU 2024 Scheme student must internalize: - **Formal Parameter**: The variable name listed in the subprogram declaration that acts as a *local identifier* within the subprogram's scope. - **Actual Parameter (Argument)**: The expression, variable, or constant passed to the subprogram at the call site. - **Binding Mechanism**: The language-defined rule that creates the link between the formal and actual parameter. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically expects students to differentiate between **positional parameters** (bound by ordinal position) and **keyword parameters** (bound by explicit name matching). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **post office parcel system** as an analogy for parameter passing: | Analogy Component | Programming Equivalent | |---|---| | Parcel sender writes name of recipient on box | Caller writes actual parameter | | Box has a fixed slot for the name | Formal parameter declaration | | Delivery clerk follows written rule to place parcel | Language's parameter-passing mechanism | | Recipient gets parcel (read-only) vs receives replacement (modifiable) | Pass-by-Value vs Pass-by-Reference | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition**: The fundamental difference between all parameter-passing mechanisms is the question — *"Who owns the data after the call?"* If the called subprogram gets a *copy*, it is pass-by-value. If it gets a *reference (handle)*, modifications propagate back. Every other mechanism is a clever variation of these two extremes. ### 1.3 Classification Overview The standard KTU taxonomy classifies parameter-passing mechanisms into the following categories: 1. **Positional vs Keyword Parameters** (syntactic binding styles) 2. **Default Parameters** (compile-time defaults) 3. **Variable-Length Parameter Lists** (varargs) 4. **Pass-by-Value (In-mode)** 5. **Pass-by-Reference (In-out-mode)** 6. **Pass-by-Result (Out-mode)** 7. **Pass-by-Value-Result (In/Out mode)** 8. **Pass-by-Name (Thunk-based)** 9. **Pass-by-Reference-Value / Read-Only References** (modern hybrid) The **physical constant** central to runtime efficiency of parameter passing is the **word-size of the underlying architecture** — typically **32 bits (4 bytes)** or **64 bits (8 bytes)** — which determines the cost of copying versus passing a reference pointer. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Mechanism #### 2.1.1 Positional vs Keyword Parameters **Positional parameters** rely on the *left-to-right ordinal position* of arguments matching the order of formal parameters in the declaration. The compiler performs this binding at **compile time** without storing parameter names in the runtime symbol table. **Keyword parameters** explicitly associate the formal parameter name with the actual value, allowing arbitrary ordering. The compiler builds a name-value lookup table at the call site. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Python supports both — `func(a, b, c)` is positional, while `func(c=10, a=5, b=2)` uses keyword binding. Ada requires keyword notation for clarity in subprogram calls. #### 2.1.2 Default Parameters Default parameters are evaluated *at the call site* if the corresponding actual argument is omitted. They enable backward-compatible API evolution without breaking existing client code. #### 2.1.3 Variable-Length Parameter Lists (Varargs) A function accepting an arbitrary number of trailing arguments. Implementation uses either: - A *count + pointer* tuple (C-style `printf`) - A *null-terminated* list - An *array object* with explicit length (Java `Object... args`) - An *iterator/pair-iterator* pair (C++ `initializer_list`) #### 2.1.4 Pass-by-Value (In-Mode) The actual parameter is **evaluated**, and its *r-value* is copied into the formal parameter's storage location. The formal parameter behaves as a local variable initialized with the actual value. Any modification to the formal parameter inside the subprogram **does not affect** the actual parameter. **Semantics**: $$ \text{formal}_i \leftarrow \text{eval}(\text{actual}_i) $$ #### 2.1.5 Pass-by-Reference (In-Out-Mode) The address (l-value) of the actual parameter is passed to the subprogram. The formal parameter becomes an *alias* for the actual parameter. Modifications to the formal parameter are **directly visible** to the caller. **Semantics**: $$ \text{formal}_i \equiv \text{actual}_i \quad \text{(same memory cell)} $$ #### 2.1.6 Pass-by-Result (Out-Mode) A two-phase operation: 1. **At call**: The formal parameter is treated as an uninitialized local variable. 2. **At return**: The value of the formal parameter is *copied back* to the actual parameter's location. Primarily used in **Ada** with mode `out`. #### 2.1.7 Pass-by-Value-Result (In/Out-Mode) A combination of Pass-by-Value and Pass-by-Result: 1. **At call**: Copy actual value into formal (like pass-by-value). 2. **At return**: Copy final formal value back to actual (like pass-by-result). This is the implementation strategy used by **Fortran IV** and modern Java for non-primitive types (with object references). #### 2.1.8 Pass-by-Name (Thunk-Based) The formal parameter is replaced textually by the actual parameter expression everywhere it appears in the subprogram body. Each access re-evaluates the actual expression in the *caller's environment*. This requires **thunks** (closures) for implementation. Used historically in **Algol 60** and **Simula**. #### 2.1.9 Pass-by-Reference-Value (Hybrid) A modern compromise where references are passed by value. Prevents *aliasing* issues by ensuring the reference itself cannot be reassigned, but the underlying object remains mutable. Used in **C++** (`T&`), **Rust** (`&mut`), and **Java** (for object references). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The table below is the KTU 2024 ESE high-yield summary** — memorize the `Effect` and `Alias` columns for any parameter-passing question. | Mechanism | In / Out | Allocates Copy? | Modifies Caller? | Alias Created? | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Pass-by-Value | In | Yes | No | No | C, Java (primitives), Pascal | | Pass-by-Reference | In / Out | No | Yes | Yes | Fortran IV, Pascal `var`, C++ `&` | | Pass-by-Result | Out | Yes (return only) | Yes | No | Ada `out` | | Pass-by-Value-Result | In / Out | Yes (twice) | Yes | No | Fortran, Ada `in out` | | Pass-by-Name | In / Out | No (thunk) | Yes (on access) | Sometimes | Algol 60, Simula | | Pass-by-Ref-Value | In / Out | No (pointer copy) | Yes | No (rebind blocked) | C++, Java, Rust | **Memory-Cost Formula** (analyze efficiency): $$ \text{Total Call Cost} = \text{Setup Overhead} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{BindCost}_i + \text{CopyCost}_i \right) $$ Where for a parameter of size $S_i$ bytes: $$ \text{CopyCost}_i^{\text{value}} = O(S_i), \qquad \text{CopyCost}_i^{\text{reference}} = O(W) $$ with $W$ = machine word size (typically **4 bytes** for 32-bit, **8 bytes** for 64-bit architectures). ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Embedded Systems**: Pass-by-value is preferred to avoid unexpected memory aliasing in real-time control loops. - **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**: Pass-by-reference-avoidance for large arrays; explicit pointer passing used for cache-line alignment. - **Operating Systems**: Kernel functions use pass-by-reference extensively for in/out system call parameters (`read(fd, &buf, n)`). - **Functional Programming**: Closures inherently implement pass-by-name through lexical binding — a critical concept for understanding Haskell, Lisp, and JavaScript. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Trace Example Consider the following universal trace example used across all parameter-passing analyses: ``` Main Program Subprogram swap(p, q) ----------- --------------------- x := 10 t := p y := 20 p := q call swap(x, y) q := t print x, y ``` Initial state: $\text{x} = 10, \text{y} = 20$. --- #### 3.1.1 Trace Under Pass-by-Value **Step 1**: At call, evaluate and copy r-values. $$ p \leftarrow 10, \quad q \leftarrow 20 $$ **Step 2**: Inside subprogram, execute `t := p` → $t = 10$. **Step 3**: Execute `p := q` → $p = 20$. **Step 4**: Execute `q := t` → $q = 10$. **Step 5**: Return. Local copies $p, q, t$ are destroyed. **Step 6**: Caller's variables unchanged. $$ \text{Final: } x = 10, \quad y = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \text{No swap visible.} $$ --- #### 3.1.2 Trace Under Pass-by-Reference **Step 1**: At call, bind formal parameters to actual parameter addresses. $$ p \equiv \text{addr}(x), \quad q \equiv \text{addr}(y) $$ **Step 2**: Execute `t := p` → $t = 10$ (dereferencing). **Step 3**: Execute `p := q` → $x \leftarrow y = 20$. **Step 4**: Execute `q := t` → $y \leftarrow t = 10$. **Step 5**: Return. $$ \text{Final: } x = 20, \quad y = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Swap successful.} $$ --- #### 3.1.3 Trace Under Pass-by-Value-Result **Step 1**: At call, copy r-values into local storage of formal parameters. $$ p \leftarrow 10, \quad q \leftarrow 20 $$ **Step 2**: Execute swap logic on local copies as in 3.1.1. $$ p_{\text{local}} = 20, \quad q_{\text{local}} = 10 $$ **Step 3**: At return, copy values back to actual parameter locations. $$ x \leftarrow p_{\text{local}} = 20, \quad y \leftarrow q_{\text{local}} = 10 $$ **Step 4**: $$ \text{Final: } x = 20, \quad y = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Swap successful (but copy-based).} $$ --- #### 3.1.4 Trace Under Pass-by-Result **Step 1**: At call, allocate uninitialized local storage. $$ p \leftarrow \text{undefined}, \quad q \leftarrow \text{undefined} $$ **Step 2**: Execute swap logic. $$ p_{\text{local}} = 20, \quad q_{\text{local}} = 10 $$ **Step 3**: At return, copy back. $$ x \leftarrow 20, \quad y \leftarrow 10 $$ --- #### 3.1.5 Trace Under Pass-by-Name **Step 1**: At call, no copy made; formal parameters replaced by textual substitution. **Step 2**: Subprogram body becomes: $$ t := x; \quad x := y; \quad y := t $$ **Step 3**: Re-evaluation in caller's environment yields: $$ x = 20, \quad y = 10 $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix The following **side-by-side derivation matrix** is a guaranteed KTU question pattern. Memorize the outcomes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial State:} \quad & (x, y) = (10, 20) \\[4pt] \text{After Pass-by-Value:} \quad & (x, y) = (10, 20) \\[4pt] \text{After Pass-by-Reference:} \quad & (x, y) = (20, 10) \\[4pt] \text{After Pass-by-Result:} \quad & (x, y) = (20, 10) \\[4pt] \text{After Pass-by-Value-Result:} \quad & (x, y) = (20, 10) \\[4pt] \text{After Pass-by-Name:} \quad & (x, y) = (20, 10) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Aliasing Proof for Pass-by-Reference **Theorem (Aliasing)**: Under pass-by-reference, if the same variable is passed as two distinct actual parameters, the corresponding formal parameters become *aliases* of the same memory cell. **Proof**: Let actual parameters $a_1$ and $a_2$ refer to the same memory location $L$. The reference-passing semantics assigns: $$ \text{formal}_1 \equiv \text{formal}_2 \equiv L $$ Hence any assignment to $\text{formal}_1$ is immediately reflected in $\text{formal}_2$, and vice versa. This violates the *referential transparency* expected in pure functional paradigms. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.4 Cost Analysis Derivation For a function with $n$ parameters each of size $S_i$ on a machine with word size $W$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{\text{value}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(S_i) = O\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right) \\[4pt] \text{Cost}_{\text{reference}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(W) = O(n \cdot W) \\[4pt] \text{Speedup Ratio} \quad R &= \frac{\text{Cost}_{\text{value}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{reference}}} = \frac{\sum S_i}{n \cdot W} \end{aligned} $$ For large aggregate types where $S_i \gg W$, we get $R \gg 1$, confirming reference passing is asymptotically cheaper. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Memory Layout for Pass-by-Value vs Pass-by-Reference ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CALLER["CALLER STACK FRAME"] ax["x = 10
addr 0x100"] ay["y = 20
addr 0x104"] end subgraph CALLEE["CALLEE STACK FRAME PASS BY VALUE"] fp1["p = 10
addr 0x200
COPY"] fq1["q = 20
addr 0x204
COPY"] end ax -. copies r value .-> fp1 ay -. copies r value .-> fq1 ``` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CALLER2["CALLER STACK FRAME"] bx["x = 10
addr 0x100"] by["y = 20
addr 0x104"] end subgraph CALLEE2["CALLEE STACK FRAME PASS BY REFERENCE"] fp2["p = 0x100
addr 0x200
POINTER"] fq2["q = 0x104
addr 0x208
POINTER"] end fp2 ==>|alias| bx fq2 ==>|alias| by ``` ### 4.2 Parameter Passing Mechanism Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD PPM["PARAMETER PASSING MECHANISMS"] PPM --> SYN["SYNTACTIC"] PPM --> SEM["SEMANTIC"] SYN --> POS["Positional"] SYN --> KEY["Keyword"] SYN --> DEF["Default"] SYN --> VAR["Varargs"] SEM --> INV["In Mode"] SEM --> OUV["Out Mode"] SEM --> IOV["In Out Mode"] INV --> PBV["Pass by Value"] IOV --> PBR["Pass by Reference"] OUV --> PBRES["Pass by Result"] IOV --> PBVR["Pass by Value Result"] IOV --> PBN["Pass by Name"] IOV --> PBRV["Pass by Reference Value"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Subprogram Call ```mermaid flowchart TD CALLER_BLK["CALLER MODULE
Compiles actuals"] ARG_EVAL["ARGUMENT EVALUATOR
Computes r value or l value"] BIND["BINDING ENGINE
Performs mechanism selection"] COPY["COPY UNIT
Allocates and writes memory"] ALIAS["ALIAS UNIT
Stores pointer alias"] CALLEE_BLK["CALLEE MODULE
Executes body"] COPYBACK["COPYBACK UNIT
Returns values to caller"] RESULT["CALLER RECEIVES RESULT"] CALLER_BLK --> ARG_EVAL ARG_EVAL --> BIND BIND -->|value mode| COPY BIND -->|reference mode| ALIAS COPY --> CALLEE_BLK ALIAS --> CALLEE_BLK CALLEE_BLK --> COPYBACK COPYBACK --> RESULT ``` ### 4.4 Lifecycle of a Pass-by-Value-Result Call ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller participant Runtime participant Callee Caller->>Runtime: invoke sub(actual_x, actual_y) Runtime->>Runtime: COPY actual_x to formal_p Runtime->>Runtime: COPY actual_y to formal_q Runtime->>Callee: begin execution Callee->>Callee: t = p Callee->>Callee: p = q Callee->>Callee: q = t Callee->>Runtime: return Runtime->>Caller: COPY formal_p back to actual_x Runtime->>Caller: COPY formal_q back to actual_y ``` ### 4.5 Decision Matrix for Mechanism Selection ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1{"Large struct
size greater than 2W?"} Q2{"Need caller
to see mutation?"} Q3{"Need referential
transparency?"} A1["Use Pass by
Reference Value"] A2["Use Pass
by Value"] A3["Use Pass
by Reference"] A4["Use Pure
Functional Style"] Q1 -->|Yes| A1 Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| Q3 Q2 -->|No| A2 Q3 -->|Yes| A4 Q3 -->|No| A3 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Differentiate between **formal parameters** and **actual parameters** with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Formal Parameter | Actual Parameter | |---|---|---| | Definition | Variable name in subprogram header | Expression/variable at call site | | Scope | Local to subprogram | Caller's scope | | Binding time | Compile-time declaration | Call-time evaluation | **Example (1 mark):** ``` procedure add(A, B: integer); // A, B are formal ... add(x, y); // x, y are actual ``` **Rubric**: Definition (1m), Difference table (1m), Example (1m). --- **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q2.** What is **pass-by-reference**? State **two disadvantages**. **Model Answer (1 + 1 + 1 marks):** **Definition**: In pass-by-reference, the *address* of the actual parameter is passed to the subprogram, making the formal parameter an alias for the actual parameter. **Disadvantages**: 1. **Aliasing**: Same variable passed twice creates unexpected coupling. 2. **Side effects**: Reduces program readability and referential transparency. 3. **Security risk**: Subprogram can accidentally or maliciously modify caller state. --- ### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain **pass-by-value**, **pass-by-reference**, and **pass-by-value-result** mechanisms with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Pass-by-Value (2 marks):* Actual parameter's value is copied into a fresh local variable inside the callee. Modifications do not propagate back. Used in C, Java primitives. *Pass-by-Reference (2 marks):* Address of actual parameter is passed; formal parameter becomes an alias. Modifications are visible. Used in Pascal `var`, C++ references. *Pass-by-Value-Result (2 marks):* Hybrid — value is copied in at call, and result is copied out at return. Used in Fortran IV, Ada `in out`. *Neat diagram (1 mark):* Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.1. **(b)** Consider the following program and determine the output under **pass-by-reference** and **pass-by-value-result**. Justify. **(7 marks)** ``` int x = 5, y = 10; void sub(int a, int b) { a = a + 1; b = b + 2; print(a, b); } main() { sub(x, y); print(x, y); } ``` **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Initial State:** $$ (x, y) = (5, 10) $$ **Pass-by-Reference Trace:** - Bind $a \equiv \text{addr}(x)$, $b \equiv \text{addr}(y)$ - Execute $a = a + 1$ → $x$ becomes $6$ - Execute $b = b + 2$ → $y$ becomes $12$ - First print: $a = 6, b = 12$ - Return. Second print: $x = 6, y = 12$ **Final Output (Pass-by-Reference):** ``` 6 12 6 12 ``` **Pass-by-Value-Result Trace:** - Copy: $a = 5, b = 10$ (local) - Execute $a = a + 1$ → local $a = 6$ - Execute $b = b + 2$ → local $b = 12$ - First print: $a = 6, b = 12$ - Return. Copy back: $x = 6, y = 12$ - Second print: $x = 6, y = 12$ **Final Output (Pass-by-Value-Result):** ``` 6 12 6 12 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout** > - Students frequently confuse **pass-by-value-result with pass-by-reference** and claim aliasing occurs — *it does not*. Cost of both copy-in and copy-out must be highlighted for full marks. > - Forgetting to copy back at return loses 2 marks. > - For 7-mark sub-parts, always show the **state trace** with both before and after values; mere narrative explanation gets only 3 marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Compare the five major parameter-passing mechanisms in a single table covering the criteria: **In/Out mode**, **modifies caller?**, **creates alias?**, and **typical language usage**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer Table (7 marks):** | Mechanism | Mode | Modifies Caller | Creates Alias | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pass-by-Value | In | No | No | C, Java, Pascal | | Pass-by-Reference | In / Out | Yes | Yes | Fortran, C++ `&` | | Pass-by-Result | Out | Yes | No | Ada `out` | | Pass-by-Value-Result | In / Out | Yes | No | Ada `in out`, Fortran | | Pass-by-Name | In / Out | Yes | No (thunk-based) | Algol 60 | **Rubric**: 5 mechanisms × 1 mark each, plus 2 marks for column selection justification. --- **(b)** Explain **keyword parameters** and **default parameters** with Python code examples. When are **varargs** necessary? Provide a C-style `printf` analysis. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Keyword Parameters (2 marks):** ```python def greet(name, msg): print(msg, name) greet(msg="Hello", name="Alice") # order independent ``` **Default Parameters (2 marks):** ```python def power(base, exp=2): return base ** exp print(power(5)) # uses exp=2 → 25 print(power(5, 3)) # explicit → 125 ``` **Varargs — C printf (3 marks):** `printf(const char *fmt, ...)` accepts variable arguments. The format string is positional; subsequent varargs are interpreted according to `%d`, `%s`, etc. specifications. **Why necessary**: When the number of inputs cannot be predetermined at compile time (logging systems, mathematical functions like `max(a, b, c, ...)`). **Rubric**: Keyword example (2m), Default example (2m), Varargs necessity + printf (3m). --- > [!WARNING] > **General KTU Valuation Pitfalls for Parameter Passing** > 1. **Writing "C uses pass-by-reference"** — incorrect. C always passes **values**; passing pointers is still pass-by-value of the pointer. *Loses 2 marks immediately.* > 2. **Confusing `out` and `in out`** in Ada — `out` does **not** copy in, only out. > 3. **Forgetting that Java's object parameters are pass-by-value of the reference** — not pass-by-reference. This is a heavily tested KTU trap. > 4. **Skipping the trace** in 7-mark problems — students who only narrate without showing state transitions typically lose 3–4 marks. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Parameter-Passing Mechanisms** - **Formal vs Actual**: Header names vs call-site expressions. - **Five core semantic mechanisms**: Value, Reference, Result, Value-Result, Name. - **Three syntactic styles**: Positional, Keyword, Default. - **Varargs**: Implemented via count-pointer pair, null-terminated list, or array object. - **C is pass-by-value only** — including pointers (the pointer is copied, not the pointee). - **Java**: Primitives by value; objects by value of the reference (hybrid reference-value). - **C++**: Default is pass-by-value; use `&` for reference, `*` for pointer. - **Ada**: Explicit `in`, `out`, `in out` modes — most rigorous of all mainstream languages. - **Fortran**: Historically always pass-by-reference; modern Fortran supports `VALUE` attribute. - **Aliasing occurs only in pass-by-reference**, not in pass-by-value-result. - **Thunks** are the implementation artifacts of pass-by-name. - **Reference-value** is the modern safe hybrid — used in C++, Rust, Java, Kotlin, Swift. - **Cost**: Value $\rightarrow O(S)$, Reference $\rightarrow O(W)$ where $W$ is machine word size. - **Default parameters** are evaluated at the call site, not at declaration. - **Keyword + Default** together enable clean, backward-compatible API design (Python, Kotlin). - **Closures** in functional languages capture variables by reference-value (immutable binding). - **Trace tables** (state diagrams showing `actual` and `formal` columns) are the **mandatory** technique for any 7+ mark parameter-passing problem in KTU exams. - **Examiner mantra**: *"Show me the trace, then I'll give you the marks."*

topics.title: Procedure Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Procedure Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procedure Environment** is the *logical and runtime data structure* (typically implemented as an **activation record / stack frame**) that the runtime system associates with every active invocation of a procedure/subprogram. It encapsulates the **memory locations, bindings, control links, access links, and bookkeeping information** required to correctly execute and later return from that procedure invocation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with Scott's *Programming Language Pragmatics* and Sebesta's *Concepts of Programming Languages*), a *procedure environment* binds the following seven logical elements:

1. The **control state** – *where to return* after the procedure finishes.
2. The **access / static link** – *where to look* for non-local names.
3. The **dynamic link** – *who called me* (caller's frame on stack).
4. The **return value location** – *where to store* the function's result.
5. The **parameters** – the actual arguments passed in.
6. The **local variables / temporaries** – storage created on entry.
7. The **saved machine state** – register values to restore on return.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Cubicle" Model

Imagine a large corporate office where each employee (a *procedure call*) is assigned a **movable cubicle (activation record)** the moment they start a task:

- The cubicle has a **nameplate** saying *"Return to Desk #B12"* (control link).
- It has a **phone directory** pointing to the parent department (access link / static link).
- It contains a **task list** (local variables) unique to that task.
- When the task ends, the cubicle is **folded up and removed**; the employee walks back to their original desk.

| Cubicle Element | Programming Equivalent | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| Nameplate with callback room | Return address | Call duration |
| Department directory | Static / Access link | Lexical scope |
| Caller's cubicle pointer | Dynamic link | Call chain |
| Task list & drawer | Local variables + temporaries | Procedure execution |
| Report envelope | Return value slot | Until consumed by caller |

> [!NOTE]
> The cubicle analogy captures the **stack discipline** of procedure environments: the most recently entered procedure is the first one to finish, exactly like Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stacking.

---

## 1.3 Why Procedure Environments Matter in Language Design

| Design Decision | Impact on Environment |
|---|---|
| Recursion allowed? | Yes → environment MUST be stack-dynamic (cannot reuse one static slot) |
| Nested procedures (Pascal, Ada, Python) | Yes → static link / display required |
| First-class functions (closures) | Yes → environment may be **heap-allocated** and outlive call |
| Tail-call optimization | Yes → caller's frame can be reused for the callee |
| Co-routines / continuations | Yes → control link becomes tree-structured, not stack |

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activation** – the execution of a procedure after its formal parameters have been evaluated and bound.
> **Activation Record (AR)** – the contiguous block of memory holding the environment of one activation.
> **Activation Tree** – the dynamic tree whose nodes are activations and whose edges represent *"called-by"* relations.
> **Control Stack** – the runtime stack of currently live activation records, growing with each call and shrinking with each return.
> **Calling Sequence** – the code executed at the call site that creates the AR; **Return Sequence** – the symmetric epilogue.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Logical Layers of a Procedure Environment

Every active procedure has its environment logically divided into the following layers. Understanding which layer is *static* (allocated at compile time) versus *stack-dynamic* (allocated on call) versus *heap-dynamic* (allocated by `new`/`malloc`) is the **#1 high-yield concept** for KTU exams.

| # | Component | Typical Location | Set Up By | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Return Address** | Caller's AR (or dedicated slot) | Call instruction | Until return |
| 2 | **Dynamic Link** (control link) | This AR | Calling sequence | This activation |
| 3 | **Static / Access Link** | This AR | Calling sequence (in static-scope langs) | This activation |
| 4 | **Parameters / Arguments** | This AR (or registers) | Caller, before call | This activation |
| 5 | **Saved Registers / Machine State** | This AR | Calling sequence | This activation |
| 6 | **Local Variables & Temporaries** | This AR | Prologue of callee | This activation |
| 7 | **Return Value** | Callee's AR or caller-reserved slot | Return sequence | Until consumed |

> [!NOTE]
> In **pure static allocation** (e.g., FORTRAN 77, no recursion), the entire AR exists once for the whole program. In **stack-dynamic** languages (C, Java, Pascal), an AR is pushed on every call and popped on every return. In **heap-dynamic / closure** languages (Python, Scheme, ML), some environment components may escape to the heap.

---

## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms — How Environments Receive Values

The *environment* of a procedure is parameterised by how formal parameters are bound to actuals. Six classical mechanisms appear in KTU textbooks (Sebesta Ch. 9):

| # | Mechanism | Binding Time | Formal Is Alias Of | Allows Modification? | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pass-by-Value** | Call time | A *copy* in callee's AR | No (on the original) | Cheap |
| 2 | **Pass-by-Reference** | Call time | The caller's actual variable | Yes (writes through) | Indirect access |
| 3 | **Pass-by-Value-Result** | Call time, copy-back on return | Local copy → written back | Yes (on local copy) | Two copies |
| 4 | **Pass-by-Name** | Each use | Re-evaluated textually each access | Yes (if LHS) | Expensive (thunk) |
| 5 | **Pass-by-Result** | Call time | Uninitialised; written back on return | Yes (on caller's var) | One copy-out |
| 6 | **Pass-by-Need (lazy)** | First use | Thunk memoised | Yes | Laziness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **By-Reference vs By-Value-Result** – they look identical for simple cases, but if the actual is aliased to a global that the callee also accesses, **by-reference** reflects the global change immediately, while **by-value-result** only writes back at the end. This is a classic KTU two-mark question.

---

## 2.3 Scope, Visibility, and Lifetime — The Three Siblings

A procedure environment enforces three distinct notions, and confusing them is the most common exam pitfall:

| Notion | Meaning | Decided By |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | *Where in the source text* is the name visible? | Lexical (static) rules — usually |
| **Visibility** | *Which declaration* of the name is currently active? | Scope rules + current activation |
| **Lifetime** | *How long* does the binding (storage) exist? | Activation lifetime / heap |

The famous equation:

$$\text{Lifetime} \;\supseteq\; \text{Visibility Window} \;\subseteq\; \text{Scope Region}$$

---

## 2.4 Static vs Dynamic Scoping — How the Access Link Is Computed

The **access link (static link)** is the part of the environment that lets a procedure find its lexical ancestors.

* **Static (Lexical) Scoping** – Access link points to the AR of the *statically enclosing* procedure. The compiler can determine the chain length at compile time.
* **Dynamic Scoping** – Access link points to the AR of the *most recent active invocation of an ancestor at runtime*. The chain length is unknown until execution.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU favourite: *"In a language with dynamic scoping, what does a reference to a free variable resolve to?"* — Answer: the most recently entered AR that contains a binding for that name, **searched from the caller outward through the dynamic call chain**, not through the lexical nesting.

---

## 2.5 Storage Allocation Strategies Summary

| Strategy | When Bound | When Allocated | When Deallocated | Example Languages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Compile | Before execution | Program end | FORTRAN globals, `static` C variables |
| **Stack-dynamic** | On call | On procedure entry | On procedure exit | C locals, Java locals |
| **Explicit heap (new/malloc)** | `new` | `new` | `delete/free` or GC | C `malloc`, Java `new` |
| **Implicit heap (closures)** | Closure capture | Closure creation | When unreachable (GC) | Scheme, Python, ML |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Procedure Environment Metrics

| Metric | Formula / Rule | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **AR size (bytes)** | $S_{AR} = \sum_{i} \vert \text{param}_i \vert + \sum_{j} \vert \text{local}_j \vert + \sum_{k} \vert \text{tempor}_k \vert + \vert \text{control} \vert + \vert \text{access} \vert + \vert \text{saved} \vert + \vert \text{retVal} \vert$ | bytes |
| **Max simultaneous ARs** | $\leq \text{depth of activation tree at deepest point}$ | count |
| **Recursion depth bound** | $\text{Depth}_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{StackSize} - \text{Reserved}}{S_{AR}} \right\rfloor$ | calls |
| **Stack pointer update** | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - S_{AR}$ | bytes (grows down) |
| **Frame pointer delta** | $FP_{callee} = SP_{old} + \text{offset}$ | bytes |
| **Display entry update** | $display[d] \leftarrow FP_{callee}$ | pointer slot |
| **Static-link depth** | depth = lexical nesting level difference | int |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert x \vert$ (rendered as `\vert`) instead of the keyboard pipe so the table cells remain unbroken.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Procedure Environments

* **Compilers** — Codegen for any language with procedures must emit calling/return sequences that manipulate the AR.
* **Debuggers** — Stack walkers reconstruct the call chain by following dynamic links.
* **Operating Systems** — Each thread has its own control stack built of procedure environments.
* **Garbage Collectors** — Distinguish stack roots (live locals) from heap roots when tracing closures.
* **Security/Sandboxing** — Frame integrity can be enforced (e.g., Windows x86 Shadow Stacks, Intel CET).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing the Maximum Recursion Depth

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A C program runs on a system with a thread stack of **1 MiB**. Each call to function `recurse` consumes **128 bytes** of activation record (parameters + locals + saved PC + saved FP). The OS reserves **16 KiB** for signal frames. What is the maximum safe recursion depth before stack overflow?

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1.** Convert everything to the same unit (bytes):
$$\text{Stack} = 1~\text{MiB} = 2^{20}~\text{bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576~\text{bytes}$$

**Step 2.** Convert the reservation:
$$\text{Reserved} = 16~\text{KiB} = 16 \times 2^{10} = 16{,}384~\text{bytes}$$

**Step 3.** Compute available room for ARs:
$$\text{Avail} = \text{Stack} - \text{Reserved} = 1{,}048{,}576 - 16{,}384 = 1{,}032{,}192~\text{bytes}$$

**Step 4.** Divide by AR size:
$$\text{Depth}_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{Avail}}{S_{AR}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1{,}032{,}192}{128} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate the quotient:
$$\dfrac{1{,}032{,}192}{128} = 8{,}064.0$$

**Step 6.** Apply the floor:
$$\boxed{\text{Depth}_{max} = 8{,}064~\text{recursive~calls}}$$

**Interpretation:** The 8,064th nested call will overflow; the safe limit is 8,063.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Static Link Chain Computation (Nested Procedures, Pascal-style)

> **Problem:** Given the Pascal-like nesting below, the call to `inner()` happens from `outer(2)`. Draw the activation stack and indicate the **static link** for each AR.

```
PROGRAM P;
  PROCEDURE A;
    PROCEDURE B;
      PROCEDURE inner;
        ...
      BEGIN inner END;
    PROCEDURE outer(n: INTEGER);
      BEGIN B END;
  BEGIN A END.
```

Lexical nesting levels: `P = 0`, `A = 1`, `B = 2`, `outer = 2` (sibling of B), `inner = 3`.

**Activation trace when `outer(2)` calls `inner`:**

| # | Step | Active Stack (top is current) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `P` calls `A` | `[P, A]` |
| 2 | `A` calls `outer(2)` | `[P, A, outer(2)]` |
| 3 | `outer(2)` calls `B` | `[P, A, outer(2), B]` |
| 4 | `B` calls `inner` | `[P, A, outer(2), B, inner]` |

**Static link of each AR** (pointing to the AR of the *lexical parent*):

| AR | Lexical Parent | Static Link Points To |
|---|---|---|
| `P` | — | `nil` |
| `A` | `P` | `P` |
| `outer(2)` | `A` | `A` |
| `B` | `A` | `A` |
| `inner` | `B` | `B` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **dynamic chain** at step 4 is `P → A → outer(2) → B → inner`, but the **static chain** used for name resolution is `inner → B → A → P`. This distinction is the heart of static scoping.

**Cost to access a variable at lexical depth `d` from current level `c`:**

$$\text{Hops} = c - d$$

**Example:** To access a name declared in `A` (level 1) from inside `inner` (level 3):
$$\text{Hops} = 3 - 1 = 2~\text{static-link traversals}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Pass-by-Reference vs Pass-by-Value-Result (Aliased Global)

> **Problem:** Consider:
> ```
> int g = 10;
> void P(int x, int y) {
>     x = x + 5;   // line A
>     g = g + 1;   // line B
>     y = y + g;   // line C
> }
> main() { P(g, g); }
> ```
> Predict the final value of `g` under (i) pass-by-reference, (ii) pass-by-value-result.

### (i) Pass-by-Reference

| Step | Action | `g` global | `x` (alias of g) | `y` (alias of g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Entry | — | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Line A | `x = x + 5` → `g = 15` | **15** | 15 | 15 |
| Line B | `g = g + 1` → `g = 16` | **16** | 16 | 16 |
| Line C | `y = y + g` → `g = 32` | **32** | 32 | 32 |

$$\boxed{g = 32~\text{(by-reference)}}$$

### (ii) Pass-by-Value-Result

| Step | Action | `g` global | `x` (local copy) | `y` (local copy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Entry | Copy `g=10` into `x` and `y` | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Line A | `x = 10 + 5 = 15` | 10 | 15 | 10 |
| Line B | `g = 10 + 1 = 11` | **11** | 15 | 10 |
| Line C | `y = 10 + 11 = 21` | 11 | 15 | 21 |
| Return | Copy `y=21` back to `g`; copy `x=15` back to `g` (last wins) | **15** | — | — |

$$\boxed{g = 15~\text{(by-value-result)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same code, the same parameter values, but **different final answers** because the aliasing is handled differently. KTU loves this comparison.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Calling Sequence Micro-cost Analysis
