A typical **calling sequence** in a stack-based architecture executes `k` instructions. Each instruction costs `1 cycle`. The **prologue** of the callee adds `p` cycles, the **epilogue** adds `e` cycles. For `N` nested calls (e.g., recursion), derive the total time.

**Step 1.** Cost per call (caller + callee prologue):
$$T_{\text{call}} = k + p$$

**Step 2.** Cost per return (callee epilogue + caller post-call):
$$T_{\text{ret}} = e + k$$

**Step 3.** Total cost for `N` calls + `N` returns:
$$T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_{\text{call}} + N \cdot T_{\text{ret}} = N(k + p + e + k) = N(2k + p + e)$$

**Step 4.** Amortised per nesting level:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{T_{\text{total}}}{N} = 2k + p + e~\text{cycles per level}}$$

**Numerical check:** If `k = 4`, `p = 6`, `e = 5`, `N = 1000`:
$$T_{\text{total}} = 1000 \cdot (8 + 6 + 5) = 19{,}000~\text{cycles}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for "Which Environment Mechanism?"

| Language Feature | Required Environment Mechanism | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Simple recursion | Stack-dynamic AR | Each call needs its own locals |
| Mutual recursion (A↔B) | Stack-dynamic AR | Same as above; forward decls |
| Nested procedures + static scope | Static link (or Display) | Lexical parent lookup |
| Closures over local variables | Heap-allocated environment | Environment outlives call |
| First-class functions | Tagged closure record | Needs both code + captured env |
| Coroutines / continuations | Tree of continuations | LIFO discipline breaks |
| Tail calls | Caller frame reuse | Avoid stack growth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activation Record (Stack Frame) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef field fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A237E,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef retval fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef control fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92,stroke-width:1px;

    SP["SP (Stack Pointer)"]:::control
    ARtop["Activation Record Top"]:::control

    RV["Return Value (slot for caller)"]:::retval
    RET["Return Address"]:::field
    DYN["Dynamic Link (caller FP)"]:::field
    STA["Static / Access Link"]:::field
    PAR["Parameters (incoming args)"]:::field
    SAV["Saved Registers / Machine State"]:::field
    LOC["Local Variables + Temporaries"]:::field
    FP["FP (Frame Pointer)"]:::control

    SP --> ARtop
    ARtop --> RV
    RV --> RET
    RET --> DYN
    DYN --> STA
    STA --> PAR
    PAR --> SAV
    SAV --> LOC
    LOC --> FP

    note1["High addresses →"]
    note2["Low addresses (stack grows down) →"]:::control

    ARtop -.- note1
    FP -.- note2
```

**Reading order:** The frame is laid out with the **return address** at the top (low address on a downward-growing stack) and the **local variables** at the bottom (high address). The frame pointer `FP` traditionally points at the base of the parameters, allowing fixed offsets for locals.

---

## 4.2 Calling Sequence — Call / Execute / Return Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Caller as Caller AR
    participant Stack as Runtime Stack
    participant Callee as Callee AR

    Caller->>Caller: 1. Evaluate actuals into temp slots
    Caller->>Stack: 2. SP := SP - S_AR
    Caller->>Callee: 3. Push return address
    Caller->>Callee: 4. Push dynamic link (old FP)
    Caller->>Callee: 5. Set static link to lexical parent's FP
    Caller->>Callee: 6. Copy parameters into callee's param slots
    Caller->>Callee: 7. Save caller-saved registers
    Caller->>Callee: 8. Transfer control to callee entry
    Callee->>Callee: 9. Reserve locals + temporaries
    Callee->>Callee: 10. Execute procedure body
    Callee->>Stack: 11. Place return value in agreed slot
    Callee->>Caller: 12. Restore saved registers
    Callee->>Caller: 13. SP := FP + offset (pop frame)
    Callee->>Caller: 14. Jump to return address
    Caller->>Caller: 15. Use return value, continue execution
```

> [!NOTE]
> Steps **1–8** form the **calling sequence**; **9–10** are the procedure body; **11–14** are the **return sequence**. Step **15** resumes the caller. The exact split between caller and callee code is language-dependent (e.g., C passes arguments in registers, Pascal pushes them).

---

## 4.3 Activation Tree — Dynamic Call Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef nodeA fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000;
    classDef nodeB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000;
    classDef nodeC fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000;

    mainNode["main"]:::nodeA
    procA["procedure A (call 1)"]:::nodeB
    procB["procedure A (call 2 - recursive)"]:::nodeB
    procC["procedure A (call 3 - recursive)"]:::nodeC
    leafX["leaf X"]:::nodeC
    leafY["leaf Y"]:::nodeC
    leafZ["leaf Z"]:::nodeC

    mainNode --> procA
    mainNode --> procB
    mainNode --> procC
    procB --> leafX
    procB --> leafY
    procC --> leafZ
```

**Tree ↔ Stack correspondence:**
* A **depth-first traversal** of the activation tree from any node mirrors the live portion of the control stack above that node's AR.
* The **leftmost live path** of the tree = top of stack.
* Nodes are added when entered and removed when returned from.

---

## 4.4 Display Mechanism — O(1) Static-Scope Lookups

When procedure nesting is deep, walking static links is `O(d)`. A **display** is an array indexed by lexical depth that holds the FP of the most recent activation at each level, giving `O(1)` access:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef arr fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000;
    classDef slot fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000;
    classDef ar fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000;

    D["Display Array (per activation)"]:::arr

    D --> D0["[0] FP of level-0 proc (e.g., main)"]:::slot
    D --> D1["[1] FP of level-1 proc"]:::slot
    D --> D2["[2] FP of level-2 proc"]:::slot
    D --> D3["[3] FP of level-3 proc"]:::slot

    D1 --> AR1["AR of Outer"]:::ar
    D2 --> AR2["AR of Inner"]:::ar
    D3 --> AR3["AR of Deepest"]:::ar
```

**Update rule on entering procedure at depth `d`:**
$$\text{Display}[d] \leftarrow FP_{new}; \quad \text{(old Display}[d] \text{ is saved in the AR for restoration on return)}$$

**Lookup rule for a variable declared at depth `k` from current depth `c`:**
$$\text{Address} = \text{Display}[k] + \text{offset}_{\text{variable}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Displays are constant time but waste a few words per AR; static-link chains are slower but simpler. Most modern compilers (e.g., LLVM) use **static links** with the optimiser converting common patterns into direct offsets.

---

## 4.5 Closures — Capturing the Environment on the Heap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef heap fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000;
    classDef stack fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000;

    closure["Closure Object (Heap)"]:::heap
    codePtr["Code Pointer → function machine code"]:::heap
    envPtr["Environment Pointer → captured AR copy"]:::heap

    capturedAR["Captured Environment (Heap-promoted AR)"]:::heap
    var1["x = 10 (captured local)"]:::heap
    var2["y = 20 (captured local)"]:::heap

    liveAR["Currently Live AR (Stack)"]:::stack
    callerCode["Caller still running"]:::stack

    closure --> codePtr
    closure --> envPtr
    envPtr --> capturedAR
    capturedAR --> var1
    capturedAR --> var2

    closure -.invoked from.-> callerCode
    callerCode -.references.-> liveAR
```

> [!NOTE]
> When a function escapes its lexical scope (e.g., returned as a value), the captured portion of the AR is **copied or promoted to the heap**, and the closure holds a pointer to it. The stack AR may then be safely destroyed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q.** Define **activation record**. List any five fields typically stored in it.

**Model Answer (key points expected):**
1. Activation record = contiguous memory block storing the runtime environment of *one* active invocation of a procedure.
2. Five key fields:
   * **Return address** – instruction to resume in the caller.
   * **Dynamic link** – pointer to caller's AR.
   * **Static link** – pointer to lexically enclosing AR.
   * **Parameters** – values or references passed in.
   * **Local variables / temporaries** – storage for procedure-private data.
3. Additional field often present: **saved machine registers**.

*(Mark split: 1 for definition, 2 for list — examiners expect short, precise bullets.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q.** Differentiate between **pass-by-reference** and **pass-by-value-result**. Give one scenario where they produce different results.

**Model Answer:**
* **By-reference:** formal is an *alias*; writes through the formal immediately update the caller's actual.
* **By-value-result:** formal is a *local copy*; updates propagate back **only at procedure return** via copy-out.
* **Divergence scenario:** actual is aliased to a global that the procedure also modifies. Example: `P(g, g)` where `g` is global — under by-reference every change is seen instantly, under by-value-result the global changes only on return.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module-3 questions for PECST758 follow the standard **"7 + 7"** split: each long-answer has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ❓ Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Q(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of an activation record** in a language supporting recursion, static scoping, and stack-dynamic allocation.

**Q(b) [7 Marks]** Write the **calling sequence and return sequence** for a procedure `P` with three parameters and two local variables, explaining each step.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Activation Record Structure — 7 marks**

```
         +-----------------------+  ← High address (bottom of stack)
         | Local variables       |
         | Temporaries           |
         +-----------------------+
         | Saved machine state   |
         +-----------------------+
         | Parameter 3           |
         | Parameter 2           |
         | Parameter 1           |
         +-----------------------+
   FP →  | Static / Access link  |
         | Dynamic link          |
         | Return address        |
         +-----------------------+  ← Low address (top of stack)
         | Return value slot     |   (owned by caller, freed after use)
```

**Explanation (1 mark per major point, 7 total):**
1. AR is a contiguous block created on every entry to `P`.
2. **Return address** (1 mark) – stored by call instruction; used by `RET`.
3. **Dynamic link** (1 mark) – old `FP` saved so callee can locate caller's AR on return.
4. **Static link** (1 mark) – points to the AR of the lexically enclosing procedure; required to resolve free variables under static scoping.
5. **Parameters** (1 mark) – copy of arguments pushed by the caller before the call.
6. **Local variables & temporaries** (1 mark) – allocated in the callee's prologue; deallocated on return.
7. **Saved registers / machine state** (1 mark) – any caller-saved registers the callee clobbers must be preserved.

**(b) Calling & Return Sequence — 7 marks**

**Calling Sequence (4 marks):**
1. Evaluate actuals in the caller's AR (or registers).
2. Push parameters onto stack (right-to-left typically).
3. Push return address (`PC + 1`).
4. Push old `FP` (dynamic link).
5. Set static link to the FP of `P`'s lexical parent.
6. Set new `FP := SP`.
7. Transfer control to `P`'s entry point.

**Return Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Place return value in agreed slot (caller's AR or a register).
2. Restore saved registers from AR.
3. `SP := FP + offset` (pop the callee's AR).
4. `FP := saved dynamic link`.
5. Jump to saved return address.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that the **static link** is computed at *call time* (not return time) and must be recomputed for every invocation, even if the same procedure is called from different lexical sites. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting this nuance.

---

### ❓ Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **static scoping** and **dynamic scoping** with suitable code examples. Show how the **access chain (static link)** resolves non-local references in each case.

**Q(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Display mechanism** for implementing static scoping. How does it achieve O(1) variable access? What are its trade-offs?

#### Model Solution

**(a) Static vs Dynamic Scoping — 7 marks**

**Static (Lexical) Scoping (3.5 marks):**

```pascal
PROGRAM Demo;
VAR x : INTEGER;
  PROCEDURE P;
  BEGIN
    PRINT(x);  (* Which x? *)
  END;
  PROCEDURE Q;
  VAR x : INTEGER;
  BEGIN
    x := 100;
    P;          (* P is called from inside Q *)
  END;
BEGIN
  x := 10;
  Q;
END.
```

Output under **static** scope = **10** (because `P` is lexically inside `Demo`, so its free `x` resolves to the global `x`, *not* `Q`'s local `x`, even though `Q` is the active caller).

**Dynamic Scoping (3.5 marks):**

Same code under **dynamic** scope = **100** (the most recent active binding of `x` at runtime — i.e., `Q`'s local `x`).

**Resolution mechanism:**
* **Static:** compiler sets static link at call time; the access chain follows lexical parent pointers, ignoring the call chain.
* **Dynamic:** access chain follows the *dynamic* call chain (caller → caller → …) until a binding is found.

**(b) Display Mechanism — 7 marks**

**Idea (2 marks):** Maintain a global array `display[d]` per activation, where `display[d]` holds the frame pointer of the most recent active procedure at lexical depth `d`.

**On Procedure Entry at Depth `d` (2 marks):**
1. Save current `display[d]` in the new AR's "old display" slot.
2. Set `display[d] := FP_of_new_AR`.

**On Procedure Exit at Depth `d` (1 mark):**
1. Restore `display[d]` from the saved slot.

**On Variable Access from Depth `c` to Variable at Depth `k` (1 mark):**
* Address = `display[k]` + `offset_of_variable` — **O(1) lookup** instead of `O(c − k)` chain walks.

**Trade-offs (1 mark):**
* ✅ Constant-time access.
* ✅ No walking the static-link chain.
* ❌ Requires saving/restoring one slot per call.
* ❌ Does not compose well with closures / dynamic extent environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **display** with the **dynamic chain**. The display indexes by *lexical* depth; the dynamic chain is the runtime call stack. Mixing them up costs full marks on part (b).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Conflating "static link" and "dynamic link":** The static link points to the *lexical parent*, the dynamic link to the *caller*. Examiners routinely give 0 marks if you use them interchangeably.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting that recursion requires stack-dynamic allocation:** A single static slot per procedure name cannot support recursion. State this explicitly when justifying stack ARs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Drawing activation records with the "wrong" growth direction:** Decide once whether the stack grows **down** (most C systems) or **up** and stay consistent. Mismatched arrows in diagrams lose 1–2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Skipping the cost analysis:** Even in theory questions, an examiner expects a single line quantifying the **O(1) vs O(d)** cost of display vs static-link access. Mention it even if not asked.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Treating "heap-allocated AR" as "heap-allocated variable":** They are different! Closures promote *the environment* (the AR's captured part) to the heap, while the locals still live on the stack during execution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Procedure environment = activation record** = runtime bookkeeping for one call.
- ✅ AR components: *return address, dynamic link, static link, parameters, saved state, locals, return value*.
- ✅ **Recursion ⇒ stack-dynamic ARs.** Without stack ARs, recursion is impossible.
- ✅ **Calling sequence** sets up the AR; **return sequence** tears it down and restores the caller.
- ✅ **Static link** = pointer to *lexical* parent; **dynamic link** = pointer to *caller*.
- ✅ **Display** gives O(1) name resolution in deeply nested static-scope languages.
- ✅ **By-reference vs by-value-result** diverge when actuals are aliased to globals.
- ✅ **Closures** may promote the captured part of the AR to the heap.
- ✅ **Max recursion depth** ≈ `(StackSize − Reserved) / AR_size`.
- ✅ The **activation tree** mirrors live stack order under depth-first traversal.
- ✅ Static-scope name resolution: walk static links → `O(d)`; use display → `O(1)`.
- ✅ Dynamic-scope name resolution: walk the dynamic chain (caller chain) until found.
- ✅ KTU loves comparisons: *static vs dynamic scope*, *value vs reference vs value-result*, *stack vs heap AR*, *static-link vs display*.
- ✅ Always quantify complexity or cost in at least one line per long answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activations and Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activations and Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Activations?

An **activation** is an execution of a procedure (function, subroutine, or method). Each time a procedure is called, a new activation begins, and when the procedure returns, that activation ends. The data area allocated on the run-time stack for executing that procedure is called an **Activation Record (AR)** or **Stack Frame**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST758):**
> *An activation record is the consecutive block of memory that contains the data necessary for a single execution of a procedure. It includes the procedure's parameters, local variables, temporaries, and book-keeping information such as the return address.*

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Imagine you are working in a large office. Each time your boss (the **caller**) gives you a new task (calls a procedure), you:
1. Pull out a fresh **folder** from the filing cabinet (allocate an **Activation Record** on the stack).
2. Write down the **task details** (parameters), your **working notes** (local variables), and a **return address** pointing back to your boss's desk.
3. Complete the task, then file the folder away (**deallocate** the AR) and report back to your boss.

Multiple nested tasks = a **stack** of folders. The task currently on top is the one actively running. This is precisely how **control stacks** work during program execution.

## 1.2 What is Allocation?

**Allocation** is the mechanism by which memory is reserved (and later released) for variables, objects, and data structures during program execution. There are three principal strategies:

| Strategy | Timing | Location | Flexibility |
|----------|--------|----------|-------------|
| **Static Allocation** | Compile-time | Data segment | ❌ Low (fixed size) |
| **Stack Allocation** | Run-time (LIFO) | Stack segment | ⚠️ Medium |
| **Heap Allocation** | Run-time (arbitrary) | Heap segment | ✅ High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every Part B question in Module 3 asks you to draw activation records or compare allocation strategies. Master this distinction first.

## 1.3 Lifetime vs Scope — A Critical Distinction

- **Scope** of a name: A *region of the program text* where the name is visible (a **compile-time / lexical** concept).
- **Lifetime** of a variable (or *object*): The *interval of run-time execution* during which storage exists (a **run-time** concept).

A variable's lifetime may extend beyond its scope (e.g., a local variable captured by a closure), and a variable may be in scope but refer to a new activation's storage on each call.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Activation Record

A typical activation record contains the following fields (top-to-bottom or bottom-to-top, depending on convention):

```
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│   Actual Parameters         │  (passed to this procedure)
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Return Address            │  (where to resume in caller)
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Saved Machine State       │  (registers, condition codes)
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Control Link (Dynamic)    │  → points to caller's AR
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Access Link (Static)      │  → points to enclosing scope's AR
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Local Variables           │
├─────────────────────────────┤
│   Temporaries / Spill Area  │
└─────────────────────────────┘
```

### Operational Steps — "The Why and How"

1. **Call Sequence (Caller)**: Evaluates actual parameters, stores them where callee can find, saves return address, jumps to callee.
2. **Prologue (Callee)**: Allocates space for AR, saves machine registers, sets up new AR pointer.
3. **Body Execution**: Runs the procedure's statements using its AR.
4. **Epilogue (Callee)**: Places return value, restores saved state, deallocates AR.
5. **Return Sequence (Caller)**: Resumes after the call site, uses the return value.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The division of work between **caller** and **callee** varies by language. In C, almost all work is done by the **caller** (parameters pushed before call). In some Pascal implementations, callee pushes its own local variables. Examiners love this contrast.

## 2.2 The Three Allocation Strategies — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Static Allocation

- All data objects are bound to **absolute addresses at compile time**.
- No run-time support is needed for allocation — variables are placed in the **data segment**.
- **Constraints:** Size must be known; no recursion; no dynamic structures.
- **Examples:** Global variables in C, `static` local variables, Fortran 77 subroutines.

**Why and How:**
- Compiler emits assembly directives (e.g., `.data`, `.bss`) that reserve fixed bytes.
- Each variable's address is computed once: `base_address + offset`.
- Pros: Fastest possible access; deterministic memory. Cons: Inflexible.

### 2.2.2 Stack Allocation

- ARs are pushed onto a **control stack** as procedures are entered, popped on return.
- Follows **LIFO** discipline, which matches the nested call-return structure perfectly.
- **Enables:** Recursion, multiple simultaneous activations, automatic cleanup.
- **Restriction:** Local variables cannot be retained after the procedure returns (unless escaped to heap).

**Why and How:**
- A register (often called `SP` or `fp`) tracks the top of the stack.
- Each `call` instruction pushes an AR; each `ret` pops one.
- Locals are accessed at **fixed offsets** from the frame pointer — this gives O(1) access despite dynamic allocation.

### 2.2.3 Heap Allocation

- Memory is allocated and freed in **arbitrary order** — does not follow LIFO.
- Required for: dynamic data structures (linked lists, trees), objects whose lifetime is independent of the creating procedure, language features like `new`, `malloc`, dynamic arrays.
- **Managed by:** an allocator (e.g., `malloc`/`free` in C, GC in Java/Python).

**Why and How:**
- Heap is a large pool of free memory carved into variable-sized blocks.
- Allocators track free/used blocks via **free lists, bitmaps, or buddy systems**.
- Fragmentation (internal + external) is the chief challenge.

## 2.3 Activation Tree

The dynamic calls during execution form an **activation tree**:

- **Nodes** = activations.
- **Root** = activation of `main`.
- **Parent → child** edge = "caller calls callee".
- Sibling order = order of calls.
- Depth = nesting of calls.
- Preorder traversal = chronological order of activation beginnings.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following relations are the high-yield formulas KTU examiners expect for full marks in 14-mark questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|---------------|-------|
| AR Memory Address | `AR_i.address = SP + offset` | Stack pointer based |
| Static Address | `addr(x) = base + compile_time_offset` | Known at load time |
| Heap Fragmentation | `Wasted = ∑(block_size − requested_size)` | Internal fragmentation |
| Recursion depth bound | `depth ≤ available_stack_size ÷ AR_size` | Stack overflow occurs above this |
| Activation lifetime | `t_start(call) ≤ t_lifetime < t_end(return)` | Bound to procedure execution |
| Scope vs Lifetime | `scope ⊂ lifetime` (often), not always | Closure breaks this |
| Frame Pointer offset | `local_i = fp − i × word_size` | Negative offsets common |
| Parameter offset | `param_j = fp + (j+1) × word_size` | Positive offsets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Activation records underpin every debugger (call stack backtrace), every exception unwinder (`throw`/`catch` searches the dynamic chain), and every garbage collector (root set = registers + stack frames). Real-time embedded systems use **static allocation exclusively** to guarantee no GC pauses — a critical choice for automotive/aerospace software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Allocation Strategy Selection

The decision of which strategy to use depends on **scope, lifetime, and binding time** of each name. The matrix below is a **KTU high-yield deliverable** for design questions:

| Property | Static | Stack | Heap |
|----------|:------:|:-----:|:----:|
| When is size bound? | Compile-time | Procedure entry | `new` / `malloc` |
| Lifetime ends on? | Program termination | Procedure return | Explicit free / GC |
| Allows recursion? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Access speed | ⚡ Fastest | ⚡ Fast (one indirection from FP) | 🐢 Slower (pointer chase) |
| Risk of fragmentation? | None | None | ✅ High (external + internal) |
| Suitable for globals? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Suitable for dynamic arrays? | ❌ No | ⚠️ Only if size ≤ compile-time bound | ✅ Yes |
| Typical languages | Fortran 77, C `static` | C local vars, Java method vars | C `malloc`, Java `new`, Python objects |

## 3.2 Worked Example — Activation Tree Construction

**Source Code:**

```c
int main() {
    A();
    B();
    return 0;
}

void A() {
    B();
    C();
}

void B() {
    // terminal
}

void C() {
    A();
}
```

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** `main` calls `A`. Push AR for `main_A1`.
**Step 2:** `A` calls `B`. Push AR for `A_B1`. `B` returns, pop.
**Step 3:** `A` calls `C`. Push AR for `A_C1`.
**Step 4:** `C` calls `A` (recursive call). Push AR for `C_A2`.
**Step 5:** `A` calls `B`. Push AR for `A_B2`. Returns, pop.
**Step 6:** `A` calls `C`. Push AR for `A_C2`.
**Step 7:** `C` calls `A` again. Push AR for `C_A3` — at this point recursion deepens.
**Step 8:** Returns unwind in LIFO order.

**Activation Tree:**

```
        main
         |
         A
        / \
       B   C
           |
           A
          / \
         B   C
             |
             A
```

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Calling Sequence Address Computation

Given a stack growing **downward** (typical), with each AR size = 16 bytes, base `SP` = 1000:

**Step 1:** Caller is in `main`, current SP = 1000.
**Step 2:** Caller evaluates actual parameters, places them at addresses starting from SP - 4 (so first param at 996).
**Step 3:** Caller pushes return address at SP - 8 (= 992).
**Step 4:** Caller executes `CALL A`. SP is decremented by 16 to 984.
**Step 5:** In A's prologue, Frame Pointer (FP) is set to 984.

$$
\text{FP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - \text{sizeof}(\text{AR})
$$

**Step 6:** A's locals are accessed at offsets `-4, -8, -12` from FP.
**Step 7:** A's parameters (set by caller) at offsets `+4, +8` from FP.
**Step 8:** On `RET`, SP = FP + 4 (past saved FP), then FP is restored, PC jumps to saved return address.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Stack vs Heap for Linked List

**Problem:** Build a singly linked list of `n` nodes.

**Decision Walkthrough:**

1. Each node has unknown lifetime — must outlive the function that creates it ⇒ **Heap**.
2. The *head pointer* itself is local to `main` and dies at program exit ⇒ **Stack** is acceptable, but `static` would also work.
3. If list size `n` known at compile time, all nodes could be a **static array** (but wasteful).

**Conclusion:** `struct Node* head = malloc(sizeof(Node));` — pointer on stack, object on heap.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *what* is allocated where. Remember: the **variable/binding** (the pointer) is on stack; the **object it refers to** is on heap. Both have separate lifetimes.

## 3.5 Stack Frame Snapshot for `f(int x, int y)`

```
Higher addresses
─────────────────────────
  Return Address  ← caller resumes here
─────────────────────────
  Old FP           ← saved frame pointer
─────────────────────────
  x (param)        
─────────────────────────
  y (param)
─────────────────────────
  local_a
─────────────────────────
  local_b
─────────────────────────
  temporaries
─────────────────────────  ← FP (Frame Pointer) lives here
Lower addresses (SP)
```

Each variable is accessed as `FP ± constant_offset`, giving constant-time access regardless of how deep the recursion is.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Stack Diagram — Nested Procedure Calls

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph mainAR["Activation Record: main"]
        main_ret["Return Address → _start"]
        main_fp["Saved FP"]
        main_local["Local: int result = 0"]
    end

    subgraph funcA_AR["Activation Record: funcA"]
        A_ret["Return Address → main+offset"]
        A_fp["Saved FP → main"]
        A_param["Param: int n = 5"]
        A_local["Local: int temp = 10"]
    end

    subgraph funcB_AR["Activation Record: funcB"]
        B_ret["Return Address → funcA+offset"]
        B_fp["Saved FP → funcA"]
        B_param["Param: int x = 7"]
        B_local["Local: int sum = 12"]
    end

    mainAR --> funcA_AR
    funcA_AR --> funcB_AR
```

## 4.2 Activation Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n0["main (activation 1)"]
    n1["A (activation 2)"]
    n2["B (activation 3)"]
    n3["C (activation 4)"]
    n4["A (activation 5 - recursive)"]
    n5["B (activation 6)"]
    n6["C (activation 7)"]
    n7["A (activation 8)"]

    n0 --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n1 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n4 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Memory Layout of a Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH ADDRESS (0xFFFFFFFF)"]
        kernel["Kernel Space"]
        stack["STACK SEGMENT — Activation Records grow downward"]
        heap["HEAP SEGMENT — Dynamic allocations grow upward"]
        bss["BSS — Uninitialized globals (static)"]
        data["DATA — Initialized globals (static)"]
        text["TEXT — Executable code (static)"]
    end

    text --> data --> bss --> heap --> stack --> kernel
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flow — Which Allocator Wins?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Variable Declaration"] --> B{"Size known at compile time?"}
    B -- Yes --> C{"Lifetime = entire program?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["STATIC Allocation"]
    C -- No --> E{"Lifetime = single procedure call?"}
    B -- No --> F["HEAP Allocation"]
    E -- Yes --> G["STACK Allocation"]
    E -- No --> F
```

## 4.5 Calling Sequence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CALLER["CALLER (e.g., main)"]
        c1["1. Evaluate actual params"]
        c2["2. Store return address"]
        c3["3. Store control link"]
        c4["4. Execute CALL instruction"]
    end

    subgraph CALLEE["CALLEE (e.g., foo)"]
        f1["1. Push FP, set FP = SP"]
        f2["2. Save callee-saved registers"]
        f3["3. Allocate locals on stack"]
        f4["4. Execute body"]
        f5["5. Store return value"]
        f6["6. Restore registers"]
        f7["7. Pop AR, jump to return address"]
    end

    c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4 --> f1 --> f2 --> f3 --> f4 --> f5 --> f6 --> f7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define activation record. List its typical contents.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An activation record (AR) is a contiguous block of memory on the run-time stack that stores the data required for a single execution of a procedure. Typical contents include:
1. **Actual parameters** (1 mark) — values passed by the caller.
2. **Return address** (0.5 mark) — point in caller to resume after return.
3. **Saved machine state / control link / access link** (1 mark) — bookkeeping.
4. **Local variables, temporaries** (0.5 mark) — procedure's own data.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Distinguish between static and stack allocation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Static | Stack |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Binding time | Compile time | Run-time (procedure entry) |
| Lifetime | Entire program | Procedure activation |
| Recursion | Not supported | Supported |
| Memory region | Data segment | Stack segment |
| Size known at compile time? | Yes | Often no |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for any two distinguishing points.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three different storage allocation strategies used in programming languages. Mention the merits and demerits of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C program. Draw the **activation tree** and show the **contents of the control stack** at the moment when the deepest call is executing. **(7 Marks)**

```c
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}

int main() {
    int result = factorial(3);
    return 0;
}
```

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Storage Allocation Strategies (7 Marks):**

**1. Static Allocation (2 marks)**
- All bindings of names to storage are made at compile time.
- All variables occupy fixed locations in the data segment.
- **Merits:** Fastest access (direct addressing), no run-time overhead, deterministic.
- **Demerits:** No recursion, no dynamic data structures, wastes memory if arrays oversized.

**2. Stack Allocation (2 marks)**
- Each procedure activation pushes an AR onto the control stack; AR is popped on return.
- Storage for locals is bound fresh on each call → enables recursion.
- **Merits:** Automatic cleanup, supports recursion, efficient LIFO discipline.
- **Demerits:** Locals lost after return (cannot outlive procedure), risk of stack overflow with deep recursion.

**3. Heap Allocation (3 marks)**
- Memory blocks allocated and freed in arbitrary order from a free-list-managed pool.
- Required for data whose lifetime is independent of any single procedure activation.
- **Merits:** Maximum flexibility, supports all dynamic structures (lists, trees, graphs).
- **Demerits:** Slower allocation, fragmentation (internal + external), needs explicit free or GC.

---

**(b) Activation Tree and Control Stack for `factorial(3)` (7 Marks):**

**Activation Tree (3 marks):**
```
main
  └── factorial(3)
        └── factorial(2)
              └── factorial(1)   ← deepest call
```

**Control Stack at deepest moment (4 marks):**

Reading top (deepest) to bottom:
1. **AR for `factorial(1)`** — n=1, return addr → factorial(2)+offset
2. **AR for `factorial(2)`** — n=2, return addr → factorial(3)+offset
3. **AR for `factorial(3)`** — n=3, return addr → main+offset
4. **AR for `main`** — local `result`, return addr → _start

Each AR contains: parameter `n`, dynamic link to caller, return address, local temp for multiplication. The stack grows downward; each push decrements SP by sizeof(AR).

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What is an activation record? Draw and explain its typical organization. Explain the difference between the **control link (dynamic link)** and the **access link (static link)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A C program uses three local arrays of sizes 10 KB, 20 KB, and 30 KB within a deeply recursive function. Discuss whether stack allocation is appropriate. If not, suggest a better strategy and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Activation Record Organization (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An activation record is a contiguous block of memory holding the data required to execute a single procedure activation.

**Typical Organization (3 marks):**
```
┌─────────────────────────┐
│ Return value (optional) │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Actual Parameters       │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Saved Frame Pointer     │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Control Link (dynamic)  │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Access Link (static)    │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Local Variables         │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ Temporaries             │
└─────────────────────────┘
```

**Control Link vs Access Link (3 marks):**
- **Control Link (Dynamic Link):** Points to the activation record of the **caller**. It enables the callee to restore the caller's state on return. Reflects the *dynamic* call chain — changes at every call. (1.5 marks)
- **Access Link (Static Link):** Points to the activation record of the **enclosing lexical scope**. Used to access non-local variables in nested procedures. Reflects the *static* program structure — determined at compile time. (1.5 marks)

---

**(b) Stack Allocation Suitability for Large Local Arrays in Recursion (7 Marks):**

**Analysis (4 marks):**
- Each recursive activation allocates 10 + 20 + 30 = **60 KB** on the stack.
- Default process stack size on most OS = **1 MB to 8 MB**.
- Maximum safe recursion depth ≈ 1 MB ÷ 60 KB ≈ **17 calls** before stack overflow.
- Therefore, **stack allocation is inappropriate** for this scenario.

**Better Strategy (3 marks):**
- Allocate the arrays on the **heap** using `malloc` (in C) or `new` (in C++).
- Pass pointers through recursive calls — only **pointer-sized data** (~8 bytes) lives on the stack per call.
- This dramatically reduces stack usage: 60 KB per activation becomes ~8 bytes per activation.
- Alternative: declare arrays as `static` inside the function — but this breaks reentrancy for thread safety. Heap is the best general-purpose solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **static link** with **dynamic link** — examiners explicitly test this (3-mark question every year).
> 2. Forgetting that **parameters** live in the *caller's* AR or in a fixed caller–callee agreement — not in the callee's locals area.
> 3. Drawing activation trees with **wrong chronological order** — remember, it's **preorder traversal** of calls, not postorder.
> 4. For mixed-stack/heap questions, students forget to mention **fragmentation** as the chief drawback of heap allocation.
> 5. Saying "stack is faster than heap" without justification — the reason is **cache locality + no pointer indirection**, not magic.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Activation Record (AR)** = stack frame; holds parameters, return addr, saved FP, locals, temporaries, control & access links.
- **Control Stack** = run-time data structure containing all currently active ARs in LIFO order.
- **Activation Tree** = dynamic call hierarchy; root = `main`; preorder = chronological order.
- **Three Allocation Strategies:**
  - **Static** → data segment, compile-time binding, no recursion.
  - **Stack** → automatic, LIFO, enables recursion, dies on return.
  - **Heap** → arbitrary order, lifetime independent of procedure, needs allocator/GC.
- **Control Link (dynamic)** → points to **caller's** AR.
- **Access Link (static)** → points to **enclosing scope's** AR for non-local access.
- **Scope** is a **lexical/compile-time** property; **Lifetime** is a **run-time** property.
- **Calling sequence** is split between caller (param setup, return addr save) and callee (prologue + body + epilogue).
- **Fragmentation** (internal + external) is the chief cost of heap allocation.
- **Real-world rule:** default to stack for locals, heap for dynamic structures, static only for true constants or required globals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Memory Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Memory Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Memory Management (DMM)** is the mechanism by which a programming language runtime system allocates, manages, and deallocates memory to a program at **runtime** (execution time) from a pool of memory known as the **heap**, as opposed to static memory allocation which occurs on the **stack** at compile time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST758 Module 3):** Dynamic Memory Management encompasses the strategies, algorithms, and language constructs (such as `malloc`/`free` in C, `new`/`delete` in C++, and automatic **Garbage Collection** in Java/Python) used to govern the lifecycle of heap-allocated memory objects during program execution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **co-working office space** rented to multiple freelance companies:

1. **Static Memory = Assigned Office Cubicles:** Each employee (variable) gets a fixed desk at the start of the day. If they don't use it, the desk still exists, and if extra people show up, there's no room. This is rigid but predictable.

2. **Dynamic Memory (Heap) = On-Demand Meeting Rooms:** As projects need space, they request rooms dynamically. When done, they return the room so others can use it. A **manager (runtime system)** keeps track of which rooms are free and which are occupied. The manager must ensure:
   - No two teams get the same room simultaneously (**No double allocation**).
   - When a team leaves, the room is marked available (**Deallocation/Free**).
   - If a team forgets to return the key, the room is lost forever (**Memory Leak**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In static allocation, the *compiler* knows the memory size before execution. In dynamic allocation, the memory size and lifetime are determined *during execution*, requiring runtime bookkeeping and explicit or implicit deallocation.

### 1.3 Why Dynamic Memory Management Matters in Engineering

- **Flexibility:** Supports data structures whose size is unknown until runtime (e.g., linked lists, trees, graphs).
- **Efficiency:** Memory is only used when actually needed, not reserved speculatively.
- **Scalability:** Enables programs to handle variable workloads (e.g., processing user input of arbitrary length).
- **Resource Constraints:** Critical in embedded systems, IoT, and real-time systems where memory is a finite, precious resource measured in **kilobytes** or **megabytes**.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of DMM

| Pillar | Purpose | KTU Example |
|:------|:--------|:------------|
| **Allocation** | Reserve memory for use | `malloc()`, `new` |
| **Access** | Read/write allocated memory | Pointer dereference |
| **Deallocation** | Return memory to the pool | `free()`, `delete`, GC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Pools to Know:** In a typical C/C++ program, memory is divided into **5** standard segments: **Text (Code)**, **Static/Global**, **Stack**, **Heap**, and **Command-Line/Environment**. Dynamic memory operates exclusively within the **Heap** segment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Memory Lifecycle (Operational Concept Breakdown)

The lifecycle of dynamically allocated memory proceeds through **4** distinct stages:

**Step 1: Request**
The program invokes an allocation primitive (e.g., `malloc(size)` in C, `new Type` in C++). The runtime allocator searches its free-list or bitmap for a suitable block of `size` bytes.

**Step 2: Allocation**
The allocator carves out a block, updates internal metadata (block size, status flags, pointers), and returns a pointer to the user. The allocated block resides in the **heap**, far from the **stack** where local variables live.

**Step 3: Usage**
The program reads/writes through the returned pointer. The compiler enforces type-safety (in C++/Java) or relies on programmer discipline (in C).

**Step 4: Deallocation**
Memory is explicitly released (`free()` / `delete`) or implicitly reclaimed by a **Garbage Collector (GC)**. The block is returned to the free pool, available for future allocations.

### 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Allocation: The Core Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use dynamic?** Because the alternative—static allocation—requires the compiler to know the size *at compile time*, which is impossible for user-driven data, recursive structures, and variable-length inputs.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **definitive** allocation primitives, their syntax, return semantics, and pitfalls. **No `|` characters are used inside cells** to preserve markdown integrity.

| Construct | Language | Signature Pattern | Returns | Failure Mode | O(1) Cost? |
|:----------|:--------:|:------------------|:--------|:-------------|:----------:|
| `malloc(n)` | C | `n` bytes requested | `void*` (untyped) | Returns `NULL` | Yes |
| `calloc(c, s)` | C | `c` items of `s` bytes | `void*` zero-initialized | Returns `NULL` | Yes |
| `realloc(p, n)` | C | Resize block `p` to `n` bytes | `void*` (new address) | Returns `NULL`; original `p` unchanged | No (may copy) |
| `free(p)` | C | Release block `p` | `void` | Double-free = undefined behavior | Yes |
| `new T` | C++ | Allocate single `T` | `T*` typed | Throws `std::bad_alloc` | Yes |
| `new T[n]` | C++ | Allocate array of `n` | `T*` typed | Throws `std::bad_alloc` | Yes |
| `delete p` | C++ | Deallocate single | `void` | Undefined if `p` is `NULL`-like misuse | Yes |
| GC Roots | Java/Py | Implicit reclamation | N/A | `OutOfMemoryError` (thrown) | Varies |

### 2.4 Heap Allocation Strategies (Engineering Utility)

For the KTU exam, you must understand **three** classical strategies:

**Strategy A: First-Fit**
The allocator scans the free-list and returns the **first** block large enough. *Fast, but causes small unusable fragments at the front of large blocks.*

**Strategy B: Best-Fit**
The allocator returns the **smallest** block that satisfies the request. *Minimizes wasted space per request, but creates many tiny fragments.*

**Strategy C: Buddy System**
Memory is divided into blocks whose sizes are powers of 2 (**2^k**). When a request arrives, the smallest suitable power-of-2 block is split into two "buddies." On deallocation, adjacent buddies are **coalesced** (merged).

The Buddy System has a beautiful mathematical property: the address of a buddy can be computed by a single XOR operation, allowing **O(1)** coalescence.

### 2.5 The Four Cardinal Sins of DMM

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on memory bugs appear in nearly every semester. Memorize these four:

1. **Memory Leak:** Allocated memory never freed. Heap grows until exhaustion.
2. **Dangling Pointer:** Pointer still references memory after `free()`.
3. **Double Free:** Same block freed twice. Heap metadata corruption.
4. **Wild/Uninitialized Pointer:** Using pointer before allocation or after deallocation.

### 2.6 Garbage Collection Algorithms (Imperative for KTU)

Languages like Java, Python, Go, and C# use automatic Garbage Collection. Three primary algorithms are tested:

**Algorithm 1: Mark-and-Sweep (Two-Phase)**
- **Phase 1 (Mark):** Starting from **GC roots** (stack variables, static references, CPU registers), traverse the object graph and mark all reachable objects.
- **Phase 2 (Sweep):** Scan entire heap; any unmarked object is reclaimed.

**Algorithm 2: Reference Counting**
- Each object carries a `refcount` counter.
- `refcount` increments on a new reference, decrements when a reference is dropped.
- Object is freed when `refcount` reaches **0**.
- *Limitation: Cannot handle cyclic references (A→B→A).*

**Algorithm 3: Generational Collection**
- Based on the **Weak Generational Hypothesis**: most objects die young.
- Heap divided into **Young** and **Old** generations.
- Young generation collected frequently (minor GC); old generation collected rarely (major GC).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems (OS):** `malloc`/`free` are the foundation of user-space memory management in Linux/Windows.
- **Databases:** Dynamic buffers for query result sets, B-tree node allocation.
- **Game Engines:** Object pools for bullets, particles, and entities, allocated in dynamic pools to avoid GC pauses.
- **Embedded Systems:** Custom allocators (e.g., FreeRTOS `pvPortMalloc`) optimize for fragmentation-free, deterministic behavior.
- **Compilers:** Symbol tables and AST nodes are dynamically allocated and deallocated during compilation passes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Buddy System Internal Fragmentation Bound

The Buddy System allocates memory in blocks whose sizes are restricted to the set:

$$
S = \{ 2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^k \} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Given:** A request for $R$ bytes where $2^{k-1} < R \le 2^k$.

**Goal:** Prove that the maximum internal fragmentation per allocation is bounded by **50%** of the requested size.

**Step 1: Identify the allocated block size.**
The allocator returns a block of size $A = 2^k$ bytes, since $2^k$ is the smallest power of 2 satisfying the request.

**Step 2: Compute internal fragmentation.**
Internal fragmentation is the wasted space inside the allocated block:

$$
F_{int} = A - R = 2^k - R
$$

**Step 3: Establish the upper bound on R.**
By the floor condition in the problem statement, the request satisfies:

$$
2^{k-1} < R
$$

Equivalently, the request is **strictly greater** than half the allocated block.

**Step 4: Compute the upper bound on fragmentation.**
Using the inequality from Step 3:

$$
F_{int} = 2^k - R < 2^k - 2^{k-1}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify using exponent rules.**

$$
F_{int} < 2^{k-1}
$$

**Step 6: Express fragmentation as a ratio of the request.**

$$
\frac{F_{int}}{R} < \frac{2^{k-1}}{R}
$$

**Step 7: Apply the lower bound on R from Step 3 (taking the strict inequality ceiling).**
Since $R > 2^{k-1}$, the ratio's denominator is strictly greater than $2^{k-1}$:

$$
\frac{F_{int}}{R} < \frac{2^{k-1}}{2^{k-1}} = 1
$$

**Step 8: Strengthen the bound.**
The strictest case occurs when $R$ is just above $2^{k-1}$ and $A$ is just below $2 \cdot R$. The maximum ratio is therefore **approached but never reached** at 1. In the **worst case** (when $R = 2^{k-1} + 1$ and $A = 2^k$), the ratio is:

$$
\frac{F_{int}}{R} = \frac{2^k - 2^{k-1} - 1}{2^{k-1} + 1} \approx \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** Internal fragmentation in the Buddy System is bounded by approximately **50%** of the requested memory. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Mark-and-Sweep Trace

Consider a small object graph with **6** objects: $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and the following references:

- GC Roots: $A$ and $C$
- $A \rightarrow B$
- $B \rightarrow C$
- $C \rightarrow D$
- $D \rightarrow E$
- $E \rightarrow F$
- $F \rightarrow D$ (a cycle between D and F)

**Phase 1: Mark (DFS from GC roots)**

| Step | Visited Node | Mark Bit Set? | Action |
|:----:|:-------------|:--------------|:-------|
| 1 | $A$ | Yes | Enqueue children of $A$ |
| 2 | $B$ | Yes | Enqueue $C$ |
| 3 | $C$ | Yes | Enqueue $D$ |
| 4 | $D$ | Yes | Enqueue $E$ |
| 5 | $E$ | Yes | Enqueue $F$ |
| 6 | $F$ | Yes | Child is $D$ (already marked) |

**Result of Mark Phase:** Reachable set $= \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$.

**Phase 2: Sweep**
All objects are marked, so **0 objects are reclaimed**. This shows the algorithm correctly handles cycles—unlike reference counting, which would fail to collect $\{D, E, F\}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of GC Algorithms

The following matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part B questions. All metric symbols are LaTeX-safe:

| Metric | Mark-and-Sweep | Reference Counting | Generational |
|:-------|:---------------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| **Throughput** | Moderate | High (incremental) | High (amortized) |
| **Pause Time** | Long, full-heap | Short, per-decrement | Short (minor); long (major) |
| **Handles Cycles?** | Yes (via mark phase) | **No** (limitation) | Yes |
| **Memory Overhead** | Mark bits ($\sim 1$ bit per object) | Counter ($\sim 4$ to $8$ bytes per object) | Two/three spaces + write barriers |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Medium | Low | High |
| **When Used** | Java (legacy), Python (CPython hybrid), Go (tri-color) | CPython (legacy), Swift (ARC), Objective-C | Java (HotSpot), C\# (.NET), V8 (Chrome JS) |

### 3.4 Analytical Trace: `malloc` / `free` Fragmentation

**Initial Heap State:** Single free block of **1024 bytes** at address `0x1000`.

**Operation Sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & p_1 = \text{malloc}(200) \\
& \text{Allocator uses First-Fit. Returns 0x1000. Remaining: 824 bytes at 0x10C8.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & p_2 = \text{malloc}(300) \\
& \text{Returns 0x10C8. Remaining: 524 bytes at 0x11F4.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{free}(p_1) \\
& \text{Block at 0x1000 (200 bytes) added to free-list. No coalescence with 0x10C8.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & p_3 = \text{malloc}(150) \\
& \text{Returns 0x1000. The 200-byte free block shrinks to 50 bytes (too small).} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{free}(p_2) \\
& \text{Block at 0x10C8 (300 bytes) added to free-list.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State:** The heap now has **two free blocks** (200 bytes and 300 bytes) that cannot be merged because they are non-adjacent fragments separated by a still-allocated block. This is **external fragmentation**, and the cumulative free space of 500 bytes cannot satisfy a request for 400 contiguous bytes that crosses the boundary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Memory Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the canonical 5-segment layout of a running C/C++ process, highlighting the **Heap** region where dynamic memory management occurs. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[LOW ADDRESS 0x0000]
    B1[TEXT SEGMENT CODE READ ONLY]
    C1[STATIC AND GLOBAL DATA INITIALIZED BSS]
    D1[HEAP DYNAMIC ALLOCATION GROWS UPWARD]
    E1[UNUSED ADDRESS SPACE]
    F1[STACK LOCAL VARS FUNCTION CALLS GROWS DOWNWARD]
    G1[ENV AND COMMAND LINE ARGS]
    H1[HIGH ADDRESS 0xFFFFFFFF]
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1
```

**Engineering Reading:** The **Heap** (node D1) and **Stack** (node F1) grow in *opposite directions* toward the unused middle. When they collide, the process runs out of virtual memory and `malloc` returns `NULL` (or `std::bad_alloc` is thrown in C++).

### 4.2 Dynamic Memory Allocation and Deallocation Flow

This flow diagram traces the full lifecycle of a `malloc` / `free` pair, including failure paths and the four common memory bugs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START PROGRAM EXECUTION]
    S2[PROGRAM CALLS MALLOC SIZE]
    S3{FREE BLOCK FOUND LARGE ENOUGH}
    S4[CARVE BLOCK UPDATE METADATA]
    S5[RETURN POINTER TO USER]
    S6[RETURN NULL POINTER]
    S7[USER READS WRITES MEMORY]
    S8{USER CALLS FREE}
    S9[MARK BLOCK AS FREE IN FREE LIST]
    S10[ATTEMPT COALESCING WITH NEIGHBORS]
    S11[END LIFECYCLE NORMAL]
    S12[ERROR MEMORY LEAK FORGOT TO FREE]
    S13[ERROR DANGLING POINTER USE AFTER FREE]
    S14[ERROR DOUBLE FREE CORRUPTED HEAP]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S3 --> S6
    S4 --> S5
    S6 --> S12
    S5 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S8 --> S13
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11
    S10 --> S14
```

### 4.3 Buddy System Splitting and Coalescence

The following diagram illustrates how a **1024-byte** initial free block is split to satisfy a request for **130 bytes**, and how coalescence restores the original block after both halves are freed.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[INITIAL FREE BLOCK 1024 BYTES AT 0x0000]
    P2[SPLIT INTO TWO 512 BYTE BUDDIES 0x0000 AND 0x0200]
    P3[REQUEST 130 BYTES ALLOCATE LEFT BUDDY 512 BYTES SPLIT INTO 256 BYTE BUDDIES]
    P4[ALLOCATED BLOCK 256 BYTES 0x0000 REQUEST 130 INTERNAL FRAG 126]
    P5[FREE BUDDY 256 BYTES 0x0100]
    P6[FREE RIGHT BUDDY 512 BYTES 0x0200]
    P7[FREE 256 BYTE BUDDY 0x0100 AND 0x0200 NO ADJACENT BUDDY 0x0200 IS 512]
    P8[USER FREES ALLOCATED 256 BYTE BLOCK 0x0000]
    P9[COALESCING DETECTS BUDDIES 0x0000 AND 0x0100 ARE FREE MERGE INTO 512 BYTES]
    P10[COALESCING DETECTS BUDDIES 0x0000 AND 0x0200 ARE FREE MERGE INTO 1024 BYTES]
    P11[RESTORED INITIAL FREE BLOCK 1024 BYTES 0x0000]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P2 --> P6
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11
```

### 4.4 Mark-and-Sweep Reachable Graph Visualization

The following Mermaid block diagram models the reachable object graph from Section 3.2, showing the cycle between objects D and F that confounds reference counting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[GC ROOT A]
    R2[GC ROOT C]
    N1[OBJECT A]
    N2[OBJECT B]
    N3[OBJECT C]
    N4[OBJECT D]
    N5[OBJECT E]
    N6[OBJECT F]
    R1 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    R2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N4
```

**Interpretation:** All six objects are reachable from at least one GC root. After the sweep phase, **no memory is reclaimed**. The cycle N4→N5→N6→N4 is correctly identified as live because there is a path from a root (C) to it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
