## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
> **Differentiate between static memory allocation and dynamic memory allocation. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Static Allocation | Dynamic Allocation |
|:-------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| **When** | At compile time | At runtime |
| **Where** | Stack / Data segment | Heap |
| **Size** | Fixed, must be known at compile time | Variable, can be decided at runtime |
| **Lifetime** | Tied to scope (block, function) | Tied to explicit `free`/`delete` or GC |
| **Example (C)** | `int arr[100];` | `int *p = malloc(100 * sizeof(int));` |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High |

**(1 mark)** definition of static, **(1 mark)** definition of dynamic, **(1 mark)** one example each. *(Total: 3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
> **What is a memory leak? Under what circumstances does it occur, and how can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **memory leak** occurs when heap-allocated memory is **not deallocated** after the program no longer needs it, causing the process's resident memory to grow unboundedly.

**Circumstances:** Losing all pointers to a dynamically allocated block (e.g., reassigning a pointer without first calling `free`), exiting a function without freeing local dynamically allocated memory, or an exception path that bypasses the `free()` call.

**Prevention Strategies:**  
**(1)** Always pair every `malloc`/`new` with a `free`/`delete` in the same scope. **(2)** Use smart pointers (`std::unique_ptr`, `std::shared_ptr`) in C++. **(3)** Use languages with automatic Garbage Collection (Java, Python). **(4)** Employ static analysis tools like **Valgrind** or **AddressSanitizer** during testing.

**(1 mark)** definition, **(1 mark)** circumstance, **(1 mark)** prevention. *(Total: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) – Dynamic Allocation Strategies

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model – Module 3 ESE Pattern]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **Buddy System** algorithm for dynamic memory allocation in detail. Illustrate with an example of how a **1024-byte** initial block is split to satisfy requests of **200 bytes** and **300 bytes**, and then how the blocks are merged back when freed.  
> **    (7 Marks)**  
> **(b)** Compare **First-Fit**, **Best-Fit**, and **Worst-Fit** allocation strategies with respect to **throughput**, **external fragmentation**, and **search complexity**. Which strategy is most commonly used in real-world allocators, and why?  
> **    (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Buddy System is a memory allocation algorithm that divides memory into blocks whose sizes are powers of two. Each block has a uniquely paired "buddy" of the same size, and buddies can be **merged (coalesced)** when both are free.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**  
1. The heap is initialized as a single free block of size $2^k$.  
2. On a request of $R$ bytes, find the smallest $2^m \ge R$.  
3. If $2^m$ is available, allocate it. Otherwise, split a larger block into two buddies of size $2^{m-1}$.  
4. Recursively split until a block of size $2^m$ is obtained.  
5. On `free(p)`, check the buddy of `p` (computed via XOR on the address). If the buddy is also free and of the same size, merge them. Repeat upward.

**Buddy Address Computation (1 mark):**  
For a block of size $2^k$ at address $A$, the buddy's address is:

$$
B = A \oplus 2^k
$$

**Illustrative Trace (3 marks):**

**Initial State:** Free block of size **1024 bytes** at address `0x0000`.

**Request 1: 200 bytes.** Smallest $2^m \ge 200$ is $2^8 = 256$. Split 1024 into two 512-byte buddies. Split the lower 512 (at `0x0000`) into two 256-byte buddies. Allocate the lower 256 (at `0x0000`). Internal fragmentation = $256 - 200 = 56$ bytes.  
Heap now: `[Allocated 256 @ 0x0000] [Free 256 @ 0x0100] [Free 512 @ 0x0200]`

**Request 2: 300 bytes.** Smallest $2^m \ge 300$ is $2^9 = 512$. Take the free 512-byte block at `0x0200` and allocate it directly. Internal fragmentation = $512 - 300 = 212$ bytes.  
Heap now: `[Allocated 256 @ 0x0000] [Free 256 @ 0x0100] [Allocated 512 @ 0x0200]`

**Free Block 1 (the 256-byte block at `0x0000`):** Its buddy is at `0x0100`, which is free and of the same size. **Merge into 512 bytes** at `0x0000`. The new buddy is at `0x0200`, which is allocated. Stop.  
Heap now: `[Free 512 @ 0x0000] [Allocated 512 @ 0x0200]`

**Free Block 2 (the 512-byte block at `0x0200`):** Its buddy is at `0x0000`, which is free and of the same size. **Merge into 1024 bytes** at `0x0000`.  
Heap now: `[Free 1024 @ 0x0000]` — **fully restored**.

---

**Model Solution (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Metric | First-Fit | Best-Fit | Worst-Fit |
|:-------|:----------|:---------|:----------|
| **Selection Rule** | First block large enough | Smallest block that fits | Largest available block |
| **Throughput** | **High** (early termination) | **Low** (full scan) | **Low** (full scan) |
| **External Fragmentation** | Moderate (splits from front) | **High** (tiny unusable fragments) | **Low** (large blocks remain) |
| **Search Complexity** | $O(n)$ worst, $O(1)$ average | $O(n)$ always | $O(n)$ always |
| **Space Utilization** | Moderate | Best in theory | Worst in theory |

**Real-World Choice (2 marks):**  
Most production allocators (e.g., **glibc's `ptmalloc2`**, Google's **tcmalloc**, Facebook's **jemalloc**) use a **segregated free-list** approach: free blocks are bucketed by size class, and within each bucket, a variant of **First-Fit** is applied. This combines the throughput of First-Fit with the space efficiency of Best-Fit, while avoiding full-list scans via bucketing.

**Justification (1 mark):** Segregated fits reduce search time to $O(1)$ amortized, minimize external fragmentation by ensuring requests land in size-appropriate buckets, and scale well under multithreaded workloads through per-thread caches (as in tcmalloc/jemalloc).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Q-A):**  
> 1. **Do NOT** state that the Buddy System guarantees zero fragmentation — internal fragmentation still exists (up to ~50%).  
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse internal fragmentation (inside an allocated block) with external fragmentation (between free blocks).  
> 3. **Always** show the address-level trace; vague textual descriptions lose **2 to 3 marks**.  
> 4. **Always** mention the XOR-based buddy address calculation; it is a frequent sub-part.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Garbage Collection

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Module 3 ESE Pattern]**  
> **(a)** Describe the **Mark-and-Sweep** Garbage Collection algorithm with a suitable diagram. Explain what **GC roots** are and how the algorithm handles **cyclic references**.  
> **    (7 Marks)**  
> **(b)** Compare **Reference Counting** with **Mark-and-Sweep**. Discuss why modern JVMs use a **Generational Garbage Collector** instead of pure Mark-and-Sweep. What is the **Weak Generational Hypothesis**?  
> **    (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):**

The **Mark-and-Sweep** algorithm operates in two distinct phases on the entire heap:

**Phase 1: Mark.**  
- The collector identifies a set of **GC roots**: pointers that are guaranteed to be live, such as:
  - Local variables on every thread's **call stack**
  - **Static fields** of loaded classes
  - **CPU registers** (especially the program counter and stack pointer)
  - JNI global references  
- Starting from each GC root, the collector performs a **graph traversal** (typically Depth-First Search) over the object graph. Each visited object has its **mark bit** set to `1`.

**Phase 2: Sweep.**  
- The collector linearly scans the **entire heap**.
- For every object, it checks the mark bit. If the bit is `0` (unmarked), the object is **unreachable** and is added to the free-list. If the bit is `1` (marked), the bit is reset to `0` for the next collection cycle.

**GC Roots Explanation (2 marks):**  
GC roots are the entry points into the object graph — they represent references the running program can directly access. Without a defined root set, the collector cannot determine which objects are "in use." A common exam point: **stack-allocated references in C are not GC roots in a Java-like managed runtime**, because the runtime cannot introspect arbitrary C stacks. In a pure managed environment, every active stack frame's local variable table is scanned.

**Cyclic Reference Handling (2 marks):**  
The algorithm handles cycles **correctly and automatically**. Consider objects $A$ and $B$ such that $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow A$, but neither is reachable from any GC root. During the Mark phase, neither is visited (no root points to them). Their mark bits remain `0`. During the Sweep phase, both are reclaimed. This is the **decisive advantage** of Mark-and-Sweep over Reference Counting, which cannot reclaim such cycles.

**Diagram (see Section 4.4 above; reference it explicitly in the answer).**

---

**Model Solution (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reference Counting | Mark-and-Sweep |
|:-------|:-------------------|:---------------|
| **When Collected** | Immediately on `refcount = 0` | Periodically (when heap pressure triggers GC) |
| **Pause Time** | Short, distributed across program | Long, stop-the-world pause |
| **Throughput** | Lower (counter updates on every assignment) | Higher (no per-access overhead) |
| **Cycles** | **Cannot collect** | **Can collect** |
| **Memory Overhead** | $4$ to $8$ bytes per object | $1$ bit per object (mark bit) |
| **Real-time Friendly?** | Yes (deferred free possible) | No (long pauses) |

**Why Modern JVMs Use Generational GC (3 marks):**

1. **Empirical Observation:** Studies of production Java workloads (e.g., SPECjbb, DaCapo) show that **80% to 95%** of all allocated objects become unreachable (garbage) within a few megabytes of allocation or within milliseconds of creation.  
2. **Optimization:** Pure Mark-and-Sweep scans the *entire* heap on every collection, wasting effort on the (large) long-lived object population that is rarely garbage. Generational GC exploits this pattern by **partitioning the heap by age**.  
3. **Result:** **Minor collections** of the Young Generation are extremely fast and frequent, reclaiming most garbage cheaply. **Major collections** of the Old Generation are rare and amortized, providing good overall throughput.

**Weak Generational Hypothesis (1 mark):**

The **Weak Generational Hypothesis** states that:

> *Most allocated objects die young, while a small fraction of long-lived objects tend to survive for a long time.*

This empirical observation is the foundational premise of generational garbage collection. It allows the JVM to design two physically separate memory regions (Young and Old) and tune collection algorithms independently for each: a fast, copy-style collector for the Young Generation, and a slower, mark-compact collector for the Old Generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Q-B):**  
> 1. **Do NOT** claim Reference Counting can collect cycles — this is the most common conceptual error.  
> 2. **Always** explicitly name all four types of GC roots (stack locals, static fields, CPU registers, JNI refs).  
> 3. **Always** state the percentages of the Weak Generational Hypothesis (80–95%) with the source observation.  
> 4. **Never** confuse Minor GC (Young Gen only) with Major GC (Full Heap).  
> 5. Sketches/diagrams of the mark phase gain easy marks; **always include one**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Dynamic Memory Management**

- [ ] **Static vs Dynamic:** Static → compile-time, stack, fixed size. Dynamic → runtime, heap, variable size.
- [ ] **Five Memory Segments:** Text, Static/Global, Heap, Stack, Environment. Dynamic memory lives in the **Heap**.
- [ ] **C Primitives:** `malloc(size)`, `calloc(count, size)`, `realloc(ptr, new_size)`, `free(ptr)`. All return `void*` except `free`.
- [ ] **C++ Primitives:** `new Type`, `new Type[n]`, `delete ptr`, `delete[] arr`. Throw `std::bad_alloc` on failure.
- [ ] **Allocation Strategies:** First-Fit (fastest), Best-Fit (most space-efficient in theory, but most fragmented), Worst-Fit (rarely used), Buddy System (power-of-2, O(1) coalesce).
- [ ] **Buddy System Bound:** Internal fragmentation $\le 50\%$ of requested size. Buddy address: $B = A \oplus 2^k$.
- [ ] **Four Memory Bugs:** Memory Leak, Dangling Pointer, Double Free, Wild Pointer. Test using **Valgrind** or **AddressSanitizer**.
- [ ] **Garbage Collection Algorithms:** Mark-and-Sweep (handles cycles, long pauses), Reference Counting (fast, cannot handle cycles), Generational (weak generational hypothesis, two regions).
- [ ] **GC Roots:** Stack locals, static fields, CPU registers, JNI global references. **Four** canonical categories.
- [ ] **Weak Generational Hypothesis:** 80–95% of objects die young; small fraction survives long-term. Foundation of HotSpot, .NET CLR, V8.
- [ ] **Fragmentation Types:** Internal (inside an allocated block, e.g., Buddy System) vs External (between free blocks, e.g., First-Fit).
- [ ] **High-Yield Comparisons:** Know the trade-offs in throughput, pause time, space overhead, and cycle handling for every GC algorithm.
- [ ] **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions, **always** include a labeled diagram and a comparison table — these alone can secure **4 to 5 marks** even with partially incomplete prose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exception Handling and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exception Handling and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Exception Handling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception Handling** is a programming language mechanism designed to intercept and process **runtime anomalies** (exceptional conditions) that disrupt the normal sequential flow of program execution. It separates the **error-detection logic** (placed in protected blocks) from the **error-recovery logic** (placed in dedicated handlers), enabling robust, fault-tolerant software.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an **exception** is any condition that the programmer chooses to handle outside the normal execution path — ranging from hardware faults (division by zero, memory exhaustion) to software-defined semantic violations (file-not-found, index out-of-range).

The four canonical constructs used across KTU-recommended languages are:

| Construct | Role |
|---|---|
| `raise` / `throw` | Explicitly signal an exception |
| `try` / `protected` | Mark a region whose exceptions will be intercepted |
| `catch` / `exception handler` | Define recovery code for a specific exception type |
| `finally` / `cleanup` | Specify code that always runs (release resources) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air-Traffic Tower

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy**: Imagine a busy airport. Pilots (your program statements) follow their flight plan in a straight line. Suddenly, bad weather (an exception) hits. Instead of each pilot improvising their own recovery, they **radio the control tower** (`raise`), which uses a **pre-approved emergency playbook** (`handler`) to redirect them. The runway is always cleared afterward (`finally`).

This separation of "what to do normally" from "what to do abnormally" is the philosophical core of structured exception handling.

---

## 1.3 Execution Environments — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **programming language environment** is the data structure (and associated runtime machinery) that maps **names to entities** (variables, functions, types) and tracks their **attributes** such as type, value, lifetime, and memory address. The environment is consulted during *name resolution*, *binding*, and *type checking*.

Two environment classes matter for KTU Module 3:

1. **Compile-time environment (Static Environment)** — built by the compiler; governs **scope rules** and **type checking**.
2. **Runtime environment (Dynamic Environment)** — built during execution; governs **activation records**, **stack frames**, and **dynamic memory allocation**.

### 1.3.1 Binding Notations Used Throughout KTU Notes

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\rho$ (rho) | Environment (a finite mapping from names → bindings) |
| $\rho(x)$ | Lookup of name $x$ in environment $\rho$ |
| $\rho + \{x \mapsto v\}$ | Environment extension with new binding |
| $\sigma$ (sigma) | Store (maps addresses → values) |
| $\llbracket e \rrbracket \rho \sigma$ | Evaluation of expression $e$ in env $\rho$ and store $\sigma$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Exceptions

Exceptions are organized hierarchically in most modern languages:

- **Synchronous Exceptions** — occur at a predictable point during execution (arithmetic overflow, null pointer dereference).
- **Asynchronous Exceptions** — caused by external events unrelated to the current instruction (interrupts, signals, `Ctrl+C`).
- **Hardware-Generated** — page faults, divide-by-zero traps.
- **Software-Generated** — explicit `throw` statements; user-defined error classes.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 3 emphasizes **structured exception handling** (Java/C++/Python style) rather than the older *error codes + goto* paradigm of C.

---

## 2.2 The Four-Phase Exception Lifecycle

Every well-formed exception event goes through these phases:

1. **Raise / Throw** — A statement like `throw new IOException("disk full")` creates an exception object and signals the runtime.
2. **Propagate** — The runtime walks up the dynamic call stack looking for a matching handler.
3. **Match** — The `catch` clause whose parameter type is a supertype of the thrown object's class is selected.
4. **Recover / Cleanup** — Handler body executes; the `finally` block runs (if present); control resumes after the protected region.

---

## 2.3 Exception Propagation Mechanism (Stack Unwinding)

When no handler exists in the current activation, the runtime **unwinds** the call stack frame-by-frame until it either:
- finds a matching handler, OR
- reaches `main()`, at which point the program terminates abnormally with an **uncaught exception** diagnostic.

**Visualization:**

```
[main]      ← no catch, re-throw to caller
  [foo()]   ← no catch, re-throw to caller
    [bar()] ← throw E() ← EXCEPTION BORN HERE
```

---

## 2.4 Environments — The Three-Layer Model

Every block-scope language (Algol family → C → Java) uses three cooperating data structures:

| Layer | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Static (Lexical) Environment | Determines **where** a name is visible at compile time |
| **L2** | Dynamic Environment / Call Stack | Tracks **which** activation is currently executing |
| **L3** | Store / Heap | Holds the **actual values** at runtime addresses |

### 2.4.1 Block-Structured Environment Operations

A block introduces a new local scope. The standard KTU operations are:

- `enter_block()` — push a new empty environment on the scope stack.
- `add_binding(name, attr)` — insert into the topmost environment.
- `lookup(name)` — search from top down; closest (innermost) wins.
- `exit_block()` — pop the topmost environment.

---

## 2.5 Scope Rules — Static vs Dynamic

| Property | Static (Lexical) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|
| Binding resolved at | **Compile time** | **Run time** |
| Based on | Program text nesting | Call chain |
| Used by | C, Java, Python, ML, Haskell | Bash, early Lisp, APL |
| Predictability | High | Low (action-at-a-distance bugs) |
| KTU Exam Importance | **High** | Moderate |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the high-density reference table for exception handling & environment mechanics. Symbols follow the operational semantics notation used in the KTU 2024 Programming Languages syllabus.

| Concept | Notation / Rule | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| Environment extension | $\rho' = \rho + \{x \mapsto \text{int}, \; x \mapsto 5\}$ | Variable declaration |
| Closed scope (let) | $\llbracket \text{let } x = e_1 \text{ in } e_2 \rrbracket \rho = \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket (\rho + \{x \mapsto \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \rho\})$ | Functional languages |
| Open scope (lambda) | Captures the **defining** environment, not the calling one | Closures |
| Stack frame size | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{size}(v_i) + \text{return\_addr} + \text{static\_link}$ | Activation record |
| Exception dispatch | $\text{handler}(E) = \min \{k \mid \text{frame}_k \text{ catches } E\}$ | Stack unwinding |
| Display table access | $d[i]$ points to the activation whose static depth is $i$ | O(1) nonlocal access |
| Scope depth | $d(x)$ = number of enclosing blocks (including global) | Static link traversal |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems (Aerospace):** Exception handlers reset to a *safe state* when sensor faults occur.
- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Transaction aborts on exceptions preserve **ACID** properties.
- **Compilers & IDEs:** Static environments power symbol resolution, autocomplete, and refactoring tools.
- **Operating Systems:** Kernel-level exception handlers (interrupt service routines) directly mirror user-mode `try/catch` semantics.
- **Network Protocols:** Connection-loss exceptions trigger exponential-backoff retry stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example — Exception Stack Unwinding (Java-style pseudocode)

**Question:** Trace the execution and identify the handler invoked.

```java
void Q() { try { P(); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Q caught"); } }
void P() throws IOException { R(); }
void R() throws IOException { throw new IOException("disk full"); }

main() { try { Q(); } catch (Exception e) { System.out.println("main caught"); } }
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

- **Step 1** — `main()` calls `Q()`. Frame stack: `[main, Q]`.
- **Step 2** — `Q()` calls `P()` inside its `try` block. Frame stack: `[main, Q, P]`.
- **Step 3** — `P()` calls `R()`. Frame stack: `[main, Q, P, R]`.
- **Step 4** — `R()` executes `throw new IOException(...)`. An exception object $E$ of type `IOException` is created.
- **Step 5** — Runtime searches frame stack top-down for a handler matching $E$.
   - Frame `R` — no handler (only `throws` declaration). **Pop.**
   - Frame `P` — no handler (only `throws`). **Pop.**
   - Frame `Q` — has `catch (IOException e)`. **Type matches** because $\text{IOException} <: \text{IOException}$.
- **Step 6** — Handler in `Q` runs; prints `"Q caught"`.
- **Step 7** — Exception is considered **handled**; control returns to `main()` after the call to `Q()`. The outer `catch (Exception e)` in `main()` is **not** triggered.

**Final output:** `Q caught`

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Static vs Dynamic Scope Resolution

Given the following pseudocode (assume dynamic scoping for now):

```
begin
  x := 10
  function P() { return x }
  function Q() { x := 20; return P() }
  print(P())   // Line A
  print(Q())   // Line B
end
```

**Under Static (Lexical) Scoping:**
- `P()` is defined where `x` is visible in the global scope. $x = 10$.
- **Line A** → $\llbracket P() \rrbracket = 10$.
- **Line B** → `Q` reassigns `x = 20`, then calls `P()`. Because `P`'s defining environment captured $x \mapsto 10$, **Line B** still prints $\mathbf{10}$.

**Under Dynamic Scoping:**
- `P()` uses the *most recent* `x` on the call chain.
- **Line A** → prints $\mathbf{10}$ (no other `x` active).
- **Line B** → `Q` sets $x = 20$, then `P()` looks up the dynamic chain and finds $x = 20$. Prints $\mathbf{20}$.

### Formal Derivation (Static Scope)

$$
\llbracket P() \rrbracket \rho_g = \llbracket x \rrbracket \rho_g
$$

where $\rho_g = \{x \mapsto 10\}$ is the environment in which `P` was **defined**. The reassignment in `Q` modifies only the local store binding, **not** the closure environment captured by `P`.

$$
\llbracket x \rrbracket \rho_g = \rho_g(x) = 10
$$

### Formal Derivation (Dynamic Scope)

Let $\rho_d^k$ denote the dynamic chain at call depth $k$.

$$
\llbracket P() \rrbracket \rho_d^{\text{from inside Q}} = \rho_d^2(x) = 20
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Activation Record Layout

Consider the C function:

```c
int foo(int a, int b) {
    int x, y;
    {
        int z;
        ...
    }
    return a + b;
}
```

The runtime stack frame for `foo` is laid out as:

| Offset | Contents | Size |
|---|---|---|
| $+0$ | Return address | $1$ word |
| $+1$ | Saved frame pointer (dynamic link) | $1$ word |
| $+2$ | Static link (to enclosing scope) | $1$ word |
| $+3$ | Parameter `a` | $1$ word |
| $+4$ | Parameter `b` | $1$ word |
| $+5$ | Local `x` | $1$ word |
| $+6$ | Local `y` | $1$ word |
| $+7$ | Inner-block `z` | $1$ word (allocated on entry, freed on exit) |

Total frame size: $S = 8 \text{ words}$.

**Derivation of total size:**

$$
S = \underbrace{3}_{\text{control}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{params}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{locals}} = 8 \text{ words}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Exception Handler Selection with Multiple Catch Clauses

```java
try {
    int[] a = new int[5];
    a[10] = 100;           // throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
} catch (NullPointerException e) {
    System.out.println("NPE");
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("AIOOBE");
} catch (RuntimeException e) {
    System.out.println("RTE");
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("EX");
}
```

**Type hierarchy:**
- `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` <: `IndexOutOfBoundsException` <: `RuntimeException` <: `Exception` <: `Throwable`

**Selection rule:** Java (and C++/Python) select the **first catch clause** (top-down) whose declared parameter type $T$ satisfies $\text{type}(E) \le: T$.

- `catch (NullPointerException)` — fails ($\text{NPE} \not\ge: \text{NPE}$? Actually, $\text{AIOOBE} <: \text{NPE}$ is **false**). Skip.
- `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException)` — succeeds because $\text{AIOOBE} <: \text{AIOOBE}$. ✓ **SELECTED.**

**Output:** `AIOOBE`

The remaining clauses are **never evaluated** even though they would also match — a critical KTU exam point.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Exception Feature Comparison Across Languages

| Feature | Java | C++ | Python | Ada |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raise keyword | `throw` | `throw` | `raise` | `raise` |
| Protected block | `try` | `try` | `try` | `protected` |
| Handler keyword | `catch` | `catch` | `except` | `exception` |
| Cleanup | `finally` | No finally | `finally` | No finally |
| User-defined types | Class hierarchy | Class hierarchy | Class hierarchy | Discrete names |
| Checked vs Unchecked | Both | Unchecked only | Unchecked only | All checked |
| Multiple handlers | Yes (catch chains) | Yes | Yes | Yes (when others) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exception Lifecycle — Mermaid State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalFlow
    NormalFlow --> ProtectedBlock: enter try
    ProtectedBlock --> ExceptionRaised: throw E
    ProtectedBlock --> NormalFlow: success
    ExceptionRaised --> HandlerSearch: propagate
    HandlerSearch --> HandlerMatch: catch T : E <: T
    HandlerSearch --> StackUnwind: no match in current frame
    StackUnwind --> HandlerSearch: caller frame
    StackUnwind --> UncaughtTermination: main reached, no handler
    HandlerMatch --> FinallyBlock: execute handler
    FinallyBlock --> NormalFlow: resume after try
    UncaughtTermination --> [*]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Structured Environment Stack — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Global Scope rho_global] --> B[Function P Scope]
    B --> C[Block inside P Scope]
    C --> D[Inner Lambda Scope]

    A1[x : int, value 10] -. lookup x .-> A
    B1[y : int, value 20] -. lookup y .-> B
    C1[z : float, value 3.14] -. lookup z .-> C
    D1[w : bool, value true] -. lookup w .-> D

    E[Lookup Algorithm] --> F{Innermost binding?}
    F -- yes --> G[Return binding]
    F -- no --> H[Search enclosing scope]
    H --> F
    G --> I[Result]
```

---

## 4.3 Activation Record Layout — Memory Map Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Stack_Growth_Direction["Lower addresses ↓ | Stack grows ↑ | Higher addresses"]
        H1["High Address — Caller Frame"]
        H2["Return Address"]
        H3["Dynamic Link FP_save"]
        H4["Static Link to enclosing scope"]
        H5["Parameter a"]
        H6["Parameter b"]
        H7["Local x"]
        H8["Local y"]
        H9["Inner block z"]
        H10["Low Address — Top of Stack"]
    end
    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> H8 --> H9 --> H10
```

---

## 4.4 Static vs Dynamic Scope — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Static_Lexical_Scope["Static Lexical Scope"]
        S1[Global : x = 10] --> S2[def P uses global x]
        S2 --> S3[Q reassigns x to 20]
        S3 --> S4[P called inside Q - still returns 10]
    end

    subgraph Dynamic_Scope["Dynamic Scope"]
        D1[Global : x = 10] --> D2[def P uses most recent x]
        D2 --> D3[Q reassigns x to 20]
        D3 --> D4[P called inside Q - returns 20]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Exception Handler Resolution Tree — Mermaid Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Exception E thrown] --> Q1{Frame has handler?}
    Q1 -- no --> Pop[Pop frame, walk to caller]
    Pop --> MainReached{Reached main?}
    MainReached -- yes --> Crash[Terminate: uncaught exception]
    MainReached -- no --> Q1
    Q1 -- yes --> Q2{Handler type T satisfies E <: T?}
    Q2 -- no --> NextCatch[Try next catch clause]
    NextCatch --> Q2
    Q2 -- yes --> Run[Execute handler body]
    Run --> Finally[Run finally block if present]
    Finally --> Resume[Resume after try block]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define exception handling. List any two advantages of using exception handling over traditional error-handling mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Exception handling is a structured mechanism that allows a program to detect, raise, and recover from runtime anomalies (exceptions) without terminating abruptly. It separates normal logic from error-handling logic.
2. **Advantages (1.5 marks):**
   - (a) Separates error-handling code from normal code, improving readability and maintainability.
   - (b) Enables propagation of errors up the call stack automatically, removing the need for return-code polling.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between static (lexical) scoping and dynamic scoping with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution time | Compile time | Run time |
| Based on | Program text nesting | Call chain |
| Predictability | High | Low |
| Example languages | C, Java, Python | Bash, early Lisp |

**Example:** If a function `P` references `x`, static scoping binds `P`'s `x` to the one in the **defining** block, while dynamic scoping binds it to the **most recent** active binding on the call stack. *(1 mark example, 2 marks distinction table.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Question A **OR** Question B — answer any **ONE**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the exception handling mechanism in detail with reference to a high-level language like Java. Discuss `try`, `catch`, `throw`, `throws`, and `finally` with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is an activation record? Draw and explain the general structure of an activation record. Describe how static and dynamic links are maintained during function calls.

---

### **Model Solution (a) — Exception Handling in Java [7 marks]**

Java provides five keywords for structured exception handling:

| Keyword | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `try` | Encloses code that may raise an exception |
| `catch` | Declares a handler for a specific exception type |
| `throw` | Explicitly raises an exception |
| `throws` | Declares exceptions a method may propagate |
| `finally` | Code that always executes (cleanup) |

**Code Example (2 marks):**

```java
import java.io.*;
class Demo {
    static void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        try {
            fr.read();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Read error: " + e.getMessage());
            throw e;
        } finally {
            fr.close();
        }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readFile("data.txt");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Handled in main: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of flow (3 marks):**
1. `main()` invokes `readFile()` inside a `try` block.
2. `readFile()` declares `throws IOException` — signalling it may propagate this exception.
3. If an `IOException` occurs inside the inner `try`, control transfers to the `catch` block.
4. The `finally` block always runs, ensuring `fr.close()` executes whether an exception occurred or not.
5. If the inner `catch` re-throws via `throw e`, the exception propagates to `main()`'s `catch`.

**Hierarchy of Java exceptions (2 marks):**
- `Throwable` → `Exception` (checked) and `Error` (unchecked).
- `RuntimeException` is unchecked (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException`).
- Checked exceptions (e.g., `IOException`) must be either caught or declared in `throws`.

---

### **Model Solution (b) — Activation Records [7 marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** An **activation record** (or stack frame) is a runtime data structure created on the call stack for each invocation of a subprogram. It holds all information needed to execute that invocation and to return control to the caller.

**Structure (3 marks):**

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Return address | Where to resume in the caller |
| Dynamic link | Pointer to caller's frame (FP) |
| Static link | Pointer to enclosing static scope's frame |
| Parameters | Actual arguments passed in |
| Local variables | Locals declared in this subprogram |
| Temporaries | Intermediate computation storage |

**Static vs Dynamic Link (3 marks):**
- The **dynamic link** is a pointer to the activation record of the *caller* — required for correct return.
- The **static link** (or display table entry) is a pointer to the activation record of the *enclosing static scope* — required to access non-local variables.

**Example:** When `Q` is called inside `P`, the static link of `Q`'s frame points to `P`'s frame, allowing `Q` to access `P`'s locals. The dynamic link of `Q` points to whoever called `Q` (could be `main`).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of a programming language environment. Differentiate between compile-time and runtime environments. Describe the operations performed on a block-structured environment.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe static and dynamic scoping rules in detail. Show, with an example, how the same program produces different outputs under each scoping rule.

---

### **Model Solution (a) — Environments [7 marks]**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A programming language environment is a mapping from names to their attributes (type, value, location, lifetime). It is the central data structure consulted during name resolution and binding.

**Compile-time vs Runtime Environments (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Compile-time Environment | Runtime Environment |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Type checking, scope analysis | Value storage, address computation |
| Contains | Declarations, types, signatures | Activation records, heap objects |
| Structure | Symbol table, scope tree | Call stack, heap |
| Built by | Compiler front-end | Runtime support / OS |

**Operations on a Block-Structured Environment (3 marks):**

1. **`enter_scope()`** — Push a fresh empty environment frame onto the scope stack (called on entering a block).
2. **`add(name, attribute)`** — Bind `name` to `attribute` in the **current** (innermost) frame.
3. **`lookup(name)`** — Search frames from innermost to outermost; return the **first** match.
4. **`exit_scope()`** — Pop the current frame, discarding all its bindings.

These operations enforce the **most-recent (innermost) binding rule** that defines lexical scoping.

---

### **Model Solution (b) — Static vs Dynamic Scoping [7 marks]**

**Static (Lexical) Scoping (3 marks):**
- A name reference is bound to the **closest enclosing declaration** in the **program text**.
- Resolution occurs at compile time; the binding is fixed once the program is compiled.
- Used by virtually all modern languages: C, Java, Python, ML, Haskell.
- **Predictable**, easier to reason about, easier to optimize.

**Dynamic Scoping (2 marks):**
- A name reference is bound to the **most recent active declaration** on the **runtime call stack**.
- Resolution occurs at execution time.
- Used by Bash, APL, early dialects of Lisp, Emacs Lisp.
- **Less predictable**; small changes in caller code can silently change behavior.

**Example program and outputs (2 marks):**

```
Global: x = 1

procedure P()
    print(x)

procedure Q()
    x = 2
    P()

call P()      // Line 1
call Q()      // Line 2
```

| Line | Static Scoping Output | Dynamic Scoping Output |
|---|---|---|
| Line 1: `call P()` | `1` | `1` |
| Line 2: `call Q()` | `1` | `2` |

Under static scoping, `P` always uses the global `x = 1` (its defining environment). Under dynamic scoping, `P` uses `x = 2` when called from `Q` because `Q` is currently active.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing static and dynamic links** — Many students write "static link points to caller" — WRONG. It points to the **enclosing static scope**, not the caller.
> 2. **Thinking all catch clauses execute** — In Java/C++, only the **first matching** `catch` runs. Subsequent clauses are skipped even if they would also match.
> 3. **Assuming `finally` runs only on exception** — `finally` runs **always**, even when no exception occurs or when a `return` is executed.
> 4. **Mixing checked and unchecked exceptions** — Java requires checked exceptions to be caught or declared; C++ has no such rule.
> 5. **Forgetting environment extension immutability** — In functional languages, $\rho + \{x \mapsto v\}$ creates a **new** environment; the original $\rho$ is unchanged.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **exception** is a runtime anomaly; **exception handling** separates detection from recovery.
- ✅ The four canonical constructs are: `try`, `catch`, `throw`, `finally` (analogous: `protected`, `exception`, `raise`, cleanup).
- ✅ **Stack unwinding** propagates an unhandled exception up the dynamic call chain.
- ✅ Only the **first matching catch** clause executes; subsequent ones are bypassed.
- ✅ A **finally** block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred.
- ✅ An **environment** is a name-to-attribute mapping; an **activation record** is the runtime instance of a subprogram call.
- ✅ **Static (lexical) scoping** uses program text; **dynamic scoping** uses the call chain.
- ✅ A **static link** points to the enclosing scope's frame; a **dynamic link** points to the caller's frame.
- ✅ **Display tables** enable O(1) access to non-local variables in block-structured languages.
- ✅ Closures capture their **defining environment**, enabling lexical scoping in functional languages.
- ✅ Java supports **checked** exceptions (must be caught/declared); C++ and Python have only **unchecked** exceptions.
- ✅ Symbol tables (compile-time) and stack/heap (runtime) together implement a language's environment machinery.
- ✅ The notation $\rho + \{x \mapsto v\}$ denotes **environment extension** — a core KTU exam notation.
- ✅ **Most-recent binding rule:** innermost scope wins during name lookup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Processing Parameter Modes in TinyAda
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Processing Parameter Modes in TinyAda

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Parameter Modes** in Ada (and its pedagogical subset, **TinyAda**) are explicit, compiler-enforced declarations attached to a subprogram's formal parameters that dictate the **directional flow of data** between the caller and the callee. TinyAda, being a teaching-oriented distillation of Ada, preserves the three canonical Ada-83/Ada-95 parameter modes:

1. **IN mode** – Default read-only parameter; data flows strictly from caller to callee.
2. **OUT mode** – Default write-only parameter; data flows strictly from callee back to caller.
3. **IN OUT mode** – Bidirectional parameter; data flows both ways (read and write).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TinyAda is used in this module as a *case study* to demonstrate how a real compiler (the one written by the student in the corresponding compiler construction lab/practicals) translates high-level parameter mode semantics into low-level intermediate code. Parameter modes are the **prime example** of how language design enforces semantic correctness at compile time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **post office parcel system** between two offices — the **Caller** (sending office) and the **Callee** (receiving office).

- **IN mode** → A **sealed outgoing parcel**: The Caller packs documents inside and the Callee may *only read* them. The Callee cannot add pages or seal a reply. This is a one-way information transfer where the caller is the sole contributor.
- **OUT mode** → An **empty return envelope**: The Callee fills in fresh results and ships them back. The Caller does not put anything in — it just waits to receive the completed form. This is useful for "give me an answer" scenarios.
- **IN OUT mode** → A **reusable folder**: The Caller hands over a file with some draft data, the Callee reads it, modifies the contents, and returns the updated folder. Both parties contribute information; the data is shared and mutable.

This parcel model is exactly what the **activation record** (stack frame) of a subprogram call handles: the compiler must decide, *for each parameter*, whether to copy data in, copy data out, or do both.

## 1.3 Why TinyAda Uses Modes Instead of Pointers

Most languages (C, C++, Pascal) achieve parameter passing by *implicit* mechanisms (value, reference, value-result). Ada/TinyAda makes this **explicit and declarative** in the procedure header itself. This:

- Eliminates ambiguous pointer aliasing bugs.
- Allows the compiler to **optimize** (e.g., pass small IN parameters in CPU registers).
- Makes subprogram contracts **documented and verifiable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Mode in Ada/TinyAda:** If no mode is specified, the parameter defaults to **IN mode**. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 1.4 The Three Modes at a Glance

| Mode | Data Flow | Caller may read? | Callee may read? | Callee may write? | Caller sees change? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **IN** | Caller → Callee | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **OUT** | Callee → Caller | ❌ | ❌ (initially) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **IN OUT** | Bidirectional | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Semantic Rules of Each Mode

### 2.1.1 IN Mode — The Read-Only Path

**Compilation Logic (Why and How):**
- The compiler generates code to **copy** the actual parameter's value into the callee's activation record **before** the call.
- Inside the callee, the formal parameter behaves as a **local constant**. Any attempt to assign to it is a **compile-time error**.
- The caller’s original variable is **never touched** after the copy is made.

**Practical Use Case:** Passing an input array, a threshold value, or a configuration flag.

### 2.1.2 OUT Mode — The Write-Only Path

**Compilation Logic:**
- Before the call, the compiler treats the formal as **uninitialized** (in Ada semantics, OUT parameters are deassigned upon entry — the actual parameter's initial value is essentially discarded if it is a scalar).
- The callee must **assign** a value to the parameter before normal completion; failure to do so leaves the actual undefined (a flagged warning in Ada compilers).
- On return, the final value of the formal is **copied back** to the caller's actual.

**Practical Use Case:** Returning multiple results without bundling into a record, or initializing an output buffer.

### 2.1.3 IN OUT Mode — The Read-Write Path

**Compilation Logic:**
- Combines both behaviors: the actual is copied in, modified inside, and copied back.
- Equivalent to **value-result passing** (copy-in / copy-out) in many compiler implementations.
- Equivalent to **pass-by-reference** in semantically simpler languages — but with strict no-aliasing guarantees.

**Practical Use Case:** Updating a counter, modifying a matrix in place, accumulating a running total.

## 2.2 Activation Record Layout (Implementation View)

When the compiler processes `Proc(X : IN Integer; Y : OUT Integer; Z : IN OUT Integer)`, the activation record stores:

- For **X (IN)**: a snapshot copy of X.
- For **Y (OUT)**: storage space, initially unassigned.
- For **Z (IN OUT)**: a snapshot copy of Z on entry; on return, this copy overwrites the caller's Z.

The compiler also generates **epilogue code** (copy-back) for OUT and IN OUT parameters, which is the heart of the case study.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | IN Mode | OUT Mode | IN OUT Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default mode? | **Yes** | No (must be explicit) | No (must be explicit) |
| Direction | Unidirectional (in) | Unidirectional (out) | Bidirectional |
| Read in callee | Allowed | Disallowed until assigned | Allowed |
| Write in callee | **Compile error** | Allowed (required for scalars) | Allowed |
| Aliasing with global? | Cannot happen | Cannot happen | Cannot happen (Ada rule) |
| Pass mechanism (typical) | Copy-in | Copy-out | Copy-in / Copy-out |
| Register optimization? | **Frequent** (esp. for small scalars) | Rare (needs addressable storage) | Rare |
| Const-correctness equivalent | `const T&` in C++ | N/A in C++ | `T&` in C++ |
| TinyAda keyword | `IN` | `OUT` | `IN OUT` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** IN modes let you pass sensor thresholds safely without corruption.
- **Parallel Computing:** Knowing that IN parameters are pure read-only inputs enables the compiler to safely reorder calls and even parallelize them.
- **Formal Verification:** Strict mode semantics make Ada programs amenable to SPARK-style proofs — vital in avionics, railway signaling, and medical devices.
- **Compiler Design Education:** TinyAda’s mode handling is the canonical example of how **semantic analysis** in a compiler enforces language rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Ada/TinyAda disallows aliasing between parameters and global variables accessed in a subprogram. This is a unique safety property not present in C/C++ and is a frequent 7-mark question topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The TinyAda Case Study Procedure

Consider this canonical TinyAda example, often used in KTU board examinations:

```ada
procedure MAIN is
   A : INTEGER := 10;
   B : INTEGER := 20;
   C : INTEGER := 30;
   procedure SWAP(X : IN OUT INTEGER; Y : IN OUT INTEGER) is
      T : INTEGER;
   begin
      T := X;
      X := Y;
      Y := T;
   end SWAP;
   procedure REPORT(P : IN INTEGER; Q : OUT INTEGER) is
   begin
      Q := P * 2;
   end REPORT;
begin
   SWAP(A, B);
   REPORT(C, A);
end MAIN;
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

We trace the activation records, the stack state, and the final variable values.

### Step 1: Initial State (Before MAIN body executes)

| Variable | Memory Address (logical) | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| A | 1000 | 10 |
| B | 1004 | 20 |
| C | 1008 | 30 |

### Step 2: Call `SWAP(A, B)`

**Copy-In Phase (Prologue):**

The compiler generates code equivalent to:

```
Load address of A  → R1
Load value  [R1]   → R2  (=10)
Store R2 in SWAP.X
Load address of B  → R3
Load value  [R3]   → R4  (=20)
Store R4 in SWAP.Y
```

**Stack frame of SWAP:**

| Slot | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| X (IN OUT) | 10 |
| Y (IN OUT) | 20 |
| T (local) | ? |

**Body Execution:**

```
T := X      → T = 10
X := Y      → X = 20
Y := T      → Y = 10
```

**Copy-Back Phase (Epilogue):**

```
Store X (=20) into A  [address 1000]
Store Y (=10) into B  [address 1004]
```

### Step 3: After `SWAP(A, B)` returns

| Variable | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| A | 20 |
| B | 10 |
| C | 30 |

### Step 4: Call `REPORT(C, A)`

**Copy-In Phase (Prologue for IN):**

```
Load value of C (=30) → copy into P
```

**OUT parameter treatment:** `A` is conceptually **deassigned** (for scalar OUT, the original value 20 is discarded by Ada semantics). The activation record allocates space for Q but leaves it uninitialized.

**Stack frame of REPORT:**

| Slot | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| P (IN) | 30 |
| Q (OUT) | uninitialized |

**Body Execution:**

```
Q := P * 2  → Q = 60
```

**Copy-Back Phase (Epilogue):**

```
Store Q (=60) into A  [address 1000]
```

### Step 3.5: Final State

| Variable | Final Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| A | 60 |
| B | 10 |
| C | 30 |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compilation Actions per Mode

For each mode, the compiler emits specific instructions. We formalize this as a derivation table:

| Compilation Phase | IN Mode Action | OUT Mode Action | IN OUT Mode Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Symbol table entry** | `mode = IN, read=true, write=false` | `mode = OUT, read=false, write=true` | `mode = IN OUT, read=true, write=true` |
| **Prologue** | `formal := actual` (copy-in) | `formal := undefined` (deassign) | `formal := actual` (copy-in) |
| **Body check** | Reject assignments to formal | Require assignment before use | Allow both read and write |
| **Epilogue** | No copy-back | `actual := formal` (copy-out) | `actual := formal` (copy-out) |
| **Optimization opportunity** | Pass in **register** if scalar | Must use **memory** slot | Must use **memory** slot |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Output Trace

For the following TinyAda program, KTU frequently asks to *determine the output*:

```ada
procedure MAIN is
   X : INTEGER := 5;
   Y : INTEGER := 7;
   procedure INCR(A : IN INTEGER; B : IN OUT INTEGER; C : OUT INTEGER) is
   begin
      C := A + B;
      B := B + 1;
   end INCR;
begin
   INCR(X, Y, X);
   PUT(Y);
   PUT(X);
end MAIN;
```

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before INCR call:} \quad & X = 5,\ Y = 7 \\
\text{Prologue: copy-in} \quad & A := 5,\ B := 7,\ C := \text{undefined} \\
\text{Execute body:} \quad & C := A + B = 5 + 7 = 12 \\
& B := B + 1 = 7 + 1 = 8 \\
\text{Epilogue: copy-out} \quad & C \rightarrow X \Rightarrow X = 12 \\
& B \rightarrow Y \Rightarrow Y = 8 \\
\text{Final output:} \quad & \texttt{PUT(Y)} \Rightarrow 8 \\
& \texttt{PUT(X)} \Rightarrow 12
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice how the *same* variable `X` is passed as both IN and OUT. This is legal in Ada/TinyAda — and the OUT copy-back **overwrites** the original. This is a classic KTU trap question where students forget that the OUT copy-back happens *after* the body executes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parameter Mode Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subprogram Call Invoked] --> B{Inspect Formal Parameter Mode}
    B --> C[IN Mode Detected]
    B --> D[OUT Mode Detected]
    B --> E[IN OUT Mode Detected]
    C --> C1[Prologue: Copy Actual into Formal]
    C1 --> C2[Mark Formal as Read-Only in Symbol Table]
    C2 --> C3[Execute Subprogram Body]
    C3 --> C4[Epilogue: No Copy-Back]
    C4 --> Z[Return to Caller]
    D --> D1[Prologue: Deassign Formal Undefined]
    D1 --> D2[Mark Formal as Write-Only in Symbol Table]
    D2 --> D3[Execute Subprogram Body with Required Assignment]
    D3 --> D4[Epilogue: Copy Formal Back to Actual]
    D4 --> Z
    E --> E1[Prologue: Copy Actual into Formal]
    E1 --> E2[Mark Formal as Read-Write]
    E2 --> E3[Execute Subprogram Body with Modifications]
    E3 --> E4[Epilogue: Copy Modified Formal Back to Actual]
    E4 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Activation Record Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CALLER[CALLER Activation Record]
        A1[Variable A]
        B1[Variable B]
        C1[Variable C]
    end
    subgraph ARGS[Argument Passing Region]
        X1[Copy of A to X]
        Y1[Copy of B to Y]
        Z1[Uninitialized Slot for Z]
    end
    subgraph CALLEE[CALLEE Activation Record]
        F1[Formal X: IN OUT]
        F2[Formal Y: IN OUT]
        F3[Formal Z: OUT]
        L1[Local Variable T]
    end
    subgraph EPILOGUE[Epilogue Copy-Back]
        CB1[X back to A]
        CB2[Y back to B]
        CB3[Z back to C]
    end
    A1 --> X1
    B1 --> Y1
    C1 --> Z1
    X1 --> F1
    Y1 --> F2
    Z1 --> F3
    F1 --> L1
    L1 --> F1
    F1 --> CB1
    F2 --> CB2
    F3 --> CB3
    CB1 --> A1
    CB2 --> B1
    CB3 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Symbol Table Attribute Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Symbol Table Entry for Formal Parameter] --> ST1[Name: X]
    ST --> ST2[Type: INTEGER]
    ST --> ST3[Mode: IN OUT]
    ST --> ST4[Read Permission: TRUE]
    ST --> ST5[Write Permission: TRUE]
    ST --> ST6[Storage Class: Memory Slot]
    ST --> ST7[Offset in Activation Record: +8]
    ST2 --> ST4
    ST3 --> ST4
    ST3 --> ST5
    ST3 --> ST6
```

## 4.4 Compilation Pipeline Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[TinyAda Source with Parameter Modes] --> LEX[Lexical Analysis]
    LEX --> PARSE[Syntax Analysis: Parse Formal Parameter List]
    PARSE --> SEM[Semantic Analysis: Enforce Mode Rules]
    SEM --> ST[Update Symbol Table with Mode Attributes]
    ST --> CG[Code Generation]
    CG --> IC[Intermediate Code with Copy-In and Copy-Out]
    IC --> OPT[Optimizer: Register Allocation for IN Scalars]
    OPT --> TGT[Target Code: Assembly with Prologue/Epilogue]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The COPY-IN and COPY-OUT blocks are the *physical manifestation* of mode semantics. In a KTU exam, drawing a similar diagram and labelling the copy-back arrows for IN OUT and OUT parameters fetches full marks for "explain parameter mode processing" type questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: List the three parameter modes in Ada/TinyAda. Which one is the default?**

**Model Answer:**
The three parameter modes in Ada/TinyAda are:

1. **IN** — Read-only; data flows from caller to callee.
2. **OUT** — Write-only; data flows from callee to caller.
3. **IN OUT** — Read-write; bidirectional data flow.

**IN** is the default mode. If a parameter in a procedure header has no explicit mode keyword, the compiler treats it as IN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Students often incorrectly state "IN OUT" as the default. Avoid this — IN is the Ada-standard default.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Why does Ada disallow aliasing between an IN OUT parameter and a global variable accessed in the same subprogram? Give a one-line reason.**

**Model Answer:**
To **prevent ambiguous updates** and ensure the program has a deterministic, well-defined semantics, since the compiler's copy-in/copy-out mechanism could otherwise produce inconsistent results depending on evaluation order.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q (a)** With suitable TinyAda code examples, explain the three parameter modes IN, OUT, and IN OUT. Discuss how the compiler generates prologue and epilogue code for each mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three parameter modes in TinyAda specify the direction of data flow between a caller and a callee subprogram.

**(1) IN Mode:** Data flows only from the caller to the callee. The formal parameter acts as a local constant inside the callee; any assignment to it is a compile-time error. The compiler emits a *prologue* that copies the actual parameter's value into the formal's storage slot, and *no epilogue* is needed.

```ada
procedure PRINT_VAL(V : IN INTEGER) is
begin
   PUT(V);
end PRINT_VAL;
```

Here `V` cannot be reassigned inside the procedure.

**(2) OUT Mode:** Data flows only from the callee to the caller. The formal is deassigned on entry (uninitialized for scalars). The callee must assign a value before normal return. The compiler emits an *epilogue* that copies the final value of the formal back into the caller's actual.

```ada
procedure DOUBLE_VAL(V : IN INTEGER; R : OUT INTEGER) is
begin
   R := V * 2;
end DOUBLE_VAL;
```

**(3) IN OUT Mode:** Bidirectional. The compiler emits both a copy-in prologue and a copy-back epilogue. This is equivalent to value-result (copy-in/copy-out) passing.

```ada
procedure SWAP(A : IN OUT INTEGER; B : IN OUT INTEGER) is
   T : INTEGER;
begin
   T := A; A := B; B := T;
end SWAP;
```

**Prologue/Epilogue Summary:**

- IN: `formal := actual` (entry) — no exit action.
- OUT: `formal := undefined` (entry) — `actual := formal` (exit).
- IN OUT: `formal := actual` (entry) — `actual := formal` (exit).

---

**Q (b)** Consider the following TinyAda program. Trace the execution and determine the final values of X, Y, and Z, with a clear explanation of each copy-in and copy-out step. **(7 marks)**

```ada
procedure MAIN is
   X : INTEGER := 2;
   Y : INTEGER := 3;
   Z : INTEGER := 5;
   procedure P(A : IN INTEGER; B : IN OUT INTEGER; C : OUT INTEGER) is
   begin
      B := A + B;
      C := A * B;
   end P;
begin
   P(X, Y, Z);
end MAIN;
```

**Model Solution:**

**Initial state:** X=2, Y=3, Z=5.

**Step 1 — Prologue (Copy-In):**

- A (IN) ← X = **2**
- B (IN OUT) ← Y = **3**
- C (OUT) ← undefined (Z’s value 5 is logically discarded for scalar OUT)

**Step 2 — Body Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &:= A + B = 2 + 3 = 5 \\
C &:= A \times B = 2 \times 5 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Epilogue (Copy-Back):**

- B → Y ⇒ Y = 5
- C → Z ⇒ Z = 10

**Final values:**

| Variable | Final Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| X | 2 (unchanged, only used as IN) |
| Y | 5 (overwritten by IN OUT copy-back) |
| Z | 10 (overwritten by OUT copy-back) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Differentiate between pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, and pass-by-value-result. Show how each of these corresponds to Ada/TinyAda parameter modes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pass-by-Value:** The actual parameter's value is copied into the callee's formal. Modifications to the formal do not affect the actual. This corresponds to Ada's **IN mode** — the formal is a local copy and any write is forbidden.

**Pass-by-Reference:** The formal parameter becomes an alias (alternate name) for the actual. All reads and writes via the formal directly affect the actual's memory. Ada's **IN OUT mode** is *semantically* similar to pass-by-reference in that the callee can both read and write, but TinyAda achieves this through copy-in/copy-out (value-result) for safety, not raw aliasing. Strict reference semantics would violate Ada's no-aliasing rule.

**Pass-by-Value-Result (Copy-In/Copy-Out):** A copy of the actual is made on entry, the callee operates on the local copy, and the final value is copied back to the actual on exit. This is exactly how Ada's **IN OUT mode** is implemented in most compilers.

**Comparison Table:**

| Mechanism | Mode Equivalent | Modifies Actual? | Multiple Copies? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Pass-by-Value | IN | No | One (entry) |
| Pass-by-Reference | IN OUT (semantic) | Yes | Zero (direct alias) |
| Pass-by-Value-Result | IN OUT (implementation) | Yes | Two (in + out) |
| Output-only | OUT | Yes | One (exit) |

---

**Q (b)** A TinyAda procedure is declared as:

```ada
procedure COMPUTE(P : IN INTEGER; Q : OUT INTEGER; R : IN OUT INTEGER);
```

A main program calls it as `COMPUTE(7, R, R);` where `R` is an integer variable initially holding 4. If the body of COMPUTE is `BEGIN Q := P + R; R := P * 2; END;`, determine the final value of R and explain your reasoning step-by-step, highlighting the impact of using the same actual for OUT and IN OUT. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Initial value:** R = 4.

**Step 1 — Prologue (Copy-In):**

- P (IN) ← actual R = **4** (R is the first actual supplied, but only after evaluating all actuals — TinyAda evaluates actuals left-to-right before binding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** In Ada/TinyAda, the actual parameters are evaluated *before* the call, but each formal is bound to a distinct copy. So the OUT formal Q and IN OUT formal R each get their own logical copy slot, even though they refer to the same source variable R.

- Q (OUT) ← undefined
- R_formal (IN OUT) ← actual R = 4

**Step 2 — Body Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &:= P + R_{formal} = 4 + 4 = 8 \\
R_{formal} &:= P \times 2 = 4 \times 2 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Epilogue (Copy-Back):**

- Q → R ⇒ R = 8
- R_formal → R ⇒ R = 8 (second copy-back overwrites with same value)

**Final value of R = 8.**

The key takeaway: when the same variable is used for multiple modes, the final value depends on the *order of epilogue copy-back*, not the order of formal declarations. In TinyAda, copy-back typically follows the order of formal parameters in the header (left to right), so Q copies first, then R_formal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting that OUT parameters discard the initial value** for scalars — students often write `Q := P + R = 4 + 4 = 8` using R's original 4, which is *coincidentally* correct here but conceptually wrong for true OUT semantics. Always show the deassignment step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing epilogue order** — the order in which copy-backs occur matters when actuals are aliased. KTU specifically tests this.
> 3. **Treating OUT like IN OUT** — students forget that OUT parameters cannot be *read* until assigned inside the body. Writing `Q := Q + 1` on a freshly entered OUT is illegal.
> 4. **Ignoring aliasing restriction** — Ada forbids aliasing between parameters. In some Ada variants, `COMPUTE(7, R, R)` where the same variable is used for two distinct modes can trigger a runtime or compile-time check; TinyAda is permissive but KTU expects you to discuss this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- ✅ **Three parameter modes** in TinyAda: **IN**, **OUT**, **IN OUT**.
- ✅ **Default mode = IN** (most-asked 2-marker).
- ✅ **IN** = copy-in, read-only, no epilogue.
- ✅ **OUT** = deassign on entry, write-only, copy-out on exit.
- ✅ **IN OUT** = copy-in + copy-out, value-result semantics.
- ✅ **Aliasing rule:** Ada forbids an IN OUT parameter from aliasing a global or another parameter accessed in the same subprogram.
- ✅ **Optimization:** Small scalar IN parameters can be passed in CPU registers; OUT and IN OUT cannot (need addressable storage).
- ✅ **Trace questions** are KTU's favorite: practice drawing the activation record and labelling copy-in / copy-back arrows.
- ✅ **Epilogue order matters** when actuals are shared between modes — always state the order.
- ✅ **OUT parameters are deassigned** on entry for scalar types (do not assume the original value is preserved).
- ✅ **The compiler enforces mode rules at semantic analysis time** — this is the link between TinyAda and the compiler construction concepts of Module 3.
- ✅ **Equivalent C++ terms** (for cross-reference only): `IN` ≈ `const T&`, `IN OUT` ≈ `T&`, no exact C++ equivalent for `OUT`.
- ✅ **TinyAda's purpose** in this module is to demonstrate *language design decisions* — Ada chose explicit modes for safety; C chose implicit pointer aliasing for flexibility; TinyAda preserves Ada's choice for pedagogical clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Data Types and Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Abstract Data Types and Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Abstract Data Types and Modules

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Abstract Data Type (ADT)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the user's point of view — specifically in terms of:
> 1. **Possible values** the data can take
> 2. **Possible operations** defined on the data
> 3. **The behavior** of those operations (their effects and return values)
>
> Crucially, an ADT **hides the internal representation** (how the data is stored) and **exposes only the operations** (how the data is manipulated).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Phrase for Exams:** *"An ADT is defined by its operations, not by its implementation."*

**Components of an ADT (Triple Form):**

$$ADT = \langle D, F, A \rangle$$

Where:
- $D$ = Set of data values (the *type*)
- $F$ = Set of functions (the *operations*)
- $A$ = Set of axioms (the *semantic rules* governing the operations)

---

### 1.2 What is a Module?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Module** is a separately compilable program unit that groups related **declarations** (types, variables, constants) and **subprograms** (functions/procedures) into a single named unit. A module has two distinct parts:
> 1. **Interface (Specification)** — What the module *exposes* to the outside world
> 2. **Implementation (Body)** — How the module *internally* achieves its behavior (hidden from users)

---

### 1.3 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — Make It Stick!

> [!TIP]
> **The ATM Machine Analogy (for ADT):**
> Imagine an ATM machine. You (the user) interact with it using buttons: `Withdraw`, `Deposit`, `Check Balance`. You don't care **how** the ATM stores money internally (does it track bills? database? ledgers?). You only care about the **operations** and their **guaranteed behavior**. If you press `Withdraw(5000)`, the balance decreases by 5000. That's an **ADT** — *behavior contract without implementation exposure*.

> [!TIP]
> **The Library Book Cabinet Analogy (for Module):**
> Think of a wooden cabinet with labeled drawers. The *labels on the drawers* (e.g., "Mathematics Books", "History Books") are the **Interface** — they tell you what's available. The *chaos inside the drawer* (how books are organized) is the **Implementation** — hidden from you. You don't need to know if books are arranged alphabetically or by size. The cabinet itself is a **Module** — a self-contained, named unit with a clear boundary.

---

### 1.4 Why Do We Need ADTs and Modules?

| Problem Without ADTs/Modules | Solution Provided by ADTs/Modules |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Data structures intertwined with code → **maintenance nightmare** | Clean separation of *what* from *how* |
| Changing internal representation breaks client code | **Representation independence** — internal changes don't affect users |
| Global data accessible everywhere → **unsafe** | **Encapsulation** — only intended operations are accessible |
| Large programs are unmanageable | **Modularity** — divide program into manageable, named units |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** ADTs and Modules are the **two foundational pillars** of structured software design. Most modern programming paradigms (Object-Oriented, Functional) are built on top of these two ideas.

---

### 1.5 ADT vs Module — A Subtle Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusion Trap:** Students often treat ADTs and Modules as *synonyms*. They are **related but distinct** concepts.
>
> - An **ADT** is a *conceptual/mathematical* notion — a *type* defined by its operations and semantics. It is **language-independent**.
> - A **Module** is a *language construct* — a *syntactic mechanism* used to **realize** ADTs in actual code. It is **language-dependent**.

| Aspect | ADT | Module |
|--------|-----|--------|
| Nature | Conceptual / Mathematical | Syntactic / Language Feature |
| Purpose | Define a *type* and its *behavior* | Group related code into *named units* |
| Encapsulation | Yes (semantic) | Yes (physical — via visibility rules) |
| Example | "A Stack ADT" | `package Stack` in Ada, `class Stack` in C++ |
| Exists in | Mathematics, Specifications | Actual programming languages |
| One-to-one? | One ADT can be implemented using **multiple** modules | One module can contain **multiple** ADTs |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Abstract Data Type

An ADT specification consists of **three logical sections** (this is the standard KTU board-exam answer format):

### 📋 (a) Type Declaration
What kind of values does the ADT hold? (a *mathematical* description)
- Example: `Stack` holds a sequence of elements of type `ElementType`.

### ⚙️ (b) Function/Operation Declarations
What operations are available? For each operation, specify:
- **Name** (e.g., `push`, `pop`, `top`)
- **Domain** (input types / parameters)
- **Range** (output / return type)
- **Preconditions** (what must be true *before* the operation)
- **Postconditions** (what is guaranteed *after* the operation)

### 📐 (c) Axioms / Semantic Rules
Formal equations that define the *meaning* of the operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Axiom (Stack ADT):**
> $$\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$$
> This axiom says: "After pushing $x$ onto stack $S$, the top element is $x$." Such axioms are the **true defining contract** of an ADT.

---

## 2.2 The Stack ADT — Worked Specification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked ADT** in KTU exams. Memorize this specification.

### Type Section
```text
ADT Stack(ElementType)
```

### Operations Section

| Operation | Signature | Precondition | Postcondition | Effect |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------------|--------|
| `create` | $\rightarrow$ Stack | — | Returns a fresh empty stack | $\text{create}() = \epsilon$ |
| `push` | Stack $\times$ ElementType $\rightarrow$ Stack | — | Returns a new stack with $x$ on top | $\text{push}(S, x) = \langle x, S \rangle$ |
| `pop` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Stack | $S \neq \epsilon$ | Returns stack with top removed | — |
| `top` | Stack $\rightarrow$ ElementType | $S \neq \epsilon$ | Returns top element | — |
| `isEmpty` | Stack $\rightarrow$ Boolean | — | Returns true if $S = \epsilon$ | — |

### Axioms Section

$$\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$$

$$\text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S$$

$$\text{isEmpty}(\text{create}()) = \text{true}$$

$$\text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false}$$

$$\text{top}(\text{create}()) = \text{error}_{\text{underflow}}$$

---

## 2.3 The Queue ADT — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> The **Queue** is the second-most-tested ADT. It is a **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) structure.

| Operation | Signature | Precondition | Effect |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| `create` | $\rightarrow$ Queue | — | Returns empty queue |
| `enqueue` | Queue $\times$ ElementType $\rightarrow$ Queue | — | Adds element to **rear** |
| `dequeue` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Queue | $Q \neq \epsilon$ | Removes element from **front** |
| `front` | Queue $\rightarrow$ ElementType | $Q \neq \epsilon$ | Returns front element |
| `isEmpty` | Queue $\rightarrow$ Boolean | — | Returns true if $Q = \epsilon$ |

---

## 2.4 Anatomy of a Module

A module has **two mandatory parts**:

### 📜 (a) Interface / Specification
Lists the *public* (exported) names that other modules can use.
- **Type declarations** (e.g., `Stack` as a *type name*, not the structure inside)
- **Subprogram headers** (signatures only — no bodies)
- **Constants, exceptions** exposed to the world

### 🔒 (b) Implementation / Body
- **Private** (hidden) declarations — local types, helper functions
- **Subprogram bodies** — the actual code
- Cannot be seen by client modules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Representation Independence (KTU High-Yield Topic):**
> A well-designed module allows the implementer to change the *internal representation* (e.g., from array to linked list) **without affecting any client code**, as long as the *interface* and *semantics* remain unchanged.

---

## 2.5 Module Realizations Across Languages

| Language | Module Construct | Mechanism for Hiding |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Ada** | `package` | `private` part separates spec from body |
| **Modula-2** | `MODULE` | `EXPORT` and `IMPORT` lists |
| **C++** | `namespace` + `class` | `public`, `private`, `protected` |
| **Java** | `package` | `public`, `private`, `protected`, default |
| **ML** | `structure` / `signature` | Explicit signature matching |
| **Python** | `module` | `_underscore` convention, `__all__` |
| **Haskell** | `module` | `export` list |

---

## 2.6 📚 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision.**

| # | Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Used For |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | ADT Triple | $ADT = \langle D, F, A \rangle$ | Defining an ADT |
| 2 | Stack Empty Test | $\text{isEmpty}(\text{create}()) = \text{true}$ | Stack semantics |
| 3 | Stack Top After Push | $\text{top}(\text{push}(S,x)) = x$ | Stack axioms |
| 4 | Stack Pop After Push | $\text{pop}(\text{push}(S,x)) = S$ | Stack axioms |
| 5 | Module Structure | $\text{Module} = \text{Interface} + \text{Implementation}$ | Module design |
| 6 | FIFO Property (Queue) | $\text{dequeue}(\text{enqueue}(\epsilon, a)) = a$ | Queue semantics |
| 7 | Encapsulation | $\text{User} \rightarrow \text{Interface} \rightarrow \text{Implementation}$ | Visibility control |
| 8 | Representation Independence | $\Delta_{\text{impl}} \Rightarrow \Delta_{\text{client}} = \emptyset$ | Modular design |
| 9 | Time Complexity (Stack) | $\text{push}/\text{pop}/\text{top} = O(1)$ | Array implementation |
| 10 | Time Complexity (Queue) | $\text{enqueue}/\text{dequeue} = O(1)$ | Circular array implementation |

> Where $\Delta_{\text{impl}}$ denotes a change in internal implementation, and $\Delta_{\text{client}} = \emptyset$ means *no change* is forced on the client.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | ADT/Module Used | Purpose |
|--------|-----------------|---------|
| Operating Systems | Stack ADT | Process call stack, interrupt handling |
| Networking | Queue ADT | Packet buffering (FIFO scheduling) |
| Databases | B-Tree ADT | Indexed data retrieval |
| Compilers | Symbol Table Module | Variable name management |
| Embedded Systems | Priority Queue ADT | Real-time task scheduling |
| Graphics | Stack Module | Undo/Redo operations |
| AI/ML | Graph ADT | Search algorithms (DFS uses Stack, BFS uses Queue) |
