## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Building the Ground Term Algebra $T_\Sigma$ for a Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Construction of $T_\Sigma$ (Stack of Naturals)**

**Signature:** $\Sigma = (S, F)$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \{\text{stack},\; \text{nat},\; \text{bool}\} \\
F &= \{
   \text{new} : \to \text{stack},\;
   \text{push} : \text{stack} \times \text{nat} \to \text{stack}, \\
&\quad\;\;\text{top} : \text{stack} \to \text{nat},\;
   \text{pop} : \text{stack} \to \text{stack},\;
   \text{isEmpty} : \text{stack} \to \text{bool},\;
   0 : \to \text{nat},\;
   \text{succ} : \text{nat} \to \text{nat}
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Construction (by induction on term depth):**

*Base layer (depth 0):* For each nullary operation in $F$, introduce a term:

$$
T_0 = \{\,\text{new},\; 0\,\}
$$

*Inductive layer (depth $n+1$):* If $t_1, \ldots, t_k$ are terms of sorts $s_1, \ldots, s_k$ already constructed, and $f : s_1 \times \cdots \times s_k \to s \in F$, then $f(t_1, \ldots, t_k)$ is a term of sort $s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= T_0 \;\cup\; \{\,\text{succ}(0),\; \text{push}(\text{new}, 0)\,\} \\
T_2 &= T_1 \;\cup\; \{\,\text{push}(\text{new}, \text{succ}(0)),\; \text{push}(\text{push}(\text{new}, 0), 0),\; \text{top}(\text{push}(\text{new}, 0)),\; \text{pop}(\text{push}(\text{new}, 0)),\; \text{isEmpty}(\text{new}),\; \text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(\text{new}, 0))\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limit:** $\;T_\Sigma = \bigcup_{n \geq 0} T_n$.

Each element of $T_\Sigma$ is a *unique finite syntactic tree* — uniqueness follows because the construction is purely inductive.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why the Initial Algebra Exists and Is Unique (Up to Isomorphism)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Initiality).** *For any algebraic specification $(\Sigma, E)$, the quotient $T_{\Sigma/E}$ is an initial object in the category $\mathbf{Alg}(\Sigma, E)$ of $\Sigma$-algebras satisfying $E$.*

**Proof Sketch (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1.** Build the term algebra $T_\Sigma(X)$ over a countably infinite variable set $X$ of every sort.

**Step 2.** Define the congruence $\equiv_E$ as the smallest equivalence on $T_\Sigma$ such that:
  (i) it is a $\Sigma$-algebra congruence (closed under all operations), and
  (ii) for every axiom $l = r \in E$ and every substitution $\sigma: X \to T_\Sigma$, we have $\sigma(l) \equiv_E \sigma(r)$.

**Step 3.** Existence. Form the quotient algebra:
$$T_{\Sigma/E} := T_\Sigma / \equiv_E$$
The quotient is well-defined because $\equiv_E$ is a congruence. Every operation $f$ lifts to $[t] \mapsto [f(t,\ldots)]$ because $\equiv_E$ is a congruence.

**Step 4.** Initiality. Let $A \in \mathbf{Alg}(\Sigma, E)$. Define $h : T_\Sigma \to \vert A\vert$ by:
$$h(t) = \text{val}_{A, \sigma_t}(t)$$
where $\sigma_t$ maps each variable appearing in $t$ to itself viewed in $A$ (using the universal property of free algebras). Because $A \models E$, we have $h(t) = h(t')$ whenever $t \equiv_E t'$. Hence $h$ factors uniquely through the quotient, yielding a homomorphism $\bar{h} : T_{\Sigma/E} \to A$.

**Step 5.** Uniqueness. If $T'_{\Sigma/E}$ is another initial algebra, the unique homomorphisms $T_{\Sigma/E} \rightleftarrows T'_{\Sigma/E}$ compose to identity morphisms, hence they are mutually inverse isomorphisms. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Showing an Array Implementation Satisfies the Stack Axioms

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification.** Let $A_{\text{arr}}$ be the implementation:
> - $\vert A_{\text{arr}}\vert_{\text{stack}} = \mathbb{N} \times \text{Array}[\text{nat}]$ (a pair: size + backing array).
> - $\text{new} \mapsto (0, [\;])$.
> - $\text{push}((n, a), x) \mapsto (n+1, a[n] := x)$.
> - $\text{top}((n, a)) \mapsto a[n-1]$.
> - $\text{pop}((n, a)) \mapsto (n-1, a)$ when $n > 0$.

Check axiom $\text{top}(\text{push}(s, e)) = e$:
$$\text{top}(\text{push}((n,a),\, x)) = \text{top}((n+1,\, a[n{:=}x])) = (a[n{:=}x])[n] = x \;=\; e \quad\checkmark$$

Check axiom $\text{pop}(\text{push}(s, e)) = s$:
$$\text{pop}(\text{push}((n,a),\, x)) = \text{pop}((n+1,\, a[n{:=}x])) = (n,\, a[n{:=}x]) \;\;\text{(but the original } a \text{ was unsliced)}$$

Aha — we must therefore define the implementation so that the underlying array is logically *immutable* in the abstraction, i.e., we extract a slice. This is exactly the role of the **abstraction function** $\alpha : \vert A_{\text{arr}}\vert \to T_{\Sigma/E}$ in software-engineering ADT literature.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Specifications vs. Their Models

| ADT | Sorts | Key Axiom | Initial Algebra Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural numbers | $\mathbb{N}$ | $\text{succ}(n) \neq 0$ | $\{0, \text{succ}(0), \text{succ}^2(0), \ldots\}$ |
| Boolean | $\{T, F\}$ | $\neg\neg x = x$, $x \lor \neg x = T$ | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| Stack | `stack, elem` | $\text{pop}(\text{push}(s,e)) = s$ | Lists of `elem` (up to congruence) |
| Queue | `queue, elem` | $\text{front}(\text{enq}(\text{new}, e)) = e$ | Lists of `elem` |
| Set | `set, elem` | $\text{mem}(\text{ins}(s,e), e) = T$ | Finite subsets of `elem` |
| Map | `map, key, val` | $\text{lookup}(\text{update}(m,k,v), k) = v$ | Finite functions $k \mapsto v$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Signatures vs. implementations:** Students often confuse the *signature* (syntax) with the *algebra* (semantics). A signature alone does not determine the ADT — axioms are mandatory.
> 2. **Equation orientation matters:** When asked to "prove" an equation, students frequently invoke axioms in the wrong direction. Always reduce both sides to the *same normal form*.
> 3. **Initiality misuse:** Saying "the initial algebra is the only model" is wrong — there are *many* models; the initial one is the *freest* (least constrained).
> 4. **Confusing $T_\Sigma$ with $T_{\Sigma/E}$:** $T_\Sigma$ contains *all* syntactic terms (potentially infinite junk). The quotient removes junk via axioms.
> 5. **Forgetting variable substitution:** Equations must be checked under *all* substitutions $\sigma$, not just the identity.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*Define the term "Abstract Data Type" mathematically. What is the role of a signature in its specification?*

**Model Answer:**
An Abstract Data Type is a pair $\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma, E)$ where $\Sigma = (S, F)$ is a *signature* consisting of a set $S$ of sorts and a set $F$ of operation symbols each with an arity, and $E$ is a set of equational axioms. The signature declares the *syntactic interface* — the names and types of operations — without ascribing meaning. It separates *what can be said* (signature) from *what must be true* (axioms). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*What is the "initial algebra" semantics of an ADT, and why is it preferred over other algebras?*

**Model Answer:**
The initial algebra of a specification $(\Sigma, E)$ is the quotient $T_{\Sigma/E} = T_\Sigma / \equiv_E$, where $\equiv_E$ is the congruence generated by the axioms. It is the *free* algebra satisfying $E$ — every other satisfying algebra $A$ admits a unique homomorphism from $T_{\Sigma/E}$. This guarantees **no junk** (every element denotes a ground term) and **no confusion** (terms are equal iff axioms force them to be), making it the canonical, implementation-independent model. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Module question is 14 marks, with internal choice. Both alternatives are shown below.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Give the algebraic specification $(\Sigma, E)$ for a **Queue** ADT of integers. Clearly list the sorts, operations, and axioms. Ensure your axioms completely determine the queue behaviour.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Prove that any two implementations of a queue that satisfy the axioms are semantically equivalent up to isomorphism.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

*Signature:* Sorts $S = \{\text{queue},\; \text{int},\; \text{bool}\}$.

Operations:
- $\text{new} : \to \text{queue}$
- $\text{enq} : \text{queue} \times \text{int} \to \text{queue}$
- $\text{deq} : \text{queue} \to \text{queue}$
- $\text{front} : \text{queue} \to \text{int}$
- $\text{isEmpty} : \text{queue} \to \text{bool}$

*Axioms (assuming front/deq undefined on empty queue, encoded via isEmpty):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{isEmpty}(\text{new}) &= \text{true} \\
\text{isEmpty}(\text{enq}(q, x)) &= \text{false} \\
\text{front}(\text{enq}(\text{new}, x)) &= x \\
\text{front}(\text{enq}(\text{enq}(q, y), x)) &= \text{front}(\text{enq}(q, y)) \\
\text{deq}(\text{enq}(\text{new}, x)) &= \text{new} \\
\text{deq}(\text{enq}(\text{enq}(q, y), x)) &= \text{enq}(\text{deq}(\text{enq}(q, y)), x)
\end{aligned}
$$

These axioms pin down the FIFO behaviour uniquely. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Let $A, B \in \mathbf{Alg}(\Sigma, E)$ be two implementations of the queue ADT. By the Initiality Theorem, the quotient algebra $T_{\Sigma/E}$ admits unique homomorphisms
$$\bar{h}_A : T_{\Sigma/E} \to A \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{h}_B : T_{\Sigma/E} \to B.$$
Composition $\bar{h}_B \circ \bar{h}_A^{-1}$ (defined because each is bijective on the carrier due to *no junk, no confusion*) gives a homomorphism $A \to B$. Conversely $\bar{h}_A \circ \bar{h}_B^{-1} : B \to A$. The two compositions are mutual inverses by the uniqueness clause of initiality (the identity is the only endomorphism of an initial object in a category with *no junk*).

Hence $A$ and $B$ are isomorphic as $\Sigma$-algebras — *semantically equivalent*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the construction of the **term algebra** $T_\Sigma$ for a signature $\Sigma$. Use the signature of the natural numbers with operations $0 : \to \text{nat}$ and $\text{succ} : \text{nat} \to \text{nat}$ to illustrate.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Define a **homomorphism** between two $\Sigma$-algebras. State and prove the universal property that characterises the term algebra $T_\Sigma(X)$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The term algebra $T_\Sigma$ is the *least set* closed under operation application. Construction by induction on term depth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= \{\,c \mid c : \to s \in F\,\} \quad \text{(nullary operations)} \\
T_{n+1} &= T_n \;\cup\; \{\,f(t_1,\ldots,t_k) \mid f:s_1\times\cdots\times s_k\to s \in F,\; t_i \in T_n \text{ of sort } s_i\,\} \\
T_\Sigma &= \bigcup_{n \geq 0} T_n
\end{aligned}
$$

*For natural numbers* $(\Sigma_{\mathbb{N}} = \{\text{nat}\}, \{0 : \to \text{nat},\; \text{succ} : \text{nat} \to \text{nat}\})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= \{0\} \\
T_1 &= \{0,\; \text{succ}(0)\} \\
T_2 &= \{0,\; \text{succ}(0),\; \text{succ}(\text{succ}(0))\} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
T_\Sigma &= \{0,\; \text{succ}(0),\; \text{succ}^2(0),\; \text{succ}^3(0),\; \ldots\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each natural number $n$ is represented *exactly once* as $\text{succ}^n(0)$, demonstrating the *no junk, no confusion* property. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

*Definition.* A $\Sigma$-algebra homomorphism $h : A \to B$ is a family of functions $\{h_s : A_s \to B_s\}_{s \in S}$ such that for every $f : s_1 \times \cdots \times s_k \to s \in F$ and every $(a_1,\ldots,a_k)$ with $a_i \in A_{s_i}$:

$$
h_s\big(A_f(a_1,\ldots,a_k)\big) \;=\; B_f\big(h_{s_1}(a_1),\ldots,h_{s_k}(a_k)\big).
$$

*Universal Property of $T_\Sigma(X)$:* For every $\Sigma$-algebra $A$ and every map $\sigma : X \to \vert A\vert$, there exists a *unique* homomorphism $h : T_\Sigma(X) \to A$ such that $h(x) = \sigma(x)$ for every variable $x \in X$.

*Proof.* Define $h : T_\Sigma(X) \to \vert A\vert$ by structural recursion:

$$
h(t) =
\begin{cases}
\sigma(x) & \text{if } t = x \in X, \\[4pt]
A_f\big(h(t_1),\ldots,h(t_k)\big) & \text{if } t = f(t_1,\ldots,t_k).
\end{cases}
$$

This is well-defined because every term has a unique syntactic decomposition (induction on term depth). It is a homomorphism because $h$ commutes with operations by construction. Uniqueness: any two homomorphisms agreeing on $X$ must agree on all compound terms by induction on depth. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] An ADT is a *pair* $\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma, E)$ — never one alone.
- [ ] **Signature $\Sigma = (S, F)$** declares *sorts* and *operation symbols* with arities.
- [ ] **Axioms $E$** are *equations* (possibly conditional) that pin down semantics.
- [ ] A **$\Sigma$-algebra** assigns concrete sets and functions to the signature.
- [ ] The **term algebra** $T_\Sigma$ is the *free* $\Sigma$-algebra — pure syntax, no equations imposed.
- [ ] The **initial algebra** $T_{\Sigma/E}$ is $T_\Sigma$ *quotiented* by the congruence generated by $E$.
- [ ] **Initiality = no junk + no confusion.** Every term denotes a value; two terms are equal iff axioms force it.
- [ ] **Homomorphisms** are structure-preserving maps between algebras.
- [ ] The **universal property** of $T_\Sigma(X)$: every variable-assignment extends uniquely to a homomorphism.
- [ ] Two implementations of an ADT are *equivalent* iff there is an isomorphism between their algebras that respects $E$.
- [ ] **Abstraction function** $\alpha$ in software engineering = the homomorphism from a concrete algebra to the initial one.
- [ ] The Stack axioms $\text{top}(\text{push}(s,e)) = e$ and $\text{pop}(\text{push}(s,e)) = s$ are the canonical textbook example.
- [ ] The mathematics of ADTs is the foundation of *formal specification languages* (Z, VDM, CafeOBJ, Maude, Alloy).
- [ ] Category-theoretic view: an ADT = the *initial object* in the category of algebras satisfying $E$.
- [ ] KTU exam favourite: drawing the signature, listing axioms, and *proving* an equation by rewriting both sides to the same normal form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:**
> When the KTU question asks "specify an ADT", always structure your answer as: **(i) Sorts → (ii) Operations with arities → (iii) Axioms → (iv) Mention of initial-algebra semantics**. This four-part format consistently fetches full marks under KTU valuation key patterns.
